According to the doctrine of robbery, the use of force or threat of force must be contemporaneous with the taking of the property to constitute robbery. The force used must be incidental to the taking, and not merely a subsequent act. In cases where the force is used after the property has been taken, the offense is more properly characterized as larceny from the person, followed by a separate act of battery or assault. Therefore, a conviction for robbery is least likely to be upheld when the force is not used to effect the taking of the property but is used afterward, as in the scenario described in option (D). 

Answer: (D)

evidence_store.text: Again, the simple-robbery statute requires the use or threat of imminent use of force "against any person to overcome the person's resistance or powers of resistance to, or to compel acquiescence in, the taking or carrying away" of personal property. Minn. Stat. § 609.24 (emphasis added). Commission of a simple robbery "does not require that the use of force or threats actually precede or accompany the taking" but rather "requires only that the use of force or threats precede or accompany either the taking or the carrying away." State v. Kvale , 302 N.W.2d 650, 653 (Minn. 1981) (emphasis added). Thus, caselaw treats the "taking" and the "carrying away" as separate acts. See id . (stating that the use of force "overcame the victim's power to resist and compelled his acquiescence both in the completed taking and in the contemporaneous carrying away of the [property]"); see also State v. Thonesavanh , 904 N.W.2d 432, 438 (Minn. 2017) (noting that the supreme court had previously held that "even temporary 'control or dominion' over another's property was sufficient to 'complete' the offense of simple robbery, even if there was no evidence that the defendant had 'carr[ied] away' the property" (alteration in original) (quoting State v. Solomon , 359 N.W.2d 19, 21 (Minn. 1984) )).

Robbery is larceny from the person or presence of the victim by means of violence or intimidation. The use of force constituting a battery is sufficient, and if the perpetrator uses force to overcome the person's resistance to the taking, there is sufficient force to constitute a robbery. All that is required for taking is that the perpetrator have possession of the item, and the slightest movement will suffice for the carrying away.

Second-degree robbery is defined as forcibly stealing property. Section 569.030. A person "forcibly steals" if "in the course of stealing, ... he uses or threatens the immediate use of physical force upon another person for the purpose of" either defeating resistance to the theft or compelling the surrender of the property. Section 569.010(1); see also State v. Coleman, 463 S.W.3d 353, 354 (Mo. banc 2015). "[D]etermining the existence of a threat is an objective test that depends on whether a reasonable person would believe the defendant's conduct was a threat of the immediate use of physical force." Coleman, 463 S.W.3d at 355. "[A]s a matter of general law the force necessary to constitute robbery may be constructive as well as actual, and may consist in the intimidation of the victim, or putting him in fear." State v. Foster, 665 S.W.2d 348, 350 (Mo. App. S.D. 1984). The threat or use of physical force must come from the defendant and must be imparted upon the victim. See State v. Ide, 933 S.W.2d 849, 853 (Mo. App. W.D. 1996).

Next the defendants assert that, consistent with common law, force used during robbery to overcome a victim's resistance must occur before or contemporaneously with the acquisition of control over the property. Because Illinois has allowed robbery convictions where the wrongdoer uses force only after the theft, they argue, Illinois robbery covers more territory than generic robbery. They point to People v. Merchant , 361 Ill.App.3d 69, 297 Ill.Dec. 89, 836 N.E.2d 820, 824 (2005), in which the court approved a robbery conviction where, after the offender grabbed a $ 20 bill from the victim's hand, a struggle broke out and the robber pushed the victim against a wall. The court relied on earlier decisions sustaining convictions for robbery "where a struggle ensued following the taking, or the perpetrator used force when escaping the scene." Id. 297 Ill.Dec. 89, 836 N.E.2d at 823. It reasoned that although the defendant did not use force to snatch the money, the evidence suggested that the victim and defendant "struggled over possession of the money," and this physical conflict elevated the crime to a robbery when "combined with the immediacy of the struggle following the taking." Id. 297 Ill.Dec. 89, 836 N.E.2d at 824.

CALJIC No. 9.40.2 provides: "To constitute the crime of robbery, the perpetrator must have formed the specific intent to permanently deprive an owner of [his] [her] property before or at the time that the act of taking the property occurred. If this intent was not formed until after the property was taken from the person or immediate presence of the victim, the crime of robbery has not been committed." Defendant rightly observes that it is possible for someone to form an intent to permanently deprive another of property before "the act of taking the property" is complete, but after the use of force has concluded.

label: qa_CONST. LAW_mbe_777
subject: CONST. LAW
question: Twenty percent of the residents of Green City are members of minority racial groups. These residents are evenly distributed among the many different residential areas of the city. The five city council members of Green City are elected from five single-member electoral districts that are nearly equally populated. No candidate has ever been elected to the city council who was a member of a minority racial group. A group of citizens who are members of minority racial groups file suit in federal di
final_answer:
To determine the most obvious basis for the plaintiffs' claim, we need to analyze the situation and the constitutional provisions provided.

1. **Thirteenth Amendment (A):** This amendment abolishes slavery and involuntary servitude, except as punishment for a crime. It does not directly relate to voting rights or electoral systems.

2. **Due Process Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment (B):** While this clause is broad and protects various rights, including those related to liberty and property, it is not the most direct or obvious basis for claims specifically about voting rights and racial discrimination in the context of elections.

3. **Privileges and Immunities Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment (C):** This clause protects the rights and privileges of U.S. citizenship from interference by state laws. However, it is not specifically tailored to address voting rights issues based on race.

4. **Fifteenth Amendment (D):** This amendment explicitly prohibits the federal government and each state from denying a citizen the right to vote based on that citizen's "race, color, or previous condition of servitude." It directly addresses the issue of racial discrimination in voting, making it the most relevant and obvious basis for the plaintiffs' claim that the single-member districting system diminishes the ability of minority racial group voters to affect the outcome of city elections.

Given the specifics of the case, which involves allegations of racial discrimination in voting, the **Fifteenth Amendment** is the most directly applicable constitutional provision.

Answer: (D)

choices.B: The due process clause of the Fourteenth Amendment.
choices.C: The privileges and immunities clause of the Fourteenth Amendment.
choices.D: The Fifteenth Amendment.

It forbids them to discriminate against Negroes in matters having to do with voting. See Ex parte **1497 Yarbrough, 110 U.S. 651, 665, 4 S.Ct. 152, 159, 28 L.Ed. 274; Neal v. Delaware, 103 U.S. 370, 389–390, 26 L.Ed. 567; United States v. Cruikshank, 92 U.S. 542, 555–556, 23 L.Ed. 588; United States v. Reese, 92 U.S. 214, 23 L.Ed. 563. The Amendment'scommand *62 a
snap_answer:
To determine the most obvious basis for the plaintiffs' claim, we need to analyze the situation and the constitutional provisions provided in the options.

1. **The Thirteenth Amendment** primarily deals with the abolition of slavery and involuntary servitude. While it has been interpreted to reach beyond its literal terms to prohibit the "badges and incidents of slavery," it does not directly address voting rights or electoral districting. Thus, it's less directly applicable to the issue at hand.

2. **The due process clause of the Fourteenth Amendment** ensures that no state shall deprive any person of life, liberty, or property without due process of law. While this clause has been used to protect various rights, including some related to voting (such as in apportionment cases), the plaintiffs' claim seems more closely related to the specific protection of voting rights for minority groups rather than a broad due process claim.

3. **The privileges and immunities clause of the Fourteenth Amendment** prohibits states from depriving anyone of the privileges and immunities of U.S. citizenship. However, this clause has been narrowly interpreted and is not typically the primary basis for claims related to voting rights and electoral districting.

4. **The Fifteenth Amendment** explicitly protects the right to vote from being denied or abridged by the United States or any state on account of race, color, or previous condition of servitude. Given that the plaintiffs are members of minority racial groups claiming that the current electoral system diminishes their ability to affect the outcome of elections, this amendment directly addresses the issue of racial discrimination in voting.

Considering the specifics of the case, which involves allegations of diminished voting power for minority racial groups due to the electoral districting system, the **Fifteenth Amendment** is the most directly applicable. It explicitly prohibits the denial or abridgment of the right to vote on the basis of race, making it the most obvious basis for the plaintiffs' claim.

Answer: (D)
hyde_passage: The Fifteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution explicitly prohibits the denial or abridgment of the right to vote on account of race, color, or previous condition of servitude, making it the most directly applicable provision to claims of racial discrimination in voting, including those related to electoral districting that may dilute the voting power of minority racial groups. This amendment has been interpreted to reach beyond literal voter disenfranchisement to include practices that effectively diminish the ability of racial minorities to elect candidates of their choice. As such, it provides the legal basis for challenging electoral systems that result in the underrepresentation of minority groups in elected bodies.

evidence_store.text: The Court's early decisions under the Fifteenth Amendment established that it imposes but one limitation on the powers of the States. It forbids them to discriminate against Negroes in matters having to do with voting. See Ex parte **1497 Yarbrough, 110 U.S. 651, 665, 4 S.Ct. 152, 159, 28 L.Ed. 274; Neal v. Delaware, 103 U.S. 370, 389–390, 26 L.Ed. 567; United States v. Cruikshank, 92 U.S. 542, 555–556, 23 L.Ed. 588; United States v. Reese, 92 U.S. 214, 23 L.Ed. 563. The Amendment'scommand *62 and effect are wholly negative. “The Fifteenth Amendment does not confer the right of suffrage upon any one,” but has “invested the citizens of the United States with a new constitutional right which is within the protecting power of Congress. That right is exemption from discrimination in the exercise of the elective franchise on account of race, color, or previous condition of servitude.” Id., at 217–218.

Moreover, the Fifteenth Amendment applies with equal force regardless of the particular racial group targeted by the challenged law. Although originally enacted to guarantee emancipated slaves the right to vote after the Civil War, the generic language of the Fifteenth Amendment "transcend[s] the particular controversy which was the immediate impetus for its enactment." Rice , 528 U.S. at 512, 120 S.Ct. 1044. The Amendment's prohibition on racial discrimination "grants protection to all persons, not just members of a particular race." Id . Its "mandate of neutrality" is thus straightforward and universal: "If citizens of one race having certain qualifications are permitted by law to vote, those of another having the same qualifications must be" permitted to vote as well. Id . (quoting United States v. Reese , 92 U.S. 214, 218, 23 L.Ed. 563 (1875) ).

The Fifteenth Amendment's prohibition on race-based voting restrictions is both fundamental and absolute. See Shaw v. Reno , 509 U.S. 630, 639, 113 S.Ct. 2816, 125 L.Ed.2d 511 (1993). As "[t]here is no room under the Amendment for the concept that the right to vote in a particular election can be allocated based on race," the levels of scrutiny applied to other constitutional restrictions are not pertinent to a race-based franchise limitation. Rice , 528 U.S. at 523, 120 S.Ct. 1044 (emphasis added). This clear-cut rule reflects the importance of the franchise as "the essence of a democratic society" and recognizes that "any restrictions on that right strike at the heart of representative government." Reynolds , 377 U.S. at 555, 84 S.Ct. 1362.

The majority opinion suggests that Section 2 vote dilution requires that a minority group be deprived of a district in which it has electoral strength to elect a representative of its choice when such a district is possible. But nothing in Section 2 "establishes a right to have members of a protected class elected in numbers equal to their proportion in the population." 52 U.S.C. § 10301(b). "[T]he ultimate right of § 2 is equality of opportunity, not a guarantee of electoral success for minority-preferred candidates of whatever race." De Grandy , 512 U.S. at 1014 n.11, 114 S.Ct. 2647 ; see also Gingles , 478 U.S. at 96, 106 S.Ct. 2752 (O'Connor, J., concurring in the judgment).

Finally, we also seek to ensure that in remedying the identified Equal Protection violations, we do not select a plan under which black voters' rights are diminished when compared with the unconstitutional 2011 plan. We thus consider compliance with Section 2 of the VRA as an "equitable factor" in our redistricting process, and will "implement a plan that complies with federal policy disfavoring discrimination against minority voters." See Personhuballah , 155 F.Supp.3d at 564 ; see also Abrams , 521 U.S. at 90, 117 S.Ct. 1925 (noting that "[o]n its face, § 2 does not apply to a court-ordered remedial redistricting plan," but assuming without deciding that "courts should comply with [Section 2] when exercising their equitable powers to redistrict"). Section 2 prohibits "vote dilution," which occurs if election processes "are not equally open to participation by members of a [racial minority]" such that members of that minority group "have less opportunity than other members of the electorate to participate in the political process and to elect representatives of their choice." 52 U.S.C. § 10301(b) ; see also Personhuballah , 155 F.Supp.3d at 564-65 (describing factors to consider in a Section 2 analysis). Accordingly, in evaluating the proposed remedial plans, we will assess black voters' ability to elect their preferred candidates in the redrawn districts.

question: Defendant was tried for robbery. Victim Worth were the only witnesses called to.t tify. Victim testified that Defend; threatened her with a knife, grabbed her pur and ran off with it. Worth testified that hes Defendant grab Victim's purse and run av with it but that he neither saw a knife nor he any threats. On this evidence the jury co properly return a verdict of guilty of
final_answer:
To determine the correct answer, let's analyze the elements of robbery and larceny, and then apply them to the given scenario.

1. **Robbery** typically involves the taking of another person's property (larceny) coupled with the use or threat of force to accomplish the taking. The key elements are:
   - Taking and carrying away of another's property
   - Without consent
   - By force or threat of force

2. **Larceny** involves the taking and carrying away of another person's property without consent, with the intent to permanently deprive the owner of the property. The key elements are:
   - Taking and carrying away of another's property
   - Without consent
   - With intent to permanently deprive

Given the testimony:
- **Victim** testified that the Defendant threatened her with a knife (force/threat of force), grabbed her purse, and ran off with it. This testimony supports the elements of robbery.
- **Worth** testified that he saw the Defendant grab Victim's purse and run away with it but did not see a knife or hear any threats. This testimony supports the elements of larceny but does not confirm the use or threat of force necessary for robbery.

Based on the testimony:
- The jury could find the Defendant guilty of **robbery** if they believe the Victim's testimony about the threat with a knife, which establishes the use of force/threat of force.
- The jury could also find the Defendant guilty of **larceny** based on both testimonies, as both agree that the Defendant took the Victim's purse without consent and ran away with it.

Since the jury has evidence to support both robbery (if they believe the threat was made) and larceny (based on the taking of the property without consent), the correct answer reflects the possibility of either conviction based on the evidence presented.

Answer: (C)

choices.B: larceny only
choices.C: either robbery or larceny
choices.D: both robbery and larceny

evidence_store.text: Connecticut's statute defines robbery essentially as it is understood in the common law and largely throughout the United States, as requiring the use or threat of force to take property from the person of another without the person's consent. Robbery has consistently been treated as an aggravated form of larceny because the taking of property from the person of another against the victim's will by force or threat of force is inevitably capable of causing physical harm to the victim, regardless of whether the force actually employed in the taking of the property is by itself sufficient to cause pain or injury. Scholars of the criminal law underline the inherent potential for physical harm to the victim as the explanation why robbery developed as, and continues to be treated as, an aggravated felony, generally carrying harsher punishments than other forms of larceny. See 3 W. LaFave, Substantive Criminal Law § 20.3, p. 221 (3d ed. 2017) ("Robbery, a common-law felony, and today everywhere a statutory felony regardless of the amount taken, may be thought of as aggravated larceny-misappropriation of property under circumstances involving a danger to the person as well as a danger to property-and thus deserving of a greater punishment than that provided for larceny. Robbery consists of all six elements of larceny ... plus two additional requirements: [ (1) ] that the property be taken from the person or presence of the other and [ (2) ] that the taking be accomplished by means of force or putting in fear.") (emphasis added); see also id . at 222 n.4 ("Robbery may be considered a greater crime than the sum of the two lesser crimes of larceny and assault (or battery). As stated in Model Penal Code § 222.1, Comment at 98 (1980): ... [T]he robber may be distinguished from the stealthy thief by the hardihood that enables him to carry out his purpose in the presence of his victim and over his opposition-obstacles that might deter ordinary sneak thieves and that justify the feeling of special danger evoked by robbery .") (emphasis added); E. Podgor, P. Henning, and N. Cohen, Mastering Criminal Law, p. 215 (2d ed. 2015) ("Robbery, often punished by a lengthy prison term, is an aggravated form of larceny. The offense entails a larceny coupled with the use of force or a threat of violence to dispossess the victim of the property. Robbery is deemed a very serious crime, a felony in every jurisdiction, because it presents the real danger of immediate serious physical harm to the victim .... Because the actual or potential bodily harm is the primary concern with robbery, it occurs irrespective of the value of the items taken.") (emphasis added).

A person commits robbery when, in the course of committing a larceny, he uses or threatens the immediate use of physical force upon another person for the purpose of: (1) Preventing or overcoming resistance to the taking of the property or to the retention thereof immediately after the taking or (2) compelling the owner of such property or another person to deliver up the property or to engage in other conduct which aids in the commission of the larceny.

"[Section] 53a-133 of the Penal Code defines robbery as follows: 'A person commits robbery when she, in the course of committing a larceny, she or another participant in the crime uses or threatens the immediate use of physical force upon another person for the purpose of: (1) preventing or overcoming resistance to the taking of the property or the retention thereof immediately after the taking; or (2) compelling the owner of such property or another person to deliver up the property or to engage in other conduct which aids in the commission of the larceny.' Thus, the elements of robbery are (1) in the course of a larceny; and [2] uses physical force.

Defendant further contends that the evidence was insufficient to support a conclusion that the killings occurred during the commission of a robbery. (See § 189 ["All murder that is ... committed in the perpetration of ... robbery ... is murder of the first degree"].) "Robbery is the felonious taking of personal property in the possession of another, from his person or immediate presence, and against his will, accomplished by means of force or fear." (§ 211.) "[A] conviction of robbery cannot be sustained in the absence of evidence that the defendant conceived his intent to steal either before committing the act of force against the victim, or during the commission of that act; if the intent arose only after the use of force against the victim, the taking will at most constitute a theft." ( People v. Morris (1988) 46 Cal.3d 1, 19, 249 Cal.Rptr. 119, 756 P.2d 843 ; see also People v. Lindberg (2008) 45 Cal.4th 1, 28, 82 Cal.Rptr.3d 323, 190 P.3d 664 [discussing robbery-murder special circumstance].) Defendant argues that no rational jury could rule out the possibility, beyond a reasonable doubt, that defendant formed his intent to take the Jenkses' property only after the acts of force had concluded. We again disagree.

Robbery is larceny from the person or presence of the victim by means of violence or intimidation. The use of force constituting a battery is sufficient, and if the perpetrator uses force to overcome the person's resistance to the taking, there is sufficient force to constitute a robbery. All that is required for taking is that the perpetrator have possession of the item, and the slightest movement will suffice for the carrying away.

question: Modality City has had a severe traffic problem on its streets. As a result, it enacted an ordinance prohibiting all sales to the public of food or other items by persons selling directly from trucks, cars, or other vehicles located on city streets. The ordinance included an inseverable grandfather provision exempting from its prohibition vendors who, for 20 years or more, have continuously sold food or other items from such vehicles located on the streets of Modality City. Northwind Ice Cream, a
final_answer:
To determine the constitutionality of the ordinance, we must analyze it under the equal protection clause of the Fourteenth Amendment. The equal protection clause requires that similarly situated individuals be treated equally under the law. The ordinance in question prohibits sales from vehicles on city streets but includes a grandfather provision that exempts vendors who have been selling for 20 years or more. This creates a distinction between long-standing vendors, like Northwind Ice Cream, and newer vendors, like Yuppee Yogurt.

The key issue here is whether this distinction is rationally related to a legitimate government interest. The city's interest in reducing traffic congestion is a legitimate one. However, the ordinance's effectiveness in achieving this goal, especially with its grandfather provision, is questionable. The provision exempts long-standing vendors, potentially allowing them to continue contributing to traffic congestion, while newer vendors like Yuppee Yogurt are prohibited from selling, even if their activities might not significantly impact traffic.

Given the information provided and the principles outlined in the retrieved passages, the court's analysis will likely focus on whether the ordinance is rationally related to a legitimate government purpose, which is the standard for economic and social legislation under the rational basis test. The rational basis test is the most relaxed form of judicial scrutiny and requires that the legislation be rationally related to a legitimate state interest. The government does not need to prove that the law actually achieves its intended purpose, but rather that it could have had a legitimate reason for enacting the law as it did.

Option (A) is incorrect because the strict scrutiny test, which requires that a law be narrowly tailored to achieve a compelling government interest, is typically applied to laws that discriminate based on suspect classes or affect fundamental rights, neither of which is explicitly indicated in this scenario.

Option (C) refers to the "substantial relationship test," which is more stringent than the rational basis test and is not the applicable standard in this context, given that economic and social legislation is typically reviewed under the rational basis test.

Option (D) is incorrect because grandfather provisions, while potentially problematic under the equal protection clause, are not per se invalid. Their validity depends on whether they are rationally related to a legitimate government interest.

Option (B) is the most appropriate choice because the ordinance's validity is indeed governed by the rational basis test. Under this test, the courts defer to economic choices embodied in legislation if they are even plausibly justifiable. The city's interest in reducing traffic congestion is legitimate, and the question becomes whether the ordinance, including its grandfather provision, is rationally related to this interest. Given the deferential nature of the rational basis test, the court is likely to uphold the ordinance unless it is clearly unrelated to the city's legitimate interests.

Answer: (B)

The ordinance in question prohibits all sales to the public of food or other items by persons selling directly from trucks, cars, or other vehicles located on city streets, but it includes a grandfather provision that exempts vendors who have been selling for 20 years or more.

The key issue here is whether the distinction made by the ordinance between long-standing vendors and newer vendors, such as Yuppee Yogurt, is constitutionally permissible. The grandfather provision creates a classification that treats vendors differently based on the length of time they have been operating.

When a law affects economic rights and does not involve a suspect class or a fundamental right, the rational basis test is typically applied. Under this test, the law is constitutional if it is rationally related to a legitimate government interest. The city's interest in reducing traffic congestion is a legitimate government interest.

The ordinance's classification must be rationally related to this interest. While the grandfather provision may seem arbitrary, the city might argue that it is a way to phase out street vendors without causing immediate harm to long-established businesses, thus reducing disruption and potentially mitigating the economic impact on those vendors.

However, the critical issue is whether the ordinance's distinction between vendors based on their length of operation is rationally related to reducing traffic congestion. The fact that only vendors who have been operating for 20 years or more are exempted does not necessarily mean that the ordinance is rationally related to reducing traffic, as newer vendors like Yuppee Yogurt could also contribute to traffic congestion.

Despite this, courts often defer to legislative judgments in economic matters, especially when the law does not involve a suspect class or a fundamental right. The rational basis test is highly deferential, and as long as the legislature's action is rationally related to a legitimate government interest, the law will be upheld, even if the court might think that the law is unwise or that a different approach would be better.

Given these considerations, the most likely outcome is that the court will find the ordinance to be constitutional because its validity is governed by the rational basis test. The courts typically defer to economic choices embodied in legislation if they are even plausibly justifiable, which means that the ordinance will likely be upheld unless it is clearly unreasonable or arbitrary.

evidence_store.text: The rational basis test requires that an “ordinance ... be rationally related to the achievement of a legitimate government purpose.” Id. Under this test, a court gives great deference to economic and social legislation. Curse v. Dir., Office of Workers' Comp. Programs, 843 F.2d 456, 463 (11th Cir.1988); Alamo Rent–A–Car, Inc. v. Sarasota–Manatee Airport Auth., 825 F.2d 367, 370 (11th Cir.1987) (“[G]overnmental bodies [have] wide latitude in enacting social and economic legislation; the federal courts do not sit as arbiters of the wisdom or utility of these laws.”). “The general rule is that legislation is presumed to be valid and will be sustained if the classification drawn by the statute is rationally related to a legitimate state interest.” City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Ctr., 473 U.S. 432, 440, 105 S.Ct. 3249, 87 L.Ed.2d 313 (1985). Thus, a court will not overturn the legislation “ ‘unless the varying treatment of different ... persons is so unrelated to the achievement of any combination of legitimate purposes that we can only conclude that the legislature's actions were irrational.’ ” Price v. Tanner, 855 F.2d 820, 823 (11th Cir.1988) (quoting Vance v. Bradley, 440 U.S. 93, 97, 99 S.Ct. 939, 59 L.Ed.2d 171 (1979)). Moreover, the rational relationship between the means adopted and the legislation's purpose must be “at least debatable.” United States v. Carolene Prods. Co., 304 U.S. 144, 154, 58 S.Ct. 778, 82 L.Ed. 1234 (1938).

¶ 64 When assessing a substantive due process claim in the context of the right to pursue a trade or profession, we apply a rational basis standard of review. See Amunrud , 158 Wash.2d at 222, 143 P.3d 571. "Under this test, the challenged law must be rationally related to a legitimate state interest." Id. "The rational basis test is the most relaxed form of judicial scrutiny." Id. at 223, 143 P.3d 571. When applying the rational basis test, "we do not require that the government's action actually advance its stated purposes, but merely look to see whether the government could have had a legitimate reason for acting as it did." Wedges/Ledges of Cal., Inc. v. City of Phoenix , 24 F.3d 56, 66 (9th Cir. 1994). "If it is 'at least fairly debatable' that the [government]'s conduct is rationally related to a legitimate governmental interest, there has been no violation of substantive due process." Halverson v. Skagit County , 42 F.3d 1257, 1262 (9th Cir. 1994) (quoting Kawaoka v. City of Arroyo Grande , 17 F.3d 1227, 1234 (9th Cir. 1994) ).

Cmty. Res., 154 N.H. at 762, 917 A.2d 707. Finally, absent a classification based upon suspect classes, affecting fundamental rights, or involving important substantive rights, the constitutional standard of review is that of rationality. Sandra H., 150 N.H. at 638, 846 A.2d 513 ; cf. Gonya v. Comm'r, N.H. Ins. Dept., 153 N.H. 521, 532-33, 899 A.2d 278 (2006). Our rational basis test requires that legislation be rationally related to a legitimate government interest. Boulders at Strafford v. Town of Strafford, 153 N.H. 633, 639, 903 A.2d 1021 (2006). Under this test, the party challenging the statute or ordinance must show that whatever classification is promulgated is arbitrary or without some reasonable justification. Id. at 640, 903 A.2d 1021.

In more modern times, rational basis review has evolved not only to include the presumption of constitutionality and the deferential standard of review, but also to require an individual challenging legislation to show either that the legislation does not further a legitimate state interest, or that the legislation is not rationally related to this legitimate state interest. See Glucksberg , 521 U.S. at 728, 117 S.Ct. 2258 ("The Constitution also requires, however, that Washington's assisted-suicide ban be rationally related to legitimate government interests."); Romer v. Evans , 517 U.S. 620, 635, 116 S.Ct. 1620, 134 L.Ed.2d 855 (1996) ("[A] law must bear a rational relationship to a legitimate governmental purpose....").
Under the rational basis test, a law is presumed to be valid and will be upheld unless the challenger can make the difficult showing that it is not rationally related to a legitimate state interest.

label: qa_CONTRACTS_mbe_1023
subject: CONTRACTS
question: For this question only, assume that on January 15, having at that time received no reply from Lawyer, Stationer notified Lawyer that effective February 1, it was increasing the prices of certain specified items in its catalog. Is the price increase effective with respect to catalog orders Stationer receives from Lawyer during the month of February?
final_answer:
To determine whether the price increase is effective with respect to catalog orders Stationer receives from Lawyer during the month of February, we need to analyze the nature of Stationer's original offer and the rules governing firm offers under the Uniform Commercial Code (UCC).

1. **Nature of the Offer**: Stationer's catalog can be considered an offer for the sale of goods. Since Stationer is a merchant (as implied by the context of selling office supplies), and the offer is for the sale of goods, the UCC applies.

2. **Firm Offers under the UCC**: According to the UCC, specifically §2-205 (as mentioned in Source 2), a firm offer by a merchant is irrevocable for the stated period or, if no period is stated, for a reasonable time, not to exceed three months. The key elements for a firm offer are:
   - The offer must be made by a merchant.
   - The offer must be in a signed writing.
   - The offer must give assurance that it will be held open.

3. **Application to the Scenario**: The question implies that Stationer is a merchant and that the catalog (or a relevant part of it) constitutes a signed writing offering goods for sale at specified prices. However, it does not explicitly state that the catalog gives assurance that the offer (including prices) will be held open for a specific period. 

4. **Analysis of Choices**:
   - **(A)** Promissory estoppel requires a promise, reliance, and an injustice if the promise is not enforced. While Stationer's catalog could be seen as a promise, there's no clear indication that Lawyer relied on the promise of fixed prices to their detriment in a way that would invoke promissory estoppel.
   - **(B)** This choice suggests that Stationer's offer, including the price term, is irrevocable because Stationer is a merchant. For this to be true, Stationer must have given assurance that the offer, including the prices, would be held open, which is not explicitly stated in the question.
   - **(C)** This choice implies that without consideration, Stationer's assurance (if any) that prices would not increase is not binding. Under the UCC, a firm offer does not require consideration to be irrevocable, so this reasoning does not directly apply to firm offers under the UCC.
   - **(D)** This choice suggests that the period for which Stationer gave assurance that it would not raise prices exceeds three months, making the price increase effective. However, the question does not specify the duration of the assurance, only that the price increase is effective February 1, which does not necessarily imply a period longer than three months.

Given the information and focusing on the specifics of firm offers under the UCC, the most relevant factor is whether Stationer's catalog constitutes a firm offer with respect to the prices. Since the question does not explicitly state that Stationer gave assurance that the prices would remain open for a specific period, and given that a firm offer must be irrevocable for a stated period or a reasonable time not exceeding three months, we must consider what is implied by Stationer's actions and the UCC provisions.

Without explicit assurance that the prices would be held open, and considering the UCC's provisions on firm offers, the critical factor is whether Stationer's notification of a price increase before any order from Lawyer affects the irrevocability of the original offer's price term. Since the question implies a lack of explicit assurance that the offer (including prices) would be held open beyond the initial offer, and given that a firm offer's irrevocability is contingent upon such assurance, the price increase's effectiveness hinges on the interpretation of Stationer's actions as they relate to firm offers under the UCC.

**Conclusion**: Given the information provided and the principles of firm offers under the UCC, the most appropriate answer is based on the understanding that without an explicit assurance that the offer (including the price term) would be held open, Stationer's original offer does not become irrevocable solely because it is a merchant. The key factor is the presence of an assurance that the offer will be held open, which is not clearly indicated in the scenario provided.

**Answer: (B)**

choices.A: No, because Stationer's original offer, including the price term, became irrevocable under the doctrine of promissory estoppel.
choices.B: No, because Stationer is a merchant with respect to office supplies; and its original offer, including the price term, was irrevocable throughout the month of February.
choices.C: Yes, because Stationer received no consideration to support its assurance that it would not increase prices.
choices.D: Yes, because the period for which Stationer gave assurance that it would not raise prices was longer than three months.

gold_passage: Promissory estoppel encompasses the following elements: (1) a promise by the promissor; (2) made with the expectation that the promisee will rely thereon; (3) which induces reasonable reliance by the promisee; (4) of a definite and substantial nature; and (5) injustice can be avoided only by enforcement of the promise. Brown v. Branch, 758 N.E.2d 48, 52 (Ind.2001).
snap_answer:
Under the UCC, a firm offer applies when a merchant makes an offer that includes some assurance that the offer will be held open, and the assurance is given for a reasonable period of time not to exceed three months. In this case, Stationer gave no assurance that it would not raise prices, but instead, on January 15, notified Lawyer that it was increasing prices effective February 1. 

Since Stationer gave no assurance that it would hold the prices steady, there was no firm offer. Stationer's original offer, including the price term, could be revoked at any time before acceptance, as long as the revocation was made before Lawyer accepted the offer. 

Stationer's notification to Lawyer on January 15 that prices would increase on February 1 served as a revocation of its original offer as to the price term. Therefore, when Lawyer places orders in February, it will be at the new prices, as the original price term was effectively revoked.

Answer: (D)

In the absence of such an assurance, the offer may be revoked at any time before acceptance. Since Stationer provided no assurance that it would hold the prices steady, its original offer, including the price term, could be revoked at any time before acceptance, and the notification of the price increase served as a revocation of the original offer as to the price term. Therefore, the price increase is effective with respect to catalog orders Stationer receives from Lawyer during the month of February.

evidence_store.text: firm offer: A firm offer is an irrevocable offer to contract under Article 2 of the Uniform Commercial Code In a sale of goods, if the seller is a merchant under the definition of a merchant in §2-104, and promises in signed writing to keep an offer open, a firm offer is created.  A firm offer will only last for the period of time stated in the offer. If no time period is stated, the offer will stay open for a maximum of three months. [Last updated in January of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team]

§ 2A-205. FIRM OFFERS. An offer by a merchant to lease goodsto or from another person in a signed writing that by its terms gives assurance it will be held open is not revocable, for lack of consideration, during the time stated or, if no time is stated, for a reasonable time, but in no event may the period of irrevocability exceed 3 months. Any such term of assurance on a form supplied by the offeree must be separately signed by the offeror.

Generally, offers can be revoked at will by the offeror, even if she has promised not to revoke for a certain period of time. There are limitations on the offeror's power to revoke. An option contract requires that the offeree give consideration for the promise by the offeror to keep the offer open. Detrimental reliance can limit an offeror's power to revoke where the offeror could reasonably expect that the offeree would rely to his detriment on the offer, and the offeree does so rely. However, this usually is limited to those situations in which the offeror would reasonably contemplate reliance by the offeree in using the offer before it is accepted; e.g., when a general contractor uses a subcontractor's bid in making its own offer. A merchant's firm offer does not apply to any offer by a merchant; it applies only to an offer under the U.C.C. for the sale of goods where a signed writing gives assurances that the offer will be held open.
Under the U.C.C., which governs here because goods are involved, a written offer signed by a merchant giving assurances that it will stay open will be irrevocable for the time stated.

The contract at issue involves the sale of goods, and is thus governed by Article 2 of the U.C.C. Under U.C.C. section 2-206, an offer to buy goods for current or prompt shipment is construed as inviting acceptance either by a promise to ship or by current or prompt shipment of conforming or nonconforming goods.

label: qa_EVIDENCE_mbe_765
subject: EVIDENCE
question: In a prosecution of Drew for forgery, the defense objects to the testimony of West, a government expert, on the ground of inadequate qualifications. The government seeks to introduce a letter from the expert's former criminology professor, stating that West is generally acknowledged in his field as well qualified. On the issue of the expert's qualifications, the letter may be considered by
final_answer:
To determine whether the letter can be considered, we must analyze the rules of evidence, particularly those concerning expert testimony and hearsay.

1. **Expert Testimony**: Under the Federal Rules of Evidence (FRE), the qualification of a person to be considered an expert is determined by the court. The court's determination is based on the expert's knowledge, skill, experience, training, or education. The letter from the expert's former criminology professor is being offered to establish West's qualifications as an expert in the field of criminology.

2. **Hearsay Rule**: Hearsay is an out-of-court statement offered in evidence to prove the truth of the matter asserted. The letter from West's former professor is indeed an out-of-court statement. However, the purpose of introducing this letter is not to prove the truth of the matter asserted (i.e., the specific facts about West's expertise) but to establish West's qualifications as an expert.

3. **Preliminary Questions**: The admissibility of evidence, including the qualification of a person to be considered an expert, is determined by the court. The FRE allows the court to consider any relevant information when determining preliminary questions concerning the admissibility of evidence.

Given these considerations:

- The letter is being offered to establish West's qualifications, which is a preliminary question to be determined by the court.
- The letter is not being offered for the truth of the matter asserted but to aid in the determination of West's qualifications.
- The court can consider the letter without regard to the hearsay rule because it is being used to determine a preliminary question, not as substantive evidence.

Therefore, the letter may be considered by the judge without regard to the hearsay rule, as it pertains to the judge's determination of the expert's qualifications.

Answer: (B)

choices.C: neither the judge nor the jury, because it is hearsay not within any exception.
choices.D: both the judge and the jury, because the letter is not offered for a hearsay purpose.

evidence_store.text: Federal Rule of Evidence 702 governs the admissibility of expert testimony. It provides that a person "qualified as an expert by knowledge, skill, experience, training, or education" may offer opinion testimony so long as:

In sum, the Federal Rules of Evidence, the Supreme Court's decision in Daubert , and the guidance of the Second Circuit require this Court, when making a decision as to whether to admit expert testimony to: (1) determine whether the witness is qualified to testify as an expert by examining the witness's educational or experiential qualifications in the relevant field; and (2) using the non-exhaustive factors enunciated as guidelines, determine whether the proposed testimony will involve the relevant specialized knowledge that will assist the trier of fact to understand or to determine a fact in issue. While the focus of the Court's inquiry must remain on the methodology rather than the conclusions, the Court is not obligated to accept conclusions that do not flow from the facts and methodologies used. Amorgianos , 303 F.3d at 266 (internal citations omitted).
At the first step of the Daubert inquiry, courts are instructed to consider the expert's qualifications. Pursuant to Rule 702 of the Federal Rules of Evidence :

Expert testimony must also adhere to the other Federal Rules of Evidence, including Rule 403, which provides that relevant evidence may still be excluded "if its probative value is substantially outweighed by a danger of one or more of the following: unfair prejudice, confusing the issues, misleading the jury, undue delay, wasting time, or needlessly presenting cumulative evidence." Fed. R. Evid. 403. The Rule 403 inquiry is particularly important in the context of expert testimony, "given the unique weight such evidence may have in a jury's deliberations." Nimely , 414 F.3d at 397 ; see also Daubert , 509 U.S. at 595, 113 S.Ct. 2786 (" 'Expert evidence can be both powerful and quite misleading because of the difficulty in evaluating it. Because of this risk, the judge in weighing possible prejudice against probative force under Rule 403 of the present rules exercises more control over experts than over lay witnesses.' " (quoting Jack B. Weinstein, Rule 702 of the Federal Rules of Evidence Is Sound; It Should Not Be Amended , 138 F.R.D. 631, 632 (1991) ) ).

The Federal Rules of Evidence distinguish between preliminary facts to be decided by the jury, which involve whether the proffered evidence is relevant, and Preliminary facts decided by the judge, which involve whether the evidence is competent, i.e., not barred by an exclusionary rule. All preliminary fact questions that determine the applicability of an exception to the hearsay rule must be determined by the judge, because the competency of the evidence will depend on that preliminary fact determination. In making this preliminary fact determination, the trial court may consider any nonprivileged relevant evidence, even though it would not otherwise be admissible under the rules of evidence. [Fed. R. Evid. 104(a)]

label: qa_nan_mbe_136
subject: nan
question: Testator devised his farm "to my son, Selden, for life, then to Selden's children and their heirs and assigns." Selden, a widower, had two unmarried, adult children. In appropriate action to construe the will, the court will determine that the remainder to children is
final_answer:
To determine the nature of the remainder to Selden's children, we must analyze the language of the will. The will devises the farm to Selden for life, then to Selden's children and their heirs and assigns. 

Since Selden is a widower with two unmarried, adult children, we know that he has children who are in existence and ascertained at the time of the testator's death. The fact that they are unmarried and adult does not affect the vesting of the remainder. 

The key phrase here is "to Selden's children and their heirs and assigns." This phrase indicates that the remainder is given to a class of persons (Selden's children) that is open, meaning that it could include additional members in the future if Selden were to have more children. However, since Selden is a widower and the will does not express a condition that the children must survive Selden or reach a certain age, the remainder vests in the children who are in existence and ascertained at the time of the testator's death.

However, because the class of Selden's children is open (he could potentially have more children), the interest of each child is subject to being partially divested if Selden were to have more children. This means that while the interest of the current children vests, it is still subject to being reduced or partially defeased if the class of beneficiaries (Selden's children) expands.

Therefore, the remainder to the children is vested, because the children are in existence and ascertained, but it is subject to partial defeasance because the class of beneficiaries is open and could include additional members in the future.

Answer: (C)

choices.A: indefeasibly vested
choices.B: contingent
choices.C: vested subject to partial defeasance
choices.D: vested subject to complete defeasance

According to the doctrine of vested remainders, a remainder is vested when it is given to a person or persons in existence and ascertained at the time of the testator's death, but it may still be subject to partial defeasance if the class of beneficiaries is open, meaning that additional members may be born or added to the class in the future. In this case, since Selden's children are in existence and ascertained at the time of the testator's death, but the class of Selden's children is open, the remainder vests in the children subject to the possibility of partial defeasance if Selden were to have more children. Therefore, the remainder to Selden's children is vested subject to partial defeasance, as the interest of each child is subject to being reduced or partially divested if the class of beneficiaries expands.

Answer: (C)

evidence_store.text: At the time of the conveyance by the landowner, the son had a life estate, the son's widow had a contingent interest (because the son's "widow" cannot be ascertained until the son's death), and the son's children had a contingent remainder (because they have not yet been born). When the grandson and the granddaughter were born, however, their interests became vested subject to open (i.e., if the son had more children). Thus, when the grandson died, he had a vested remainder subject to open that he was free to devise by will; the city zoo took his vested remainder subject to open. At the son's death, the class of his "children" closed (because the son could not have any more children), and the granddaughter's and the zoo's vested remainders subject to open became indefeasibly vested. Also at the son's death, his widow was ascertained and her interest vested in possession. Because the second wife was the son's widow, she is entitled to the valid life estate.

A remainder is a future interest created in a transferee that is capable of taking in possession on the natural termination of the preceding estate. A remainder is vested if the beneficiaries are ascertainable and their taking in possession is not subject to a condition precedent. A vested remainder created in a class of persons that is certain to take but is subject to diminution by reason of others becoming entitled to take is a vested remainder subject to open. Vested remainders may be subject to total divestment if posession is subject to being defeated by the happening of a condition subsequent.

A vested remainder is a present fixed right in the remainderman to take possession upon the natural termination of the preceding estate with no conditions precedent imposed on the time for the remainder to vest in interest.” Id. (citing Chas. W. Priddy & Co. v. Sanderford, 221 N.C. 422, 424, 20 S.E.2d 341, 343 (1942) (stating that a “remainder is vested, when, throughout its continuance, the remainderman and his heirs have the right to the immediate possession whenever and however the preceding estate is determined; or, in other words, a remainder is vested if, so long as it lasts, the only obstacle to the right of immediate possession by the remainderman is the existence of the preceding estate; or, again, a remainder is vested if it is subject to no condition precedent save the determination of the preceding estate.”)).

remainder subject to open: In property law, a remainder subject to open is a remainder left: (1) in the interest of a class of persons; one or more of whom are alive; and (2) subject to diminution by another person joining the class of persons. One common example of a remainder subject to open is a will leaving an estate to an heir and their heir's children. In such a case, the heir's children who are alive at the time of conveyance have an interest subject to open and will share that interest with any other of the heir's children who are born after the conveyance. (A conveys to B for life, then to B's children. B has one child, C, alive at the time of conveyance. C has an interested subject to open, which will be shared with C's siblings).  A remainder interest is vested (subject to open) rather than contingent if one member of the class is alive at conveyance. However, such an interest should not be confused with a contingent, and therefore defeasible, interest in clauses that grant the interest to the surviving members of a class. For example, North Carolina has held that if the clause of will states that a child has to survive the life tenant in order to acquire interest in the property, then the child's interest is contingent and subject to defeasance if the condition (survival) is not met.  See also: Future Interest [Last updated in May of 2022 by the Wex Definitions Team]

To distinguish between vested remainders and contingent remainders, a court must determine whether at the time the instrument takes effect there is “a person who in his own right, or as a part of his estate, would take all of this property if [the life estate] ended now.” If there is such a person, then the remainder is vested subject to partial or complete defeasance. If no such person is identifiable, then the remainder is subject to a condition precedent and is a contingent remainder. In determining the type of remainder created, Georgia has consistently followed two common law principles: (a) the law favors construing “conditions to be subsequent” and (b) the law favors the “vesting of remainders in all cases of doubt,” which is also called the “early vesting” of remainders. Professor Chaffin has noted that “[a]lthough it is possible to create nondescendible defeasible remainders by using very clear conditions of survivorship by way of condition subsequent, it is difficult to do so in view of the preference for defeasibly vested rather than contingent interests.”

label: qa_nan_mbe_248
subject: nan
question: On Ladd's claim against Garage, will Ladd prevail?
final_answer:
To determine whether Ladd will prevail against Garage, let's analyze each option step by step:

(A) Strict liability in tort typically applies to certain types of activities or products that are inherently dangerous, such as keeping wild animals or manufacturing defective products. Garage's inspection of Commuter's brakes does not inherently fall into a category where strict liability would automatically apply without considering negligence or other factors. Thus, option (A) is not the best answer.

(B) If Garage was negligent in inspecting Commuter's brakes, this negligence could lead to liability for any damages caused by that negligence, including the harm to Ladd. This option directly addresses a potential breach of duty by Garage that resulted in harm, making it a plausible basis for Ladd's claim.

(C) The legal category of a bystander is relevant in cases involving emotional distress or other indirect harms. However, the question does not specify that Ladd is suing for bystander claims (e.g., emotional distress from witnessing the accident) but rather implies a direct claim against Garage. Thus, Ladd's status as a bystander or not does not directly answer the question of prevailing against Garage based on the information given.

(D) The concept of an independent and superseding cause refers to a situation where an intervening event breaks the chain of causation between the original tortfeasor's actions and the harm suffered. While Sand's conduct could potentially be seen as an intervening cause, determining whether it is an independent and superseding cause would depend on specifics not provided in the question, such as the nature of Sand's actions and their foreseeability.

Given the information provided and analyzing each option, the most direct and relevant factor in determining Ladd's ability to prevail against Garage is whether Garage was negligent in its inspection of Commuter's brakes, as this directly addresses a potential breach of duty by Garage that could have caused Ladd's harm.

Answer: (B)

choices.A: Yes, because Garage is strictly liable in tort.
choices.B: Yes, if Garage was negligent in inspecting Commuter's brakes.
choices.C: No, if Ladd was in the legal category of a bystander.
choices.D: No, because Sand 's conduct was an independent and superseding cause.

gold_passage: the essential elements of negligent inspection are the same as general negligence, i.e., the existence of a legal duty, breach of that duty, damages, and a causal connection between the defendant's conduct and the plaintiff's injury.

Answer: (B)
hyde_passage:
According to the principles of negligence law, a plaintiff must prove that the defendant owed a duty of care, breached that duty, and that the breach caused the plaintiff's harm. In the context of professional services, such as vehicle inspections, the standard of care is typically defined as the level of skill and care that a reasonably competent practitioner would exercise in similar circumstances. If Garage failed to meet this standard in inspecting Commuter's brakes, and this failure directly caused Ladd's harm, then Garage may be liable for negligence. The doctrine of negligence applies when a defendant's breach of a duty of care causes harm to another, making option (B) the correct basis for Ladd's potential claim against Garage. 

Answer: (B)

evidence_store.text: To establish a prima face case for negligence, the following elements must be proved: (i) the existence of a duty on the part of defendant to conform to a specific standard of conduct for the protection of the plaintiff against an unreasonable risk of injury; (ii) breach of that duty by defendant; (iii) the breach of the duty by defendant was the actual and proximate cause of plaintiff's injury; and (iv) damage to the plaintiff's person or property. As a general matter, no legal duty is imposed upon any person to affirmatively act for the benefit of others. However, the existence of a special relationship between the parties may create a duty. Modern cases extend the duty to employers when employees are injured in the course of employment. An act or omission to act is the cause in fact of an injury when the injury would not have occurred but for the act. The "but for" test applies where several acts combine to cause the injury, but none of the acts standing alone would have been sufficient. But for any of the acts, the injury would not have occurred. The general rule of proximate cause is that defendant is liable for all harmful results that are the normal incidents of and within the increased risk caused by his acts. This is an indirect cause case because an independent intervening force came into motion after the negligent conduct and combined with it to cause the injury. Independent intervening forces are foreseeable (and thus do not cut off defendant's liability) where defendant's negligence increased the risk that these forces would cause harm to the plaintiff.

"[T]he responsibility for determining whether the defendant has breached a duty owed to the plaintiff entails a determination of what a reasonably prudent person would foresee, what an unreasonable risk of injury would be, and what would constitute an exercise of ordinary care in light of all the surrounding circumstances." Herrera v. Quality Pontiac, 2003-NMSC-018, ¶ 33, 134 N.M. 43, 73 P.3d at 194. "The finder of fact must determine whether Defendant breached the duty of ordinary care by considering what a reasonably prudent individual would foresee, what an unreasonable risk of injury would be, and what would constitute an exercise of ordinary care in light of all surrounding circumstances." Herrera v. Quality Pontiac, 2003-NMSC-018, ¶ 33, 134 N.M. 43, 73 P.3d at 195.

Under Kentucky law, "[i]n order to state a cause of action based on negligence, a plaintiff must establish a duty on the defendant, a breach of the duty, and a causal connection between the breach of the duty and an injury suffered by the plaintiff." Lewis v. B & R. Corp. , 56 S.W.3d 432, 436-437 (Ky. Ct. App. 2001). As Johnson points out, under Kentucky law, "[a] driver of an automobile that strikes another in the rear is not subject to strict liability, but rather must be proven to have violated the duty of ordinary care before he can be found to be at fault." USAA Cas. Ins. v. Kramer , 987 S.W.2d 779, 782 (Ky. 1999) (citing Lucas v. Davis , 409 S.W.2d 297, 299-300 (Ky. 1966) ).

2. Merits. In order to succeed on her negligence claims, the plaintiff must first establish that the defendants owed the decedent a duty of reasonable care. See Jupin v. Kask, 447 Mass. 141, 146, 849 N.E.2d 829 (2006). "[T]he existence of a duty is a question of law, and is thus an appropriate subject of summary judgment." Id. "That is because such questions are resolved 'by reference to existing social values and customs and appropriate social policy.' " Pantazis v. Mack Trucks, Inc., 92 Mass. App. Ct. 477, 483, 87 N.E.3d 1191 (2017), quoting Jupin, supra at 143, 849 N.E.2d 829.

"The threshold issue in any negligence action is whether the defendant owed the plaintiff a duty." Rando v. ANCO Insulations, Inc. , 08-1163, 08-1169, p. 27 (La. 5/22/09), 16 So.3d 1065, 1086. Whether a duty is owed is a question of law for the court to decide, subject to de novo review. Id. ; Burch v. SMG Schindler Elev. Corp. , 14-1356, 14-1357, 14-1358, p. 6 (La. App. 4 Cir. 4/7/16), 191 So.3d 652, 658. "There is an almost universal duty on the part of the defendant in a negligence action to use reasonable care to avoid injury to another." Rando , 08-1163, p. 27, 16 So.3d at 1086. "In general, the duty owed by a business owner to its customers is that of reasonable care." Caronia v. McKenzie's Pastry Shoppes , 97-0681, p. 3 (La. App. 4 Cir. 10/1/97), 700 So.2d 1315, 1317. "This duty extends to keeping the premises safe from unreasonable risks of harm or warning persons of known dangers." Id. "[T]he basic standard is that the defendant must exercise the degree of care that we might expect from an ordinarily prudent person under the same or similar circumstance." Chatman , 15-1179, p. 14, 197 So.3d at 376. A reasonably prudent person will avoid creating an unreasonable risk of harm. Id.

label: qa_REAL PROP._mbe_934
subject: REAL PROP.
question: Oren owned Purpleacre, a tract of land, in fee simple. By will duly admitted to probate after his death, Oren devised Purpleacre to "any wife who survives me with remainder to such of my children as are living at her death." Oren was survived by Wen, his wife, and by three children, Cynthia, Cam, and Camelia. Thereafter, Cam died and by will duly admitted to probate devised his entire estate to David. Cynthia and Camelia were Cam's heirs at law. Later Wen died. In appropriate lawsuit to which Cy
final_answer:
To solve this, let's break down the information provided and analyze it step by step:

1. **Oren's Will**: Oren devises Purpleacre to his surviving wife (Wen) with a remainder to such of his children as are living at her (Wen's) death. This creates a life estate in Wen and a contingent remainder in Oren's children who survive Wen.

2. **Death of Cam**: Before Wen's death, one of Oren's children, Cam, dies, leaving a will that devises his entire estate to David. This means David will take any interest Cam had in Purpleacre, but only if Cam had a vested interest that could be passed on.

3. **Nature of the Remainder**: The remainder to Oren's children is contingent upon them surviving Wen. At the time of Oren's death, it was not certain which of his children would survive Wen, making the remainder contingent.

4. **Rule Against Perpetuities (RAP)**: The RAP is not violated here because the remainder is contingent upon the children surviving Wen, not upon an unborn or unascertained person. The class of Oren's children is fixed at his death, and the remainder vests (becomes certain) at Wen's death, which is within a life in being (Wen's life) plus 21 years and 10 months (the period of gestation), thus satisfying the RAP.

5. **Devisability of Remainders**: In general, vested remainders are devisable, but contingent remainders are not devisable until they vest. However, since Cam died before Wen, his interest in Purpleacre had not yet vested. Despite this, the key issue here is not the devisability of the remainder but who takes the property after Wen's death among those who are parties to the lawsuit.

6. **Outcome**: After Wen's death, the remainder vests in those of Oren's children who are then alive, which are Cynthia and Camelia. Cam, having predeceased Wen, does not survive to take the vested remainder, and thus his estate (which includes any potential interest in Purpleacre) passes to David according to Cam's will. However, since Cam's interest in Purpleacre was contingent and had not vested at the time of his death, David does not take any interest in Purpleacre through Cam.

Given this analysis, the correct answer is that title to Purpleacre is in Cynthia and Camelia because they are the ones who survived Wen and thus took the vested remainder. Cam's prior death means he does not take the property, and his will devising his estate to David does not affect the outcome regarding Purpleacre since Cam's interest had not vested.

Answer: (D)

choices.A: Cynthia, Camelia, and David, because the earliest vesting of remainders is favored and reference to Wen's death should be construed as relating to time of taking possession.
choices.B: Cynthia, Camelia, and David, because the provision requiring survival of children violates the Rule Against Perpetuities since the surviving wife might have been a person unborn at the time of writing of the will.
choices.C: Cynthia and Camelia, because Cam's remainder must descend by intestacy and is not devisable.
choices.D: Cynthia and Camelia, because the remainders were contingent upon surviving the life tenant.

According to the doctrine of contingent remainders, a remainder is contingent if it is subject to a condition precedent that must be satisfied before the interest can vest. In this case, the remainder to Oren's children is contingent upon them surviving Wen, making it a contingent remainder. The rule is that a contingent remainder does not vest until the condition is satisfied, and in this scenario, the condition is satisfied at Wen's death, at which point the remainder vests in those of Oren's children who are then alive, namely Cynthia and Camelia. Since Cam predeceased Wen, his interest in Purpleacre never vested, and thus his estate, which passed to David, does not include any interest in Purpleacre. Therefore, title to Purpleacre is in Cynthia and Camelia, as they are the ones who survived Wen and took the vested remainder. 

Answer: (D)

evidence_store.text: remainder subject to a condition precedent: Remainder subject to a condition precedent is also known as a contingent remainder. It refers to future interest in property that will pass to a person or entity only if certain circumstances exist at the time of the title-holder's death or will pass to an unknown person or entity. Examples of potential circumstances include surviving one’s brother or still operating the family’s farming business.  See also: contingent remainder [Last updated in September of 2021 by the Wex Definitions Team]

Under the Rule Against Perpetuities, an interest in property is not valid unless it will vest, if at all, not later than 21 years after a life in being at the creation of the interest. The validity of interests under the Rule is determined at the time the interests are created, taking into account the facts then existing. The "lives in being plus 21 years" period begins to run, and the measuring lives used to show the validity of an interest must be in existence, at that time. An interest is classified as a contingent remainder if the condition precedent of their reaching a certain age before taking an interest. An est would not have been vested if their taking was subject to a condition precedent. As a rule of thumb, remainders always follow life estates. An executory interest is an interest that divests the interest of another.

To distinguish between vested remainders and contingent remainders, a court must determine whether at the time the instrument takes effect there is “a person who in his own right, or as a part of his estate, would take all of this property if [the life estate] ended now.” If there is such a person, then the remainder is vested subject to partial or complete defeasance. If no such person is identifiable, then the remainder is subject to a condition precedent and is a contingent remainder. In determining the type of remainder created, Georgia has consistently followed two common law principles: (a) the law favors construing “conditions to be subsequent” and (b) the law favors the “vesting of remainders in all cases of doubt,” which is also called the “early vesting” of remainders. Professor Chaffin has noted that “[a]lthough it is possible to create nondescendible defeasible remainders by using very clear conditions of survivorship by way of condition subsequent, it is difficult to do so in view of the preference for defeasibly vested rather than contingent interests.”

A contingent remainder “is merely the possibility or prospect of an estate which exists when what would otherwise be a vested remainder is subject to a condition precedent, or is given to an uncertain person or persons.” Id. at 1019 (Ind.Ct.App.2005) (citation omitted). A remainder is vested when there is an immediate right of present enjoyment or a present fixed right of future enjoyment; that is, when it is given to an ascertained person and is not subject to a condition precedent. Bailey v. Bailey, 142 Ind.App. 119, 127, 232 N.E.2d 372, 378 (1967).

In the present case, the Timber Buyer argues that the Grandchildren have no standing to sue for damages because they were mere contingent remaindermen when the trees were cut. We conclude, though, that it is irrelevant whether the Grandchildren's remaindermen interest in the Property was vested or contingent under Mr. Burden's will: They did not bring suit until after Mrs. Bazemore's death, after their interest became a vested fee simple interest. Though neither party cites a case on point on this issue, we conclude that once a contingent remainderman's interest vests, he may bring suit for damages, even for acts committed during the life tenancy. Indeed, in discussing the limited right of a contingent remainderman to seek only injunctive relief, our Supreme Court stated that a contingent remainderman "could not maintain [an] action [for damages] during the life of the first taker ." Latham v. Roanoke R. & Lumber Co. , 139 N.C. 9, 51 S.E. 780, 780 (1905) (emphasis added). Our Supreme Court reasoned that, during the life tenancy, it is impossible to know what, if any, damage any particular contingent remainderman will suffer or which remainderman will vest and actually will suffer the damage. Id. at 11-12, 51 S.E. at 780-81. But once the life tenancy terminates, this concern goes away. Further, our General Assembly has provided that any remainderman whose interest has become a vested present interest may sue for damages for timber cut during the preceding life tenancy. N.C. Gen. Stat. § 1-537 (2017) ("Every heir may bring action for waste committed on lands ... of his own inheritance, as well in the time of his ancestor as in his own.")

label: qa_CONST. LAW_mbe_1053
subject: CONST. LAW
question: A city ordinance requires a taxicab operator's license to operate a taxicab in King City. The ordinance states that the sole criteria for the issuance of such a license are driving ability and knowledge of the geography of King City. An applicant is tested by the city for these qualifications with a detailed questionnaire, written and oral examinations, and a practical behind-the-wheel demonstration. The ordinance does not limit the number of licenses that may be issued. It does, however, allow
final_answer:
To answer this question, we must analyze the situation in light of the principles of due process and the protection of property interests.

First, let's consider John's claim. John is a licensed taxicab driver who objects to the issuance of a license to Sandy because it would impair the value of his existing license. He demands a hearing to prove this claim. The key issue here is whether John has a protected property interest that would trigger due process protections, including the right to a hearing.

The due process clause of the Fourteenth Amendment protects individuals from deprivation of life, liberty, or property by the state without due process of law. This includes the right to a hearing when a person's property interests are at stake. However, not all interests are considered "property" for due process purposes.

In this case, John's interest is in maintaining the value of his taxicab license, which he believes would be diminished by the issuance of another license. However, the ordinance does not limit the number of licenses that may be issued, and it does not guarantee John a certain level of profit or protection from competition. Therefore, John does not have a protected property interest in being free from competition.

The city ordinance provides for an objection process, but only on the grounds that an applicant does not possess the required qualifications. Since Sandy has been found qualified, and John's objection is based on the potential economic impact on his own license, it does not fall within the grounds for objection specified in the ordinance.

Given these considerations, the court should rule for city officials because the licensing ordinance does not give John any property interest in being free of competition from additional licensees.

Answer: (D)

choices.A: John, because the due process clause of the Fourteenth Amendment requires all persons whose property may be adversely affected by governmental action to be given an opportunity for a hearing before such action occurs.
choices.B: John, because the determination of whether to hold a hearing may not constitutionally be left to the discretion of the same officials whose action is being challenged.
choices.C: city officials, because John had the benefit of the licensing ordinance and, therefore, may not now question actions taken under it.
choices.D: city officials, because the licensing ordinance does not give John any property interest in being free of competition from additional licensees.

gold_passage: Property interests are not generally constitutionally established; rather, “they are created and their dimensions are defined by existing rules or understandings that stem from an independent source such as state law-rules or understandings that secure certain benefits and that support claims of entitlement to those benefits.” Bd. of Regents of State Colls. v. Roth, 408 U.S. 564, 577, 92 S.Ct. 2701, 33 L.Ed.2d 548 (1972).
