evidence_store.text: The doctrine of part performance may be used to enforce an otherwise invalid oral contract of sale, provided the acts of part performance unequivocally prove the existence of the contract.

Therefore, in order to obtain specific performance of an enforceable contract for the purchase and sale of real property, the statute of frauds requires the contract be in writing and signed by the party against whom enforcement is sought. See India Am. Trading Co., Inc. v. White, 896 So.2d 859, 860 (Fla. 3d DCA 2005) ("Pursuant to the statute [of frauds], no action can be brought to enforce a contract for the sale of land unless the contract is in writing and signed by the party to be charged." (quoting Cavallaro v. Stratford Homes, Inc., 784 So.2d 619, 621 (Fla. 5th DCA 2001). Thus, this Court has held that the statute of frauds requires satisfaction of the following two conditions in order to obtain specific performance of a contract for the sale of real property: (i) "the contract must be a writing signed by the party against whom enforcement is sought," and (ii) "the writing must contain all of the essential terms of the sale and these terms may not be explained by resort to parol evidence." Fox v. Sails at Laguna Club Dev. Corp., 403 So.2d 456, 458 (Fla. 3d DCA 1981) (citing Rundel v. Gordon, 92 Fla. 1110, 111 So. 386 (1927) ). As a general rule, "[t]here is no definitive list of essential terms that must be present and certain to satisfy the statute of frauds. Rather, the essential terms will vary widely according to the nature and complexity of each transaction and will be evaluated on a case by case basis ...." Socarras v. Claughton Hotels, Inc., 374 So.2d 1057, 1060 (Fla. 3d DCA 1979).

To obtain specific performance, there must be an enforceable contract between the parties. Pursuant to the Statute of Frauds, a contract for the sale of land is not enforceable unless it is evidenced by a writing signed by the party sought to be bound. In addition to this signature, the writing should contain a recital of consideration, the terms and conditions of the agreement, the identity of the party sought to be charged, and an identification of the contractual subject matter. A purchaser of land is almost always deemed to have an inadequate remedy at law. Each parcel of land is always considered unique; i.e., unlike any other that could be purchased with the same sum of money. Application of the factors that courts use to determine whether a third party is an intended beneficiary who can enforce a contract between the contracting parties or an incidental beneficiary who has no rights under the contract indicates that the buyer is not an intended beneficiary. The agreement did not: (i) expressly designate a third party; (ii) indicate that performance was to be made directly to a third party; or (iii) state that a third party had any rights under the contract.

a. Statute of Frauds. The Statute of Frauds generally requires that all contracts for the sale of land be in writing. G. L. c. 259, § 1. See Bibi v. Courville, 357 Mass. 782 (1970). However, "[a] plaintiff's detrimental reliance on, or part performance of, an oral agreement to convey property may estop the defendant from pleading the Statute of Frauds as a defense." Nessralla v. Peck, 403 Mass. 757, 761 (1989). Payment alone is insufficient to show part performance. See Andrews v. Charon, 289 Mass. 1, 5-6 (1935). Part performance may be demonstrated by evidence that the party physically occupied and made improvements to the property in reliance upon the agreement. Alexander v. Snell, 12 Mass. App. Ct. 323, 325 (1981). Sullivan did not allege that he attempted to improve these parcels, nor does Sullivan assert on appeal that he occupied either parcel. Accordingly, there was no part performance and the claims are barred by the Statute of Frauds.

A promise creating an interest in land must be in writing to be enforceable. This includes not only agreements for the sale of real property or an interest therein, but also leases for more than one year. However, under the part performance doctrine, conduct that unequivocally indicates that the parties have contracted for the sale of land will take the contract out of the Statute of Frauds.

label: qa_EVIDENCE_mbe_1129
subject: EVIDENCE
question: Passenger is suing Defendant for injuries suffered in the crash of a small airplane, alleging that Defendant had owned the plane and negligently failed to have it properly maintained. Defendant has asserted in defense that he never owned the plane or had any responsibility to maintain it. At trial, Passenger calls Witness to testify that Witness had sold to Defendant a liability insurance policy on the plane. The testimony of Witness is
final_answer:
To determine the admissibility of Witness's testimony about selling a liability insurance policy on the plane to Defendant, we need to consider the purpose for which the testimony is being offered and the relevance of the insurance policy to the issues in the case.

First, let's address the options provided:

(A) The original document rule, also known as the best evidence rule (Federal Rule of Evidence 1002), requires that to prove the contents of a writing, recording, or photograph, the original writing, recording, or photograph is required, with certain exceptions. This rule does not apply directly to the testimony about the sale of an insurance policy, as the policy itself is not being offered to prove its contents. Thus, option (A) is incorrect.

(B) This option refers to the principle behind Federal Rule of Evidence 411, which states that evidence of liability insurance is not admissible to prove whether a person acted negligently or otherwise wrongfully. However, this rule also allows for the admission of such evidence for other purposes, such as proving agency, ownership, or control. Since the issue here involves ownership and responsibility, this option might seem applicable at first glance, but it overlooks the potential relevance of the insurance policy to these issues.

(C) The motivation behind Defendant's maintenance (or lack thereof) of the airplane is not directly relevant to the issue of whether Defendant owned the plane or had responsibility for its maintenance. While the presence of liability insurance might suggest some level of responsibility or ownership, the motivation to invest in maintenance is more closely related to negligence allegations, which are not the primary focus of the insurance policy evidence in this context.

(D) This option suggests that the testimony about the liability insurance policy is admissible as evidence of Defendant's ownership of or responsibility for the airplane. Given the context of the case, where Defendant denies owning the plane or having any responsibility for its maintenance, evidence that Defendant purchased liability insurance for the plane could be seen as relevant to the issue of ownership or control. Federal Rule of Evidence 411 allows for the admission of liability insurance evidence for purposes other than proving negligence, such as proving ownership or control, which aligns with the needs of this case.

Therefore, considering the purpose for which the testimony is being offered and the relevance of the insurance policy to the issues in the case, the most appropriate answer is:

Answer: (D)

choices.A: inadmissible, because the policy itself is required under the original document rule.
choices.B: inadmissible, because of the rule against proof of insurance where insurance is not itself at issue.
choices.C: admissible to show that Defendant had little motivation to invest money in maintenance of the airplane.
choices.D: admissible as some evidence of Defendant's ownership of or responsibility for the airplane.

But the court may admit this evidence for another purpose, such as proving a witness’s bias or prejudice or proving agency, ownership, or control.
snap_answer:
To determine the admissibility of Witness's testimony, let's analyze the situation step by step:

1. **Relevance and Purpose**: The primary issue here is whether Defendant owned the plane or had responsibility for its maintenance. Witness's testimony about selling a liability insurance policy on the plane to Defendant could be relevant because it implies Defendant had an interest in the plane, potentially indicating ownership or responsibility.

2. **Original Document Rule (A)**: This rule generally requires that the original document be introduced into evidence when the contents of the document are sought to be proved. However, in this case, Witness's testimony is not about the contents of the insurance policy itself but rather about the fact that Defendant purchased such a policy, which can imply ownership or responsibility.

3. **Rule Against Proof of Insurance (B)**: This rule typically prohibits evidence of insurance to prove negligence or wrongful conduct. The rationale is to prevent the jury from being misled into thinking the defendant is more likely to have been negligent because they have insurance. However, this rule does not apply when the evidence of insurance is used for a purpose other than proving negligence, such as proving ownership or control.

4. **Motivation for Maintenance (C)**: While the existence of an insurance policy might suggest something about Defendant's motivations or financial situation, this is not the most direct or relevant use of Witness's testimony in this context. The primary issue is whether Defendant owned or was responsible for the plane, not his motivations regarding maintenance.

5. **Evidence of Ownership or Responsibility (D)**: The fact that Defendant purchased liability insurance on the plane can be seen as evidence that he had an interest in the plane, which supports the inference that he owned it or had responsibility for it. This is because people typically insure things they own or for which they are responsible.

Given these considerations, the most appropriate answer is that Witness's testimony is admissible as some evidence of Defendant's ownership of or responsibility for the airplane. This directly addresses the central issue in the case without running afoul of the rules against introducing evidence of insurance to prove negligence or requiring the original document.

Answer: (D)

Therefore, under Rule 411 of the Federal Rules of Evidence, evidence of insurance may be admissible if it is used for a purpose other than proving negligence, such as proving ownership or responsibility for the airplane.

label: qa_nan_mbe_175
subject: nan
question: Which of the following is Bertha's best legal theory?
final_answer:
To determine Bertha's best legal theory, let's analyze each option in the context of the provided passages, which discuss contract law principles such as material breach, substantial compliance, and the effects of breach on contractual obligations.

(A) **Her acting contract with Albert was legally severable into weekly units.** This option suggests that the contract could be divided into separate, independent parts (in this case, weekly units), where the breach of one part does not affect the others. However, without specific information about the contract's structure or the nature of the obligations, it's difficult to assess the applicability of this theory directly from the provided passages.

(B) **Her performance of the literal terms of the contract was physically impossible.** This option implies a defense based on impossibility of performance, which can indeed discharge contractual obligations if the impossibility is not the fault of the party invoking it. However, the passages provided do not directly address impossibility as a defense.

(C) **Her reliance on the engagement with Albert by declining another acting role created an estoppel against Albert.** Estoppel can prevent a party from denying or asserting something that is contrary to what they have previously stated or done, when such a statement or action has been relied upon by another party to their detriment. While estoppel is a powerful legal principle, the passages do not specifically discuss estoppel in the context of contractual obligations or breaches.

(D) **Her failure to perform for one week was not a material failure so as to discharge Albert's duty to perform.** This option directly addresses the concept of material breach, which is extensively discussed in the provided passages. A material breach is one that goes to the heart of the contract, defeating its purpose or substantially depriving the other party of the expected benefit. If Bertha's failure to perform for one week is deemed not material, then Albert's duty to perform would not be discharged. This aligns with the discussions in the passages about what constitutes a material breach and its effects on contractual obligations.

Given the information provided and the focus on material breach in the passages, **(D) Her failure to perform for one week was not a material failure so as to discharge Albert's duty to perform** appears to be the most directly relevant and applicable legal theory among the options provided. This theory aligns with the principles outlined in the passages regarding material breach and its impact on contractual obligations.

Answer: (D)

choices.A: Her acting contract with Albert was legally severable into weekly units.
choices.B: Her performance of the literal terms of the contract was physically impossible.
choices.C: Her reliance on the engagement with Albert by declining another acting role created an estoppel against Albert.
choices.D: Her failure to perform for one week was not a material failure so as to discharge Albert's duty to perform.

gold_passage: A material breach of contract “is a non-performance of a duty that is so material and important as to justify the injured party in regarding the whole transaction as at an end.” *6 Jenkins, Inc. v. Walsh Bros., Inc., 2001 ME 98, ¶ 13, 776 A.2d 1229, 1234 (quotation marks omitted); see also Forrest Assocs. v. Passamaquoddy Tribe, 2000 ME 195, ¶ 9, 760 A.2d 1041, 1044 (stating that “whether a breach has occurred ... [is a] question [ ] of fact”).
snap_answer:
To determine Bertha's best legal theory, let's analyze each option step by step:

(A) If the contract with Albert was legally severable into weekly units, this would mean that each week's performance is considered a separate contract. However, this does not directly address the issue of Bertha's failure to perform for one week and its impact on the entire contract. Severability might help in certain contractual disputes, but it doesn't necessarily alleviate the consequences of non-performance in this context.

(B) The argument that her performance of the literal terms of the contract was physically impossible could be a strong one if there were circumstances beyond Bertha's control that made it impossible for her to perform. However, the information provided does not specify such circumstances, making this option less clear as the best legal theory without additional context.

(C) Estoppel is a legal principle that can prevent a party from denying or asserting something that is contrary to what they have previously stated or done, when such a statement or action has been relied upon by another party to their detriment. If Bertha relied on her engagement with Albert and declined another acting role because of it, and if Albert knew or should have known about this reliance, then Albert might be estopped from claiming that Bertha's failure to perform for one week discharges his obligations. However, this would depend on the specifics of their communications and the terms of their agreement.

(D) The argument that her failure to perform for one week was not a material failure so as to discharge Albert's duty to perform is a contractual analysis based on the principle that not all breaches of contract will discharge the other party's obligations. A material breach is one that is significant enough to justify the other party in treating the contract as ended. If Bertha's failure to perform for just one week out of a longer contract is not considered material (especially if the contract has a long duration and this is an isolated incident), then Albert's duty to perform might still be intact.

Considering these options, the best legal theory for Bertha, given the goal of minimizing the impact of her non-performance on the contract, would be to argue that her failure to perform for one week was not significant enough to discharge Albert's obligations under the contract. This approach directly addresses the issue at hand (the breach and its consequences) and is based on standard contractual principles regarding material breach.

Answer: (D)
hyde_passage:
According to the doctrine of material breach, a party's failure to perform a contractual obligation will only discharge the other party's obligations if the breach is significant enough to justify such a response. A material breach is typically defined as one that goes to the heart of the contract, depriving the non-breaching party of the substantial benefit they were expecting to receive. In determining whether a breach is material, courts consider factors such as the extent to which the breach affects the purpose of the contract and the degree to which the non-breaching party is injured by the breach. 

Answer: (D)

evidence_store.text: A party's uncured material breach of a contract can suspend or discharge the other party's obligation to perform. See E. Allan Farnsworth, Contracts § 8.16, at 638–40 (2d ed.1990); see, e.g., Elec. Distribs., Inc. v. SFR, Inc., 166 F.3d 1074, 1086 (10th Cir.1999) (noting that a material breach excuses the other party's performance under Colorado law). The Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 237 (1981) provides that “it is a condition of each party's remaining duties to render performances ... that there be no uncured material failure by the other party to render any such performance due at an earlier time.” According to the Restatement, this doctrine is based on the principle that where performances are to be exchanged under an exchange of promises, each party is entitled to the assurance that he [or she] will not be called upon to perform his [or her] remaining duties of performance with respect to the expected exchange if there has already been an uncured material failure of performance by the other party.

The court also concluded that Scott breached the agreement. "When performance of a duty under a contract is due, any nonperformance is a breach." McCausland v. Wagner , 78 A.3d 1093, 1101 (Pa.Super. 2013). "If a breach constitutes a material failure of performance, the non-breaching party is relieved from any obligation to perform; thus, a party who has materially breached a contract may not insist upon performance of the contract by the non-breaching party." Id.

Defendants further contend the Settlement Agreement must not be enforced against them because they have substantially complied with their obligations. (Dkt. 536 at 19-20). Substantial compliance or substantial performance "does not constitute a complete defense to liability for breach of contract, but is designed rather to prevent total forfeiture by a contracting party who is guilty of only trivial breaches of contract." Ballou v. Basic Const. Co. , 407 F.2d 1137, 1140 (4th Cir. 1969). "Substantial compliance is the inverse of the proposition that a breach of the contract must be 'material' or significant...." Culpeper Reg'l Hosp. v. Jones , 64 Va. App. 207, 214, 767 S.E.2d 236 (Va. Ct. App. 2015) ; e.g. , Bile v. RREMC, LLC , No. 3:15CV051, 2016 WL 4487864, at *6 (E.D. Va. Aug. 24, 2016). "A material breach is a failure to do something that is so fundamental to the contract that the failure to perform that obligation defeats an essential purpose of the contract." Va. Elec. & Power Co. v. Bransen Energy, Inc. , 850 F.3d 645, 655 (4th Cir. 2017) (quoting Horton v. Horton , 254 Va. 111, 115, 487 S.E.2d 200 (Va. 1997) ). So if a defendant has substantially complied, his contractual shortcomings are by definition immaterial.

A material breach is one that permits the other party to rescind the contract. Borah v. McCandless, 147 Idaho 73, 79, 205 P.3d 1209, 1215 (2009). Such a breach is more than incidental-a material breach "touches the fundamental purpose of the contract, defeating the object of the parties entering into the agreement." Id.

"When performance of a duty under a contract is contemplated, any nonperformance of that duty is a breach." Spann v. Lovett & Co., Ltd. , 2012 Ark. App. 107, at 21, 389 S.W.3d 77, 93. "Usually, whether a breach of contract occurred is a question of fact." Id. "A 'first breach' by one contracting party may release the other party from its contractual duties if the first breach is material and sufficiently serious." Id. "A material breach is a failure to perform an essential term or condition that substantially defeats the purpose of the contract for the other party." Id. "[A] relatively minor failure of performance on the part of one party," however, "does not justify the other party in seeking to escape any responsibility under the terms of the contract." Id. Under these standards, we affirm the circuit court's judgment.

label: qa_nan_mbe_402
subject: nan
question: Pine, aged 25, contemplates marrying Ross, aged 25. Both are residents of the state of Champlain. Pine has not yet proposed to Ross because he is offended by the counseling requirement. Pine sues in federal court seeking a declaratory judgment that the Young Adult Marriage Counseling Act is unconstitutional. Which of the following is the clearest ground for dismissal of this action by the court?
final_answer:
To determine the clearest ground for dismissal of this action by the court, let's analyze each option step by step:

(A) Pine and Ross are residents of the same state. - This fact alone does not provide a clear ground for dismissal, as the issue at hand involves the constitutionality of a state law, which can be reviewed in federal court regardless of the residency of the parties involved, provided other conditions for federal jurisdiction are met.

(B) No substantial federal question is presented. - The constitutionality of the Young Adult Marriage Counseling Act does present a federal question, as it involves the potential violation of constitutional rights (e.g., the right to marry, equal protection, due process). Therefore, this option does not seem to be the strongest ground for dismissal.

(C) The suit presents a nonjusticiable political question. - Political questions are typically those that the Constitution commits either to another branch of government or those that are inherently incapable of resolution through the judicial process. The challenge to the Young Adult Marriage Counseling Act does not clearly fall into these categories, as it involves the judicial review of a statute's constitutionality, a task well within the judiciary's purview.

(D) The suit is unripe. - Ripeness refers to the requirement that an actual, ongoing dispute between parties, with a genuine threat of immediate injury, must exist for a court to hear the case. In this scenario, Pine has not yet proposed to Ross and is contemplating marriage, which means he has not yet been directly affected by the law. The injury (being required to undergo counseling before marriage) is speculative at this point because Pine and Ross have not taken any concrete steps towards getting married that would trigger the counseling requirement. Therefore, the case is not ripe for judicial review because there is no immediate, concrete harm or injury.

Considering these options, the clearest ground for dismissal is that the suit is unripe because Pine has not yet been directly affected by the law and the potential injury is speculative at this stage.

Answer: (D)

choices.A: Pine and Ross are residents of the same state.
choices.B: No substantial federal question is presented.
choices.C: The suit presents a nonjusticiable political question.
choices.D: The suit is unripe.

Gardner, which held that the question of ripeness turns on the likelihood of imminent injury. evidence_store.text: "[T]he best way to think of constitutional ripeness is as a specific application of the actual injury aspect of Article III standing. ... [T]o say a plaintiff's claim is constitutionally unripe is to say the plaintiff's claimed injury, if any, is not 'actual or imminent,' but instead 'conjectural or hypothetical.' " National Organization for Marriage, Inc. v. Walsh , 714 F.3d 682, 688 (2d Cir. 2013) (quoting Lujan , 504 U.S. at 560, 112 S.Ct. 2130 ); see also Bronx Household of Faith v. Board of Educ. of City of New York , 492 F.3d 89, 111 (2d Cir. 2007) (Leval, J., concurring) ("The concept of ripeness assumes that the relationship between the parties might at some point ripen into an injury sufficiently direct and realized to satisfy the requirements of Article III standing. It recognizes, however, that some disputes mature in stages, going through preliminary phases during which the injury is as yet but a speculative possibility, too remote or hypothetical to warrant present submission to a federal court.").

As used here, “ripeness” refers to “ ‘an aspect of the doctrine of justiciability, specifically, the requirement that there be an actual, as opposed to a hypothetical, injury to the individual invoking the judicial power.’ ” Beck v. City of Portland, 202 Or. App. 360, 366, 122 P.3d 131 (2005) (quoting Coast Range Conifers v. Board of Forestry, 192 Or. App. 126, 129, 83 P.3d 966 (2004), rev'd on other grounds, 339 Or. 136, 117 P.3d 990 (2005)). In U.S. West Communications v. City of Eugene, 336 Or. 181, 81 P.3d 702 (2003), the court concluded that ORS 28.020—which allows a person to seek a declaratory judgment if the person’s rights “are affected by a constitution, statute, municipal charter, ordinance, contract or franchise”—codifies a ripeness requirement for justiciability of a declaratory relief action. 336 Or. at 191, 81 P.3d 702; see also Beck, 202 Or. App. at 366, 122 P.3d 131 (describing U.S. West Communications as “appl[ying] the test for ripeness set out in [two prior cases] in which the court explicitly identified ripeness as a jurisdictional issue”). “[T]he legislature’s use of the present tense phrase ‘are affected’ implies [that] the controversy must involve a dispute based on present facts rather than on contingent or hypothetical events.” U.S. West Communications, 336 Or. at 191, 81 P.3d 702. Similarly, ORS 34.040 requires that “a substantial interest of a plaintiff has been injured” (emphasis added), thereby codifying a ripeness requirement for justiciability of a writ of review proceeding.

" Beck v. City of Portland , 202 Or. App. 360, 366, 122 P.3d 131 (2005) (quoting Coast Range Conifers v. Board of Forestry , 192 Or. App. 126, 129, 83 P.3d 966 (2004), rev'd on other grounds , 339 Or. 136, 117 P.3d 990 (2005) ). In U.S. West Communications v. City of Eugene , 336 Or. 181, 81 P.3d 702 (2003), the court concluded that ORS 28.020 -which allows a person to seek a declaratory judgment if the person's rights "are affected by a constitution, statute, municipal charter, ordinance, contract or franchise"-codifies a ripeness requirement for justiciability of a declaratory relief action. 336 Or. at 191, 81 P.3d 702 ; see also Beck , 202 Or. App. at 366, 122 P.3d 131 (describing U.S. West Communications as "appl[ying] the test for ripeness set out in [two prior cases] in which the court explicitly identified ripeness as a jurisdictional issue"). "[T]he legislature's use of the present tense phrase 'are affected' implies [that] the controversy must involve a dispute based on present facts rather than on contingent or hypothetical events." U.S. West Communications , 336 Or. at 191, 81 P.3d 702. Similarly, ORS 34.040 requires that "a substantial interest of a plaintiff has been injured " (emphasis added), thereby codifying a ripeness requirement for justiciability of a writ of review proceeding.

The justiciability doctrine of ripeness "requires a court to answer the question of 'whether the dispute has matured to the point that it warrants a judicial decision.' " Id. (quoting B & B Enters. of Wilson Cnty. v. City of Lebanon, 318 S.W.3d 839, 848 (Tenn. 2010) ). Thus, "ripeness is peculiarly a question of timing." Id. (quoting Reg'l Rail Reorganization Act Cases, 419 U.S. 102, 140, 95 S.Ct. 335, 42 L.Ed.2d 320 (1974) ). As we explained in West, the basic rationale of the ripeness doctrine "is to prevent the courts, through avoidance of premature adjudication, from entangling themselves in abstract disagreements...." Id. at 490-91 (quoting Abbott Labs. v. Gardner, 387 U.S. 136, 148, 87 S.Ct. 1507, 18 L.Ed.2d 681 (1967) ).

The ripeness doctrine exists "to prevent the courts, through premature adjudication, from entangling themselves in abstract disagreements." Thomas v. Union Carbide Agric. Prods. Co., 473 U.S. 568, 580, 105 S.Ct. 3325, 87 L.Ed.2d 409 (1985) (quoting Abbott Labs. v. Gardner, 387 U.S. 136, 148, 87 S.Ct. 1507, 18 L.Ed.2d 681 (1967)). Application of this doctrine "requires that the court exercise its discretion to determine if judicial resolution would be desirable under all of the circumstances." Brown, 763 F.2d at 801. Of primary importance is "whether the issues tendered are appropriate for judicial resolution," and, if so, the degree of "hardship to the parties if judicial relief is denied" before the claim is allowed to ripen further. Young v. Klutznick, 652 F.2d 617, 625 (6th Cir. 1981) (quoting Toilet Goods Ass'n v. Gardner, 387 U.S. 158, 162, 87 S.Ct. 1520, 18 L.Ed.2d 697 (1967)).

label: qa_CRIM. LAW_mbe_565
subject: CRIM. LAW
question: Lipsky, a college student, purchased narcotics from Speed, whom he believed to be a "street person" but who was in fact an undercover police agent. Lipsky has been charged as an accomplice to the sale of narcotics. He should be
final_answer:
To answer this question, we need to consider the principles of accomplice liability in criminal law. An accomplice is someone who assists or encourages another person to commit a crime. However, for someone to be held liable as an accomplice, they must have the requisite intent to aid in the commission of the crime.

In this scenario, Lipsky purchased narcotics from Speed, who was an undercover police agent. The key issue here is whether Lipsky had the intent to aid in the sale of narcotics, knowing that Speed was engaged in an illegal transaction. Since Speed was an undercover agent, he was not actually selling narcotics illegally but was rather conducting a sting operation. Lipsky, believing Speed to be a "street person," intended to buy narcotics, not to aid in their sale.

Given these facts, we can analyze the options:

(A) Convicted on the authority of Case A: Without knowing the specifics of Case A, we cannot determine if it applies to Lipsky's situation. Generally, to be convicted as an accomplice, one must have intended to facilitate the crime.

(B) Convicted on the authority of Case B: Similarly, without the specifics of Case B, it's hard to say if this applies. However, if Case B involves scenarios where the defendant's actions inadvertently aid in a crime without the requisite intent, it might not apply here since Lipsky's intent was to purchase, not to aid in the sale.

(C) Acquitted on the authority of Case C: If Case C involves principles that one cannot be an accomplice to a crime when the other party is not actually committing a crime (as in the case of an undercover operation where no real crime is being committed by the officer), this could potentially apply. Lipsky's actions were aimed at purchasing narcotics, not at aiding Speed, who was not actually selling narcotics illegally.

(D) Acquitted on the authority of Case D: Without specifics, it's difficult to assess. However, if Case D involves scenarios where the defendant lacks the requisite intent to be considered an accomplice, this might apply, as Lipsky's intent was to buy, not to aid in the sale.

Given the information provided and general principles of accomplice liability, Lipsky should be acquitted because he did not have the intent to aid in the sale of narcotics; he intended to purchase them. The fact that Speed was an undercover agent means that no actual sale of narcotics (in the context of illegal activity) was taking place. Thus, Lipsky cannot be considered an accomplice to a non-existent crime in the context of Speed's actions.

Answer: (C)

choices.C: acquitted on the authority of Case C.

evidence_store.text: A nearly identical challenge to the application of accomplice liability was rejected by our supreme court in State v. Briggs , 2008 UT 75, 197 P.3d 628. The court in Briggs held that the State was not required to prove constructive possession before the defendant could be convicted as an accomplice to the crime of possession. Id. ¶ 20 ("If we were to hold that it is necessary for an accomplice to be in actual possession of the contraband before accomplice liability could be established, the State could charge someone as an accomplice only when he or she had acted as a principal. Accomplice liability would cease to have any independent function.").

To prove accomplice liability, the State was required to submit sufficient evidence that: “(1) the accomplice had the purpose to make the crime succeed; (2) the accomplice's acts solicited, aided or attempted to aid another in committing the offense; and (3) ... the accomplice shared the requisite mental state for the offense.” State v. Duran, 158 N.H. 146, 151, 960 A.2d 697 (2008).

"An active participant in a drug transaction has the intent needed to aid and abet a § 924(c) violation when he knows that one of his confederates will carry a gun. In such a case, the accomplice has decided to join in the criminal venture, and share in its benefits, with full awareness of its scope-that the plan calls not just for a drug sale, but for an armed one." Id.

The crux of a conspiracy is an agreement between two or more persons to join together to accomplish something illegal. United States v. Parker , 554 F.3d 230, 234 (2d Cir. 2009) ("To prove a conspiracy, the evidence must show that 'two or more persons agreed to participate in a joint venture intended to commit an unlawful act.' " (quoting United States v. Desimone , 119 F.3d 217, 223 (2d Cir. 1997) )). We have recognized a "narrow exception" to the conspiracy rule for a transaction between a buyer and seller of drugs. Id. Under this exception, "the existence of a buyer-seller relationship does not itself establish a conspiracy; however, where there is additional evidence showing an agreement to join together to accomplish an objective beyond the sale transaction, the evidence may support a finding that the parties intentionally participated in a conspiracy." United States v. Hawkins , 547 F.3d 66, 72 (2d Cir. 2008) ; see also United States v. Rojas , 617 F.3d 669, 674 (2d Cir. 2010) ("[T]he exception does not protect either the seller or buyer from a charge that they conspired together to transfer drugs if the evidence supports a finding that they shared a conspiratorial purpose to advance other transfers, whether by the seller or by the buyer." (alteration and internal quotation marks omitted)). The question thus becomes "whether the evidence in its totality suffices to permit a jury to find beyond a reasonable doubt that the defendant was not merely a buyer or seller of narcotics, but rather that the defendant knowingly and intentionally participated in the narcotics-distribution conspiracy by agreeing to accomplish its illegal objective beyond the mere purchase or sale." Hawkins , 547 F.3d at 73-74.

According to Daniels, the Pennsylvania Superior Court cites Moss as an example of the breadth of what constitutes a substantial step for attempted delivery in Pennsylvania. See Commonwealth v. Rivers, No. 1004 EDA 2013, 2014 WL 10936727, at *2-*5, *16-*17 (Pa. Super. Ct. May 30, 2014) (non-precedential decision). In Rivers, the evidence clearly established that the defendant went beyond mere preparation (e.g., the defendant on his own initiative gave his telephone number to the undercover officer so that she could contact him to purchase illicit substances, they engaged in a telephone discussion to arrange a transaction, setting the price as well as the time and location, the defendant was proceeding toward and was near the site for the transaction when he was arrested, and he had a bag containing counterfeit cocaine). See, e.g., id. at *17. Daniels also claims that "it now appears that a person who solicits another to provide drugs-as by offering to pay a dealer-may be subject to conviction of delivery in violation of subsection (a)(30) as an accomplice." (Daniels's October 1, 2018 Letter at 3 (citing Commonwealth v. Murphy, 577 Pa. 275, 844 A.2d 1228, 1234 (2004) ).) "In federal law, by contrast, solicitation will not support a drug trafficking conviction under 21 U.S.C. §§ 841(a) and 846. See United States v. Rivera-Sanchez, 247 F.3d 905, 908-09 (9th Cir. 2001)." (Id. ) The Murphy court determined that the jury could have found that the defendant intended to aid in the transfer of drugs by the seller to the undercover officer "based on the evidence that Murphy called out to Rivas after the trooper approached him, confirmed to Rivas that the trooper was not a police officer, stayed with the trooper while Rivas got drugs, and requested compensation from the trooper for his efforts." Murphy, 844 A.2d at 1237. The state supreme court further found that the evidence showed that Murphy actually aided Rivas in transferring the drugs to the trooper by screening the trooper for the seller. Id. Finally, we expressly rejected the Ninth Circuit's understanding of federal attempt law in Martinez. 906 F.3d at 286-87.

label: qa_EVIDENCE_mbe_1115
subject: EVIDENCE
question: At Devlin's trial for burglary, Jaron supported Devlin's alibi that they were fishing together at the time of the crime. On cross-examination, Jaron was asked whether his statement on a credit card application that he had worked for his present employer for the last five years was false. Jaron denied that the statement was false. The prosecutor then calls Wilcox, the manager of the company for which Jaron works, to testify that although Jaron had been first employed five years earlier and is now
final_answer:
To determine the admissibility of Wilcox's testimony, we need to analyze the situation step by step, focusing on the rules regarding the impeachment of a witness's credibility and the use of extrinsic evidence to prove prior inconsistent statements.

1. **Impeachment by Prior Inconsistent Statements**: A witness's credibility can be impeached by showing that they have made statements inconsistent with their current testimony. This can be done directly by questioning the witness about the prior statement or, if the witness denies or cannot recall making the statement, through extrinsic evidence.

2. **Foundation for Extrinsic Evidence**: For extrinsic evidence of a prior inconsistent statement to be admissible, a foundation must be laid. This means the witness must be given an opportunity to explain or deny the prior statement, and the opposing party must have a chance to examine the witness about it, unless the interests of justice require otherwise.

3. **Jaron's Statement and Denial**: Jaron was asked about a statement on a credit card application regarding his employment history. He denied that the statement was false. This denial is crucial because it sets the stage for potentially introducing extrinsic evidence to prove the statement was indeed false, thereby impeaching Jaron's credibility.

4. **Admissibility of Wilcox's Testimony**: Wilcox's testimony is offered to prove that Jaron's statement about his employment history was false. Since Jaron denied the falsity of the statement, the prosecutor can introduce extrinsic evidence (Wilcox's testimony) to show that Jaron's statement was indeed false. This is a direct attack on Jaron's credibility, which is relevant to the case because Jaron's testimony supports Devlin's alibi.

5. **Relevance and Prejudice**: The admissibility of evidence to impeach a witness's credibility is generally subject to the judge's discretion, balancing the probative value of the evidence against its potential for prejudice. However, in this case, the issue is not about balancing but about the right to introduce evidence to contradict a witness's denial of a prior inconsistent statement.

Given these considerations, the correct answer is that Wilcox's testimony is admissible as a matter of right because Jaron denied the prior inconsistent statement on cross-examination, thereby "opening the door" for extrinsic evidence to prove the statement was false.

Answer: (B)

choices.A: admissible, in the judge's discretion, because Jaron's credibility is a fact of major consequence to the case.
choices.B: admissible, as a matter of right, because Jaron "opened the door" by his denial on cross-examination.
choices.C: inadmissible, because whether Jaron lied in his application is a matter that cannot be proved by extrinsic evidence.
choices.D: inadmissible, because the misstatement by Jaron could have been caused by misunderstanding of the application form.

snap_answer:
To determine the admissibility of Wilcox's testimony, we need to analyze the situation step by step:

1. **Jaron's Statement on Cross-Examination**: Jaron denied that his statement on the credit card application about his employment history was false. This denial makes his credibility a significant issue because it directly pertains to his honesty.

2. **Purpose of Wilcox's Testimony**: Wilcox is called to testify to contradict Jaron's statement about his employment history, which Jaron denied was false. The purpose here is to impeach Jaron's credibility by showing that he lied about his employment duration.

3. **Impeachment by Contradiction**: Generally, a witness's credibility can be impeached by showing that they have made prior inconsistent statements. However, when a witness denies making a prior statement, the denial can be contradicted by extrinsic evidence if the statement is relevant to the witness's credibility and the witness has been given an opportunity to explain or deny the statement.

4. **Specific Rule on Impeachment**: According to the Federal Rules of Evidence (and similar rules in many jurisdictions), if a witness denies making a statement on cross-examination, the statement can be proved by extrinsic evidence for the purpose of impeaching the witness's credibility.

Given these considerations:

- **Option (A)** suggests that the testimony is admissible in the judge's discretion because Jaron's credibility is a major issue. While credibility is crucial, the admissibility in this context is more about the right to contradict a denial than discretion.
  
- **Option (B)** states that the testimony is admissible as a matter of right because Jaron "opened the door" by denying the falsity of his statement on cross-examination. This aligns with the principle that a denial of a prior statement can be contradicted by extrinsic evidence.

- **Option (C)** claims that whether Jaron lied is inadmissible because it cannot be proved by extrinsic evidence. This is incorrect because extrinsic evidence can be used to contradict a witness's denial of a prior statement for impeachment purposes.

- **Option (D)** suggests that the testimony is inadmissible because Jaron's misstatement could have been due to a misunderstanding. While this might be a potential explanation, it does not preclude the admissibility of evidence to contradict Jaron's denial of making the false statement.

Based on the analysis, the correct answer is:

Answer: (B)

Specifically, Rule 608(b) of the Federal Rules of Evidence allows for the admission of extrinsic evidence to contradict a witness's denial of a prior statement for the purpose of impeaching the witness's credibility. This rule applies when a witness denies making a prior statement on cross-examination, as in Jaron's case, thereby "opening the door" to the admission of extrinsic evidence to contradict the denial. 

Answer: (B)

evidence_store.text: Rule 613(b) provides: "Extrinsic evidence of a witness's prior inconsistent statement is admissible only if the witness is given an opportunity to explain or deny the statement and an adverse party is given the opportunity to examine the witness about it, or if justice so requires."

For the purpose of impeaching the credibility of a witness, a party may show that the witness has, on another occasion, made statements that are inconsistent with some material part of his present testimony. This may be done by first questioning the witness as to the prior inconsistent statement that he has made. If the witness denies having made the statement or fails to remember it, the making of the statement may be proved by extrinsic evidence. A proper foundation must be laid by giving the witness an opportunity to explain or deny the statement, and it must be relevant to some issue in the case. Under Rule 803(3), a statement of a declarant's then-existing state of mind is admissible as a basis for a circumstantial inference that the declarant acted in accordance with his state of mind. [See also Mutual Life Insurance Co. v. Hillmon] The Federal Rules provide that the credibility of a witness may be attacked by any party, including the party calling him. [Fed. R. Evid. 607]

Federal Rule of Evidence 806 precedes and is the prototype for N.J.R.E. 806, upon which the trial judge properly relied. Ibid. Under Federal Rule of Evidence 806, federal courts have allowed the admission of prior convictions to impeach the credibility of a hearsay declarant. In United States v. Greenidge, the Third Circuit held the trial court appropriately admitted the non-testifying defendant's prior convictions to impeach his credibility after he offered his own out-of-court statements as evidence. 495 F.3d 85, 97-98 (3d Cir. 2007). The Third Circuit also ruled the district court had appropriately weighed the probative value of the prior convictions against the prejudicial effect, as required by Federal Rule of Evidence 609. Id. at 97-98.

Extrinsic evidence of a prior inconsistent statement by a witness is inadmissible unless the witness is first afforded an opportunity to explain or deny the prior statement and the opposing party is afforded an opportunity to interrogate the witness on it, or the interests of justice otherwise require. If a witness denies making or does not distinctly admit making the prior inconsistent statement, extrinsic evidence of such statement is admissible.

Under OCGA § 24-6-613 (b), extrinsic evidence of a witness' prior inconsistent statement may be admitted so long as the witness is first afforded an opportunity to explain or deny the prior inconsistent statement and the opposite party is afforded an opportunity to interrogate the witness on the prior inconsistent statement or the interests of justice otherwise require.

label: qa_TORTS_mbe_1154
subject: TORTS
question: Traveler was a passenger on a commercial aircraft owned and operated by Airline. The aircraft crashed into a mountain, killing everyone on board. The flying weather was good. Traveler's legal representative brought a wrongful death action against Airline. At trial, the legal representative offered no expert or other testimony as to the cause of the crash. On Airline's motion to dismiss at the conclusion of the legal representative's case, the court should
final_answer:
To answer this question, we must consider the doctrine of res ipsa loquitur and its application to the given scenario. Res ipsa loquitur is a doctrine that allows a plaintiff to establish a prima facie case of negligence when the plaintiff cannot prove a specific act of negligence, but the accident is of a type that would not normally occur unless someone was negligent.

In this case, the aircraft crash is the type of accident that would not normally occur unless someone (in this case, likely the airline or its personnel) was negligent. The fact that the flying weather was good suggests that external factors such as weather are less likely to be the cause of the crash, which further supports the inference of negligence.

The key elements required for the application of res ipsa loquitur, as outlined in the provided sources, are:
1. The accident is of a type that would not normally occur unless someone was negligent.
2. The negligence was attributable to the defendant.
3. The injury was not attributable to the plaintiff.

Given that the aircraft was under the control of Airline and its personnel, it can be inferred that any negligence leading to the crash would be attributable to Airline. Since Traveler was a passenger, it is reasonable to assume that the injury (or in this case, the death) was not attributable to Traveler.

Therefore, even though the legal representative offered no expert or other testimony as to the cause of the crash, the doctrine of res ipsa loquitur allows the jury to infer that the crash was due to Airline's negligence, given the nature of the accident and the circumstances.

The best answer is C.

choices.A: grant the motion, because the legal representative has offered no evidence as to the cause of the crash.
choices.B: grant the motion, because the legal representative has failed to offer evidence negating the possibility that the crash may have been caused by mechanical failure that Airline could not have prevented.
choices.C: deny the motion, because the jury may infer that the aircraft crashed due to Airline's negligence.
choices.D: deny the motion, because in the circumstances common carriers are strictly liable.

gold_passage: In order to prevail on a claim for wrongful death, the plaintiff must prove: “(1) a wrongful act, neglect, or default of the defendant that proximately caused the death and that would have entitled the decedent to maintain an action and recover damages if death had not ensued; (2) the decedent was survived by a spouse, children, parents, or other next of kin; and (3) the survivors suffered damages by reasons of the wrongful death.” Cline v. Stein, 9th Dist. Wayne No. 13CA0052, 2015-Ohio-2979, 20
snap_answer:
In a wrongful death action, particularly one involving a commercial aircraft, the plaintiff (in this case, Traveler's legal representative) must establish that the defendant (Airline) breached a duty of care owed to the plaintiff, and that this breach caused the harm or death. The doctrine of res ipsa loquitur (the thing speaks for itself) can sometimes be applied in negligence cases, suggesting that the injury would not have occurred in the absence of negligence. However, this doctrine typically requires that the instrumentality causing the injury be within the exclusive control of the defendant and that the plaintiff show that they were not responsible for the injury.

Given that the flying weather was good and there's no direct evidence presented as to the cause of the crash, one might argue that the legal representative has not met the burden of proof to establish negligence on the part of Airline. However, the key issue here is whether the legal representative has presented enough evidence to allow the case to proceed to the jury.

Option (A) suggests granting the motion to dismiss because no evidence was offered as to the cause of the crash. This seems reasonable because, without any evidence of negligence, it's challenging for the plaintiff to meet their burden of proof.

Option (B) implies that the legal representative must also negate the possibility of causes that Airline could not have prevented, which is not necessarily the legal representative's burden at this stage.

Option (C) suggests denying the motion because the jury may infer negligence from the circumstances. This aligns with the principle that a jury can infer negligence from the fact that an accident occurred, especially in situations where the defendant had exclusive control over the instrumentality that caused the harm.

Option (D) mentions strict liability, which is not typically applied in this context. Strict liability usually applies in specific situations, such as ultra-hazardous activities, and not generally to common carriers for accidents.

Considering these points, the most appropriate response is to deny the motion to dismiss because the jury may infer that the aircraft crashed due to Airline's negligence, given that the accident itself can sometimes imply negligence, especially when the defendant is in exclusive control of the instrumentality that caused the harm.

Answer: (C)
hyde_passage:
According to the doctrine of res ipsa loquitur, a plaintiff may establish a prima facie case of negligence by showing that an accident would not have occurred in the absence of negligence and that the instrumentality causing the injury was within the exclusive control of the defendant. In cases involving commercial carriers, the courts have held that the jury may infer negligence from the circumstances of the accident, even in the absence of direct evidence of the cause of the accident. The plaintiff is not required to negate all possible causes of the accident that were beyond the defendant's control, but rather to show that the accident is more likely than not to have been caused by the defendant's negligence.

Answer: (C)

evidence_store.text: While breach of duty is ordinarily a question for the trier of fact, a plaintiff's failure to offer any evidence on that element of the prima facie case will permit a directed verdict for the defendant. Under certain circumstances, the fact that a particular injury occurred may itself establish or tend to establish a breach of duty owed, permitting the trier of fact to infer the defendant's liability. This is the doctrine of res ipsa loquitur ("the thing speaks for itself"). However, for the doctrine to apply, the plaintiff must show that (i) the accident causing his injury is the type that would not normally occur unless someone was negligent; (ii) the negligence was attributable to the defendant; and (iii) the injury was not attributable to the plaintiff. The second requirement can often be satisfied by showing that the instrumentality causing the injury was in the exclusive control of the defendant.

¶ 15 "[T]he res ipsa loquitur doctrine allows the plaintiff to establish a prima facie case of negligence when he cannot prove a specific act of negligence .... Once the plaintiff establishes a prima facie case, the defendant must then offer an explanation, if he can." Pacheco , 149 Wash.2d at 441, 69 P.3d 324. Res ipsa loquitur is inapplicable only where the defendant's evidence completely explains the plaintiff's injury. Pacheco , 149 Wash.2d at 440, 69 P.3d 324. "Thus, the plaintiff may be entitled to rely on the ... doctrine even if the defendant's testimony, if believed by the jury, would explain how the event causing injury to the plaintiff occurred." Pacheco , 149 Wash.2d at 440, 69 P.3d 324. Fun-Tastic does not argue that it presented evidence explaining Brugh's injury. Instead, it contends that Brugh has not established her prima facie case.

This set of facts raises the doctrine of res ipsa loquitur, which allows the trier of fact to infer negligence simply from the fact that a particular injury occurred. Res ipsa loquitur requires the plaintiff to establish that: the event causing the injury is of a type that would not normally occur unless someone was negligent; the negligence was attributable to the defendant; and plaintiff was not at fault regarding his injury.

The doctrine of res ipsa loquitur “permits a trier of fact to draw an inference of negligence in the absence of a finding of a specific cause of the occurrence when an accident is of the kind that does not ordinarily happen unless the defendant was negligent in some respect and other responsible causes including conduct of the plaintiff are sufficiently eliminated by the evidence.” Enrich v. Windmere Corp., 416 Mass. 83, 88, 616 N.E.2d 1081 (1993); see also Coyne v. John S. Tilley Co., 368 Mass. 230, 235, 331 N.E.2d 541 (1975); Evangelio v. Metropolitan Bottling Co., 339 Mass. 177, 181, 158 N.E.2d 342 (1959). A plaintiff, however, “need not exclude all possible causes;” rather, “[she] must show that there is a greater probability than not that the accident resulted from the defendant's negligence.” Enrich, 416 Mass. at 87, 616 N.E.2d 1081; Coyne, 368 Mass. at 237, 331 N.E.2d 541 (“The evidence introduced must be of such a nature that it warrants a reasonable inference that the particular defendant-manufacturer, rather than another, bears responsibility for the negligence which, one may infer, caused the accident.”).

The doctrine of res ipsa loquitur allows an inference of negligence where it can be established that an event would not ordinarily occur absent negligence, and may establish whether a medical professional is responsible for causing an injury. Toogood v. Rogal , 573 Pa. 245, 824 A.2d 1140, 1148-49 (2003) (plurality).

question: Shore decided to destroy his dilapidated building in order to collect the insurance money. He hired Parsons to bum down the building. Parsons broke into the building and carefully searched it to make sure no one was inside. He failed, however, to see a vagrant asleep in an office closet. He started a fire. The building was destroyed, and the vagrant died from burns a week later. Two days after the fire, Shore filed an insurance claim in which he stated that he had no information about the cause
final_answer:
To determine the correct answer, let's analyze the scenario step by step:

1. **Shore's Intent and Action**: Shore decided to destroy his building to collect insurance money. This indicates his primary motive was financial gain through fraudulent means, as he intended to deceive the insurance company about the cause of the fire.

2. **Parsons' Role**: Parsons was hired by Shore to burn down the building. Parsons' actions were aimed at destroying the property, which is arson. However, Parsons' failure to ensure no one was inside led to the vagrant's death.

3. **Felony-Murder Rule**: The felony-murder rule generally states that if a person dies during the commission of a felony, the person or persons committing the felony can be charged with murder, even if the death was unintentional. The key is that the death must occur in connection with (or "in the furtherance of") a felony.

4. **Analyzing the Options**:
   - **(A) Arson**: This is the act of intentionally setting fire to property. Parsons committed arson when he started the fire, and Shore is also liable for arson because he hired Parsons to do so. The vagrant's death occurred as a direct result of the arson.
   - **(B) Fraud**: While Shore's intent to file a false insurance claim is fraudulent, the death of the vagrant is more directly related to the arson than to the act of filing a false claim.
   - **(C) Conspiracy**: Shore and Parsons conspired to commit arson. However, the conspiracy itself is not the direct cause of the vagrant's death; it's the act of arson that led to the death.
   - **(D) Burglary**: Parsons broke into the building, which could be considered burglary if the intent was to commit a crime inside (in this case, arson). However, the question focuses on the felony directly connected to the vagrant's death, which is more closely related to the arson than the act of breaking in.

Given the analysis, the most direct connection to the vagrant's death is the act of arson, as it was the immediate cause of the death. Therefore, if Shore is guilty of felony-murder, it is because the vagrant's death occurred in connection with the felony of arson.

Answer: (A)

choices.C: conspiracy.
choices.D: burglary.

evidence_store.text: The trial court did, however, properly define felony murder "as follows: A person also commits the crime of murder when in the commission of a felony, that person causes the death of another human being with or without malice." (Emphasis supplied.) See OCGA § 16-5-1 (c). And the court later gave the pattern jury instruction that explains the requisite legal relationship between the felony and the death as follows:

Our review of the record confirms that the Commonwealth presented sufficient evidence for the jury to convict Appellant of three counts of attempted homicide. Although Appellant is correct in his assertion that arson is a specifically enumerated crime for purposes of felony murder , he is incorrect that the basis for an attempted homicide conviction cannot be arson. While malice or intent to commit the underlying felony are the requisite mens rea requirements for second-degree murder, if it is established that the perpetrator of an arson holds the specific intent to kill, depending on the results, he may be properly convicted of either attempted homicide, or first-degree murder. Commonwealth v. Pierce , 567 Pa. 186, 786 A.2d 203, 208-210 (2001) (setting intentional fire to an occupied residence resulting in three deaths as the basis of defendant's three first-degree murder convictions).

Under the felony-murder rule, a strict causal or temporal relationship between the felony and the murder is not required; what is required is proof beyond a reasonable doubt that the felony and murder were part of one continuous transaction. (People v. Cavitt (2004) 33 Cal.4th 187, 207, 14 Cal.Rptr.3d 281, 91 P.3d 222.)

a. Inherently dangerous felony. In her instructions to the jury on the felony-murder in the second degree charge, the trial judge indicated that the third element that the Commonwealth had to prove was that the underlying felony was inherently dangerous or that the defendant acted with a conscious disregard for the risk to human life. Over the defendant's objection, the judge further instructed that, as a matter of law, arson is inherently dangerous to human life. The defendant argues that this was error and that the jury should have decided whether, in this case, she acted with a conscious disregard for the risk to human life.

Arson of a dwelling place, especially when committed by an adult, is, as this case confirms, "a heinous, life-threatening crime." DeCicco, 44 Mass. App. Ct. at 127, 688 N.E.2d 1010. In some other jurisdictions, causing another person's death in the course of committing arson is punished more harshly than in Massachusetts. The sentence for the more serious offense of murder in the first degree in Massachusetts, meanwhile, is life imprisonment with no eligibility for parole. G. L. c. 265, § 2 (a ). Thus, a life sentence, with parole eligibility after fifteen years, for causing death in the course of the commission of arson, does not shock the conscience or offend fundamental notions of human dignity.

question: Assume for this question only that BCD's termination of John's employment was not wrongful. If John sues BCD for the reasonable value of his services, which of the following is the most likely result?
final_answer:
To determine the most likely result if John sues BCD for the reasonable value of his services, let's analyze each option step by step:

(A) John will win, because BCD benefited as a result of John's services.
- This option suggests that because BCD benefited from John's services, John should be entitled to the reasonable value of those services. However, the mere fact that BCD benefited is not sufficient to establish a legal obligation to pay for those services, especially if there was no agreement or understanding that John would be compensated for his work.

(B) John will win, because BCD made an implied-in-fact promise to pay a reasonable commission for services that result in sales.
- An implied-in-fact contract is based on the circumstances and the conduct of the parties, suggesting that they had an understanding or agreement, even if it was not explicitly stated. If John can demonstrate that BCD's actions or the circumstances implied a promise to pay for his services, this could be a viable argument. However, the question does not provide specific details about such an implied agreement.

(C) John will lose, because there is an express contractual provision pre-empting the subject of compensation for his services.
- If there is an express contractual provision regarding compensation, this would typically take precedence over any implied agreements or claims for the reasonable value of services. The question does not specify the details of John's employment contract with BCD, but if such a provision exists, it could indeed pre-empt John's claim.

(D) John will lose, because he cannot perform his agreement to assist the customer for six months.
- This option focuses on John's ability to perform his contractual obligations rather than the issue of compensation for his services. While this might be relevant in certain contexts, it does not directly address the question of whether John can recover the reasonable value of his services from BCD.

Given the information provided and focusing on the question of suing for the reasonable value of his services, option (C) seems the most relevant. It directly addresses the issue of compensation and suggests that if there is an express contractual provision regarding compensation, John's claim for the reasonable value of his services would likely fail.

Answer: (C)

evidence_store.text: "[A] party who has contributed services to a relationship may attempt to recover for the value of the services under one of three theories-express contract, implied-in-fact contract, or a quasi-contract.

"The general rule of unjust enrichment is that where services are rendered and expenditures made by one party to or for the benefit of another, without an express contract to pay, the law will imply a promise to pay a fair compensation therefor." Krawiec v. Manly, 370 N.C. 602, 615, 811 S.E.2d 542 (2018). "In order to establish a claim for unjust enrichment, a party must have conferred a benefit on the other party, and the benefit must not be gratuitous and it must be measurable." Id. (quotations omitted). "A claim of this type is neither in tort nor contract but is described as a claim in quasi contract or a contract implied in law." Booe v. Shadrick, 322 N.C. 567, 570, 369 S.E.2d 554 (1988). Accordingly, "[i]f there is a contract between the parties the contract governs the claim and the law will not imply a contract." Id.

¶22 "Because a claim should be dismissed only if it appears to a certainty that the plaintiff would be entitled to no relief under any state of facts which could be proved in support of the claim, a district court should not dismiss alternative equitable claims if the existence or applicability of a contract remain in dispute." Northgate Village Dev., LC v. Orem City , 2014 UT App 86, ¶ 50, 325 P.3d 123 (quotation simplified). The fact that the parties acknowledged and agreed that a contract existed between them does not necessarily lead to the conclusion that it was an enforceable contract that effectively governed their rights and obligations with respect to the subject matter of the case. See Selvig v. Blockbuster Enters., LC , 2011 UT 39, ¶ 30, 266 P.3d 691 ("Where an express contract covering the subject matter of the litigation exists, recovery for unjust enrichment is not available." (quotation simplified)); Ashby v. Ashby , 2010 UT 7, ¶ 14, 227 P.3d 246 ("[A] prerequisite for recovery on an unjust enrichment theory is the absence of an enforceable contract governing the rights and obligations of the parties relating to the conduct at issue.").

"The general rule of unjust enrichment is that where services are rendered and expenditures made by one party to or for the benefit of another, without an express contract to pay, the law will imply a promise to pay a fair compensation therefor." Krawiec v. Manly , 370 N.C. 602, 615, 811 S.E.2d 542, 551 (2018) (citation omitted). However, where "a contract exists between the parties, the law will not imply a contract." Se. Shelter Corp. v. Btu, Inc. , 154 N.C. App. 321, 331, 572 S.E.2d 200, 207 (2002). Because Plaintiff and Defendant are contractual counterparties, the trial court did not err in determining that Plaintiff did not show a right to relief on his unjust enrichment cause of action.
See R.J. Wildner Contracting Co. v. Ohio Tpk. Comm'n , 913 F.Supp. 1031, 1043 (N.D. Ohio 1996) (recovery for unjust enrichment prohibited where express contract covers same subject).

question: At Defendant's trial on the charge of manslaughter in the death of the pedestrian, his best argument would be that
final_answer:
To approach this question, let's break down the key elements involved in the scenario and the options provided:

1. **Charge of Manslaughter**: Manslaughter is a criminal charge that involves the unlawful killing of another human being without malice aforethought. It can be voluntary (with intent to kill or cause harm) or involuntary (without intent to kill but resulting from reckless or negligent behavior).

2. **Voluntary Intoxication Defense**: Generally, voluntary intoxication is not a defense to crimes, especially those involving recklessness or negligence. However, it can be considered in specific intent crimes to determine if the defendant had the requisite mental state.

3. **Analysis of Options**:
   - **(A)** This option suggests that the defendant was too intoxicated to realize he was creating a substantial and unjustifiable risk. This aligns with the concept of involuntary manslaughter, which can result from reckless or negligent behavior. If the defendant can argue that his intoxication level was so high that he couldn't appreciate the risk, it might mitigate his culpability.
   - **(B)** This option implies that the defendant's actions were not voluntary due to his intoxication level. However, for this to be a viable argument, the defendant would need to demonstrate a level of intoxication that rendered him incapable of forming any intent or understanding of his actions, which is a high bar.
   - **(C)** Contributory negligence is a civil law concept that reduces the damages a plaintiff can recover if their own negligence contributed to their injury. It's not directly applicable to criminal charges like manslaughter.
   - **(D)** This option suggests that the defendant was too intoxicated to form any intent to operate the automobile voluntarily. While this touches on the issue of intent, it doesn't directly address the charge of manslaughter, which may not require specific intent.

Given the context of manslaughter and the principles of voluntary intoxication as a defense, the best argument for the defendant would be to claim that his level of intoxication prevented him from realizing the risk of his actions, which could potentially reduce his culpability for the death of the pedestrian. This aligns with option **(A)**, as it addresses the defendant's ability to appreciate the risk of his behavior, which is crucial in determining recklessness or negligence in manslaughter cases.

Answer: (A)

Manslaughter can be based on reckless or grossly negligent behavior, and intoxication might actually be seen as exacerbating the recklessness rather than mitigating it.

(B) This option posits that the Defendant's actions were not voluntary because he was too intoxicated to know where he was or what he was doing. While extreme intoxication can potentially render a person incapable of forming the intent necessary for certain crimes, this defense is more typically applied to specific intent crimes. However, it's a stronger argument than (A) because it directly challenges the voluntariness of the Defendant's actions.

(C) Contributory negligence is a civil law concept that can reduce or bar recovery in a tort case if the plaintiff's own negligence contributed to their injury. In a criminal case like manslaughter, the focus is on the defendant's culpability rather than on allocating fault between the defendant and the victim. Thus, arguing that the pedestrian was contributorily negligent would not typically be a defense to a criminal charge.

(D) This option suggests that the Defendant was too intoxicated to form the intent to voluntarily operate the automobile. Similar to option (B), it implies that the Defendant's actions were not fully voluntary due to his intoxication. However, the key issue in a manslaughter charge related to driving is not the intent to drive but the reckless or negligent manner in which the driving was performed. Intoxication might be a factor in establishing recklessness, but claiming an inability to form an intent to drive due to intoxication does not directly address the elements of the crime of manslaughter.

Considering these analyses, the best argument for the Defendant, given the context of a manslaughter charge, would involve challenging the voluntariness or intent behind his actions, which is most closely related to option (B). evidence_store.text: intoxication: Intoxication is a defense available to defendants in criminal law cases. A defendant who raises this defense claims that he should not be held liable for a crime because his compromised mental state prevented him from forming the necessary mens rea.  Many states, such as California, distinguish between voluntary and involuntary intoxication and only allow the defense to be raised in cases of involuntary intoxication.   Involuntary intoxication is the unknowing ingestion of some intoxicating liquor, drug, or other substance, caused by force or fraud. Many jurisdictions recognize involuntary intoxication is a valid defense to a crime. In these jurisdictions, a defendant can admit evidence of his intoxication to show that he did not appreciate the wrongfulness of his conduct when committing the crime and should not be held liable.  Voluntary intoxication is the willing ingestion or injection of any drink, drug, or other intoxicating substance that the defendant knows can produce an intoxicating effect. The Supreme Court in Montana v. Egelhoff held that states are constitutionally permitted to eliminate the voluntary intoxication defense, and many states have done so. For example, Delaware does not permit the defendant to admit any evidence of voluntary intoxication. Other states, such as California, allow defendants to raise voluntary intoxication only in cases of specific intent crimes (as opposed to general intent crimes) and only to prove whether the defendant acted with the necessary mens rea to establish criminal liability. However, evidence of voluntary intoxication cannot be brought to negate or lessen a charge due to the inability to form the mental state of the crime charged.   The state of Intoxication, under the influence of alcohol or drugs, can also be a crime in itself in certain circumstances, such as DWI/DUI violations and public intoxication. [Last updated in June of 2020 by the Wex Definitions Team]

Voluntary intoxication will not excuse a crime, but it is a defense to a specific intent offense if the circumstances demonstrate that intoxication precluded formation of the requisite intent. The defendant has the burden of proving his intoxication defense; thereafter, it falls to the state to negate that defense by showing beyond a reasonable doubt that specific intent was present despite the defendant's alleged intoxication. Whether voluntary intoxication in a particular case is sufficient to preclude specific intent is a question to be resolved by the trier of fact.

Murder is the unlawful killing of another human being with malice aforethought. Malice aforethought exists when the defendant has (i) intent to kill, (ii) intent to inflict great bodily injury, (iii) reckless indifference to an unjustifiably high risk to human life ("abandoned and malignant heart"), or (iv) intent to commit a felony (under the felony murder doctrine). An accomplice is liable not only for the crime he intended to aid but also for any other crimes committed during commission of the intended crime if the other crimes were probable or foreseeable. Voluntary intoxication would not be a defense to a murder charge that was based on reckless indifference to an unjustifiably high risk to human life. Voluntary intoxication caused by alcohol or drugs is a defense to a crime that requires purpose (intent) or knowledge as long as the intoxication prevents the defendant from formulating the purpose or obtaining the knowledge. It is no defense to crimes involving recklessness or negligence, however. Recklessness requires a conscious disregard of the risk. For murder based on a reckless indifference to an unjustifiably high risk to human life, voluntary intoxication would not be a defense. Duress is not a defense to murder. A criminal offense may be excused if the defendant does the act unto threat of immment infliction of death or great bodily harm, as long as the defendant reasonably believes that the threat will be carried out. However, no threat will suffice as a defense to a homicide crime. The defendant may still be liable for murder if he acted with an awareness of an unjustifiably high risk to human life.
Voluntary intoxication is not a defense to the crime charged. You may not consider his voluntary intoxication in determining whether he had the required intent to commit the crime charged.
Voluntary intoxication is not a defense. A person is voluntarily intoxicated if he or she knowingly consumed a substance that he or she knew or should have known could cause intoxication.
