evidence_store.text: Hearsay is an out-of-court statement offered in evidence to prove the truth of the matter asserted in the statement. [Fed. R. Evid. 801(c)] The "dying declaration" exception to the hearsay rule [See Fed. R. Evid. 804(b)(2)] contains a prerequisite for admitting evidence that the declarant is unavailable to testify. [Fed. R. Evid. 804(a)] If the declarant were to testify that he had no memory, he would be considered unavailable. [Fed. R. Evid. 804(a)(3)] The hearsay exception for "excited utterances" [Fed. R. Evid. 803(2)], like most, does not require the declarant to be unavailable as a condition for the admission of hearsay evidence. It merely requires that the hearsay statement relate to a startling event or condition, made while the declarant was under  the stress of excitement caused by the event or condition.

Hearsay is an out-of-court statement offered in evidence to prove the truth of the matter asserted in the statement. [Fed. R. Evid. 801(c)] For a dying declaration to be admissible, two prerequisites must be met: (i) The declarant must be unavailable to testify. [Fed. R. Evid. 804(a)] The victim, being dead, obviously is unavailable to testify. (ii) A dying declaration can only be admitted in certain kinds of cases—homicide prosecutions and civil cases. [Fed. R. Evid. 804(b)(2)] This hearsay exception further requires that the declarant's statement be made while believing death to be imminent, and that the statement concern the cause or circumstances of what the declarant believed to be impending death. [Fed. R. Evid. 804(b)(2)] Rule 803(2) makes admissible a hearsay statement relating to a startling event or condition, made while the declarant was under the stress of excitement caused by the event or condition. The only kind of prejudice that can properly be balanced under the Rule 403 probative value/prejudicial impact balancing test is unfair prejudice.

The rule against hearsay does not render a declarant's statement against interest inadmissible. FED. R. EVID. 804(b)(3). Rule 804(b)(3) requires that "the declarant be unavailable, the statement must subject the declarant to criminal liability such that a reasonable person would not have made the statement unless he believed it to be true, and the statement must be corroborated by circumstances clearly indicating trustworthiness." United States v. Bell , 367 F.3d 452, 466 (5th Cir. 2004) (citing United States v. Sarmiento-Perez, 633 F.2d 1092, 1101 (5th Cir. 1981) ). The first requirement is met here because the declarant, Castle, invoked his Fifth Amendment privilege against self-incrimination and was therefore unavailable to testify. See FED. R. EVID. 804(a)(1) (a declarant is unavailable as a witness if the declarant invokes a privilege); United States v. Young Bros., Inc., 728 F.2d 682, 690 (5th Cir. 1984) ("[I]t is clear that a witness who is unavailable because he has invoked the Fifth Amendment privilege against self-incrimination is unavailable under the terms of 804(a)(1)").

Statements made under the stress of some exciting event and relating to that event are admissible as an exception to the hearsay rule. The Federal Rules require the maker of a dying declaration to be unavailable for the declaration to be admissible. Even if a statement bolsters the credibility of the plaintiff, it can still qualify as an exception to the hearsay rule and be admissible substantive evidence. It is up to the broad discretion of the trial judge under Federal Rule 403 whether to exclude relevant evidence based on needless presentation of cumulative evidence.

Rule 804. Hearsay Exceptions; Declarant Unavailable (a) Criteria for Being Unavailable. A declarant is considered to be unavailable as a witness if the declarant: (1) is exempted from testifying about the subject matter of the declarant’s statement because the court rules that a privilege applies; (2) refuses to testify about the subject matter despite a court order to do so; (3) testifies to not remembering the subject matter; (4) cannot be present or testify at the trial or hearing because of death or a then-existing infirmity, physical illness, or mental illness; or (5) is absent from the trial or hearing and the statement’s proponent has not been able, by process or other reasonable means, to procure: (A) the declarant’s attendance, in the case of a hearsay exception under Rule 804(b)(1) or (6); or (B) the declarant’s attendance or testimony, in the case of a hearsay exception under Rule 804(b)(2), (3), or (4). But this subdivision (a) does not apply if the statement’s proponent procured or wrongfully caused the declarant’s unavailability as a witness in order to prevent the declarant from attending or testifying. (b) The Exceptions. The following are not excluded by the rule against hearsay if the declarant is unavailable as a witness: (1) Former Testimony. Testimony that: (A) was given as a witness at a trial, hearing, or lawful deposition, whether given during the current proceeding or a different one; and (B) is now offered against a party who had — or, in a civil case, whose predecessor in interest had — an opportunity and similar motive to develop it by direct, cross-, or redirect examination. (2) Statement Under the Belief of Imminent Death. In a prosecution for homicide or in a civil case, a statement that the declarant, while believing the declarant’s death to be imminent, made about its cause or circumstances. (3) Statement Against Interest. A statement that: (A) a reasonable person in the declarant’s position would have made only if the person believed it to be true because, when made, it was so contrary to the declarant’s proprietary or pecuniary interest or had so great a tendency to invalidate the declarant’s claim against someone else or to expose the declarant to civil or criminal liability; and (B) is supported by corroborating circumstances that clearly indicate its trustworthiness, if it is offered in a criminal case as one that tends to expose the declarant to criminal liability. (4) Statement of Personal or Family History. A statement about: (A) the declarant’s own birth, adoption, legitimacy, ancestry, marriage, divorce, relationship by blood, adoption, or marriage, or similar facts of personal or family history, even though the declarant had no way of acquiring personal knowledge about that fact; or (B) another person concerning any of these facts, as well as death, if the declarant was related to the person by blood, adoption, or marriage or was so intimately associated with the person’s family that the declarant’s information is likely to be accurate. (5) [Other Exceptions .] [Transferred to Rule 807.] (6) Statement Offered Against a Party That Wrongfully Caused the Declarant’s Unavailability. A statement offered against a party that wrongfully caused — or acquiesced in wrongfully causing — the declarant’s unavailability as a witness, and did so intending that result.

label: qa_nan_mbe_35
subject: nan
question: Payne had purchased one of the 140 lots and brought suit against Oscar to establish that the remaining 185 lots, as well as the 140 sold previously, can be used only for residential purposes by single families. Assuming that procedural requirements have been met to permit adjudication of the issue Payne has tendered, which of the following is the most appropriate comment?
final_answer:
To answer this question, we need to analyze the situation step by step, focusing on the key elements of property law and the principles governing the use of land for specific purposes, such as residential use.

1. **Understanding the Issue**: Payne is seeking to establish that all 325 lots (the 140 already sold and the remaining 185) in the subdivision can only be used for residential purposes by single families. This implies there's a restrictive covenant or some form of agreement that limits the use of the land.

2. **Analyzing the Options**:
   - **(A)** This option suggests that the provision (likely a restrictive covenant) only binds the grantee (the buyer of the lots), not subsequent owners or the rest of the subdivision. However, restrictive covenants can indeed bind subsequent owners if they are properly recorded and noticed.
   - **(B)** This option introduces the concept of a "common development scheme," which is crucial in determining whether a subdivision is bound by certain restrictions. If a common scheme is established for the entire subdivision, it could imply that all lots are subject to the same restrictions, regardless of when they were sold.
   - **(C)** This option raises the issue of equal protection under the law, suggesting that the outcome depends on whether there are sufficient areas zoned for multiple-family uses in the municipality. While this is an important consideration in zoning and land use law, it may not directly address the issue of whether the lots in question can be used only for single-family residential purposes based on a restrictive covenant or common scheme.
   - **(D)** This option mentions the doctrine of construing deeds against the grantor, which is a principle used to resolve ambiguities in property deeds. However, this principle may not directly apply to the issue at hand, which involves determining the use of land based on restrictive covenants or a common development scheme.

3. **Conclusion**: The most relevant factor in determining whether the remaining lots can only be used for residential purposes by single families is whether there was a common development scheme established for the entire subdivision. This would imply that the restrictions apply uniformly across the subdivision, regardless of when the lots were sold.

Answer: (B)

choices.A: Oscar should win because the provision binds only the grantee.
choices.B: The outcome turns on whether a common development scheme had been established for the entire subdivision.
choices.C: The outcome turns on whether there are sufficient land areas devoted to multiple-family uses within the municipality to afford reasonable opportunity for all economic classes to move into the area so as to satisfy the standards of equal protection of the law.
choices.D: Payne should win under an application of the doctrine which requires construction of deeds to resolve any doubt against the grantor.

For example, a scheme that restricts the development of each lot to a single-family residence a

hyde_passage:
According to the principles of property law, a common development scheme is a crucial factor in determining whether restrictive covenants apply uniformly to all lots in a subdivision, regardless of when they were sold. If a common scheme is established, it can be inferred that the developer intended for the entire subdivision to be subject to the same restrictions, thereby binding all lots to the same use restrictions. The doctrine of common scheme allows for the implication of a restrictive covenant in the deeds of subsequent purchasers, provided that the common scheme is clearly established and the subsequent purchasers had notice of the restrictions.

Answer: (B)

evidence_store.text: We have acknowledged, but never expressly adopted, the common scheme of development doctrine, also known as the doctrine of implied equitable servitudes or implied restrictive covenants. See Thompson v. Pendleton, 1997 ME 127, ¶ 11 n. 2, 697 A.2d 56; 3 W Partners v. Bridges, 651 A.2d 387, 389 (Me.1994); Olson v. Albert, 523 A.2d 585, 588 (Me.1987) (per curiam); Chase v. Burrell, 474 A.2d 180, 181–82 (Me.1984). The doctrine applies when (1) a common owner subdivides property into a number of lots for sale; (2) the common owner has a “general scheme of development” for the property as a whole, in which the use of the property will be restricted; (3) the vast majority of subdivided lots contain restrictive covenants which reflect the general scheme; (4) the property against which application of an implied covenant is sought is part of the general scheme of development; and (5) the purchaser of the lot in question has notice, actual or constructive, of the restriction. Thompson, 1997 ME 127, ¶ 11 n. 2, 697 A.2d 56 (quoting Chase, 474 A.2d at 181). “The common grantor may establish a general scheme by conveying the majority of his subdivided lots subject to a restriction that reflects the general scheme.” 3 W Partners, 651 A.2d at 389. Whether a lot is part of a common scheme of development is a factual finding that we review for clear error. See Chase, 474 A.2d at 181–82. A finding is clearly erroneous only when it lacks any competent support in the record. Id.

Where a residential subdivision is laid out according to a general scheme or plan and all the lots sold or retained therein are subject to restrictive covenants, and the value of such development to a large extent rests upon the assurance given purchasers that they may rely upon the fact that the privacy of their homes will not be invaded by the encroachment of business, and that the essential residential nature of the property will not be destroyed, the courts will enforce the restrictions and will not permit them to be destroyed by slight departures from the original plan.

Our appellate courts have held that restrictions need not be imposed upon every lot in a subdivision in order to demonstrate a general scheme of development. However, a general development scheme will not be recognized where a substantial proportion of lots lack similar restrictive covenants. Compare Franklin v. Elizabeth Realty Co. , 202 N.C. 212, 217, 162 S.E. 199, 201 (1932) (holding omission of restriction from single lot in subdivision did not destroy general plan of development), with Sedberry v. Parsons , 232 N.C. 707, 711-12, 62 S.E.2d 88, 91 (1950) (concluding no general plan of development existed where only 11 out of 21 lots contained similar restrictions).

The doctrine of implied restrictive covenants is also known as the “common scheme of development” doctrine. Tisdale v. Buch, 2013 ME 95, ¶ 13, 81 A.3d 377. “We have acknowledged, but never expressly adopted, the common scheme of development doctrine ....” Id.; see also Thompson v. Pendleton, 1997 ME 127, ¶ 11 n.2, 697 A.2d 56; 3 W Partners v. Bridges, 651 A.2d 387, 389 (Me. 1994); Olson v. Albert, 523 A.2d 585, 588 (Me. 1987); Chase v. Burrell, 474 A.2d 180, 181-82 (Me. 1984). A factual finding that a common scheme of development exists would be a prerequisite to an action seeking to enforce a deed restriction because the reciprocal servitudes arising from the common scheme provide a party standing to challenge a property owner's alleged violation. See Restatement (Third) of Servitudes § 2.14 (Am. L. Inst. 2000). Because of the interlocutory nature of this appeal, we express no opinion regarding the viability or applicability of the common scheme of development doctrine.

On the other hand, when there is a general scheme for the benefit of the purchasers in a development, and then, either by permission or acquiescence, or by a long chain of violations, the property becomes so substantially changed that the whole character of the subdivision has been altered so that the whole objective for which the restrictive covenants were originally entered into must be considered at an end, then the courts will not enforce such restrictive covenants.

label: qa_CONST. LAW_mbe_730
subject: CONST. LAW
question: Congress passed a bill prohibiting the President from granting a pardon to any person who had not served at least one-third of the sentence imposed by the court which convicted that person. The President vetoed the bill, claiming that it was unconstitutional. Nevertheless, Congress passed it over his veto by a two-thirds vote of each house. This act of Congress is
final_answer:
To determine the constitutionality of the act of Congress that prohibits the President from granting a pardon to any person who had not served at least one-third of the sentence imposed by the court, we must analyze the President's pardon power as granted by the Constitution and relevant legal precedents.

The Constitution, in Article II, Section 2, grants the President the power to grant reprieves and pardons for offenses against the United States, except in cases of impeachment. This power is broadly interpreted to include the ability to commute sentences and to place conditions on pardons. The key aspect of the President's pardon power is that it is not subject to congressional control, as established by the Constitution and supported by legal precedents (Source 1, Source 3, and Source 4).

Given this understanding, let's evaluate the options:

(A) The fact that the bill was enacted over the President's veto by a two-thirds vote of each house makes it law in terms of the legislative process. However, this does not address the issue of whether the law itself is constitutional, particularly in relation to the President's pardon power.

(B) This option suggests that the act is constitutional because it is a necessary and proper means of carrying out the powers of Congress. However, the Constitution explicitly grants the pardon power to the President, and there is no provision that allows Congress to limit this power in the manner described.

(C) This option states that the act is unconstitutional because it interferes with the plenary power of the President to grant pardons. Given the constitutional grant of pardon power to the President and the legal precedents supporting its breadth and exclusivity, this option aligns with the understanding that Congress cannot circumscribe the President's pardon power.

(D) This option is incorrect because it misstates the procedure for overriding a Presidential veto based on constitutional grounds. The Constitution requires a two-thirds vote of each house to override a veto, regardless of the grounds for the veto.

Based on the analysis, the act of Congress that prohibits the President from granting a pardon under certain conditions directly interferes with the President's constitutional power to grant pardons, which is not subject to congressional limitation.

Answer: (C)

choices.A: constitutional, because it was enacted over the President's veto by a two-thirds vote of each house.
choices.B: constitutional, because it is a necessary and proper means of carrying out the powers of Congress.
choices.C: unconstitutional, because it interferes with the plenary power of the President to grant pardons.
choices.D: unconstitutional, because a Presidential veto based upon constitutional grounds may be overridden only with the concurrence of three-fourths of the state legislatures.

II, s 2, cl. 1, derives, of the language of that clause itself, and of the unbroken practice since 1790 compels the conclusion that the power flows from the Constitution alone, not from any legislative enactments, and that it cannot be modified, abridged, or diminished by the Congress.
snap_answer:
To determine the constitutionality of the act of Congress in question, we must analyze the relevant provisions of the United States Constitution. The Constitution grants the President the power to grant reprieves and pardons, except in cases of impeachment, as stated in Article II, Section 2, Clause 1. This power is quite broad and is considered part of the executive's authority to exercise clemency.

However, the Constitution also outlines the process for enacting legislation, including the ability of Congress to override a presidential veto with a two-thirds majority vote in both the House of Representatives and the Senate, as per Article I, Section 7, Clause 2 (also known as the Presentment Clause). This provision ensures that Congress has the authority to enact laws even if the President disagrees with them, provided they can muster the necessary supermajority.

The key issue here is whether Congress can limit the President's pardon power through legislation. The President's claim that the bill is unconstitutional because it interferes with his plenary power to grant pardons raises a significant question about the separation of powers and the limits of congressional authority over executive powers.

Option (D) is incorrect because it misstates the procedure for overriding a presidential veto. The Constitution requires a two-thirds vote of each house of Congress to override a veto, not the concurrence of three-fourths of the state legislatures, which is the process for amending the Constitution.

Option (A) suggests that the act is constitutional simply because it was passed over the President's veto by a two-thirds vote of each house. While this is the correct procedure for overriding a veto, it does not address the underlying issue of whether Congress has the authority to limit the President's pardon power.

Option (B) implies that the act is constitutional because it is a necessary and proper means of carrying out the powers of Congress. However, this does not directly address the potential conflict with the President's constitutional authority to grant pardons.

Option (C) argues that the act is unconstitutional because it interferes with the President's plenary power to grant pardons. Given the broad and unqualified nature of the pardon power granted to the President in the Constitution, any attempt by Congress to limit this power could indeed be seen as an overreach of congressional authority.

Considering these points, the most relevant issue is whether Congress can limit the President's power to grant pardons, which is a constitutional authority vested in the executive branch. The Constitution does not explicitly grant Congress the power to restrict the President's pardon authority in the manner described.

Therefore, the act of Congress is unconstitutional because it interferes with the President's constitutional authority to grant pardons, which is considered a plenary power not subject to legislative limitation.

Answer: (C)
hyde_passage:
The power to grant reprieves and pardons is vested in the President, as stated in Article II, Section 2, Clause 1 of the United States Constitution, and is considered a plenary power that is not subject to limitation by Congress, except in cases of impeachment. This constitutional provision grants the President broad authority to exercise clemency, and any congressional attempt to restrict this power may be deemed an unconstitutional infringement on the executive's authority. As such, congressional legislation that interferes with the President's pardon power, such as by imposing conditions on the granting of pardons, may be considered an overreach of legislative authority and thus unconstitutional. 

Answer: (C)

evidence_store.text: Article II, Section 2 of the United States Constitution grants the President the power to grant reprieves and pardons for offenses against the United States, except in cases of impeachment. This pardon power is not subject to control by Congress, and it includes the power to commute a sentence on any conditions the President chooses (as long as the conditions do not offend some other constitutional provision). As applied to the facts here, Congress quite simply lacks the authority to circumscribe the President's power to pardon persons convicted under the statute at issue.

pardon: A pardon is the use of executive power that exempts the individual to whom it was given from punishment. The president’s pardon power is based on Article II of the Constitution which says, “...he shall have power to grant reprieves and pardons for offenses against the United States, except in cases of impeachment. Unlike a commutation, which shortens or eliminates an individual’s punishment, a pardon absolves the individual of guilt. For example, President Trump commuted Roger Stone’s prison sentence so that Mr. Stone did not serve the punishment for his guilty conduct. On the other hand, President John F. Kennedy pardoned all first time offenders convicted of crimes under the Narcotics Control Act of 1956 to signal to Congress that the law needed to be changed. [Last updated in August of 2020 by the Wex Definitions Team]

Begin with the Article II pardon power. The Constitution says that the President "shall have Power to grant Reprieves and Pardons for Offences against the United States, except in Cases of Impeachment." U.S. Const. art. II, § 2, cl. 1. The Framers modeled this provision on the pardon power of the English Crown. Schick v. Reed , 419 U.S. 256, 260-64, 95 S.Ct. 379, 42 L.Ed.2d 430 (1974). That English practice thus illuminates "the operation and effect of a pardon," making the one a helpful lantern in seeing the other. United States v. Wilson , 32 U.S. (7 Pet.) 150, 160, 8 L.Ed. 640 (1833) (Marshall, C.J.). As an act of executive mercy, id. ; see 4 William Blackstone, Commentaries *389-90, the pardon power includes the authority to commute sentences in whole or in part, Schick , 419 U.S. at 260, 95 S.Ct. 379. The President may place conditions on a pardon or commutation. Ex parte Wells , 59 U.S. (18 How.) 307, 314-15, 15 L.Ed. 421 (1855). The only potential limits on the President's pardon power are constitutional in nature, and even those are little defined. Schick , 419 U.S. at 267, 95 S.Ct. 379 ; see Ohio Adult Parole Auth. v. Woodard , 523 U.S. 272, 279-85, 118 S.Ct. 1244, 140 L.Ed.2d 387 (1998) (opinion of Rehnquist, C.J.).

A fair reading of the history of the English pardoning power, from which our Art. II, s 2, cl. 1, derives, of the language of that clause itself, and of the unbroken practice since 1790 compels the conclusion that the power flows from the Constitution alone, not from any legislative enactments, and that it cannot be modified, abridged, or diminished by the Congress.

¶36 Instead, Moran proposes that the removal of his political disabilities in Virginia represents the "functional equivalent" of a pardon under Wisconsin law. The Wisconsin Constitution provides the governor with the "power to grant reprieves, commutations and pardons." WIS. CONST. art. V, § 6. The pardon power rests solely with the governor, and there are no constitutional standards the governor must follow when determining whether to grant clemency. In re Webb , 89 Wis. 354, 356, 62 N.W. 177 (1895) ; see also Donald Leo Bach, To Forgive, Divine: The Governor's Pardoning Power , WIS. LAW. , Feb. 2005, at 12, 14.

label: qa_CONTRACTS_mbe_1077
subject: CONTRACTS
question: For this question only, assume the following facts. Logger offered a fair price for the timber rights in question, and Landholder accepted the offer. The 2,000-acre tract was an abundant wild-game habitat and had been used for many years, with Landholder's permission, by area hunters. Logger's performance of the timber contract would destroy this habitat. Without legal excuse and over Landholder's strong objection, Logger repudiated the contract before commencing performance. Landholder could no
final_answer:
To determine whether the neighbor can sue Logger for breach of the contract, we need to analyze whether the neighbor is an intended beneficiary of the contract or merely an incidental beneficiary.

According to the retrieved passages, a third-party beneficiary contract is one in which the promisor has a legal interest in performance in favor of the third party, and the performance of the terms of the contract between two parties must necessarily result in a direct benefit to a third party that was so intended by the parties (Source 3). The passages also state that to determine whether a contract was made expressly for the benefit of a third party, we must look to the intentions of the parties to the contract (Source 1).

In this case, there is no indication that Logger and Landholder intended to benefit the neighbor when they entered into the contract. The contract was for the sale of timber rights, and the neighbor's interest in the contract (i.e., having a clearer view of the nearby lake) is not directly related to the purpose of the contract. The destruction of the wild-game habitat and the neighbor's view of the lake are merely incidental consequences of the contract.

Furthermore, the passages suggest that a party must show that it will derive more than an incidental benefit from the performance of the promisor (Source 3). In this case, the neighbor's benefit is incidental, as it is not a direct result of the contract's purpose.

Therefore, based on the analysis, the neighbor will probably lose, as only an incidental beneficiary, if any, of the Logger-Landholder contract.

Answer: (A)

choices.A: lose, as only an incidental beneficiary, if any, of the Logger-Landholder contract.
choices.B: lose, as a maintainer of nuisance litigation.
choices.C: prevail, as a third-party intended beneficiary of the Logger-Landholder contract.
choices.D: prevail, as a surrogate for Landholder in view of his inability to enforce the contract.

gold_passage: Third-party beneficiary status is an exception to the privity requirement that applies in situations where the contract reflects the express or implied intention of the parties to benefit the third-party. State of Montana v. United States, 124 F.3d 1269, 1273 (Fed.Cir.1997). This analysis is commonly referred to as the “intention-to-benefit test.” Schuerman v. United States, 30 Fed.Cl. 420, 433 (1994). “Any such intent is found through an examination of the parties' entire contractual relationsh
snap_answer:
To answer this question, we need to analyze the relationship between the parties involved and the nature of the contract between Logger and Landholder.

1. **Contract Formation and Beneficiaries**: When Logger and Landholder entered into a contract for the timber rights, they created a contractual relationship between themselves. The contract's primary purpose was for Logger to harvest timber, presumably for commercial purposes, and for Landholder to receive compensation for those rights.

2. **Third-Party Beneficiaries**: In contract law, a third-party beneficiary is someone who is not a party to the contract but may have a legal right to enforce the contract. For someone to be considered a third-party beneficiary, the contract must have been made for their direct benefit. The neighbor, whose view of the nearby lake is obscured by the standing timber, might argue that they benefit from the contract's performance (i.e., the removal of the timber). However, the contract was not made with the intent to benefit the neighbor directly.

3. **Incidental Beneficiaries**: An incidental beneficiary is someone who may benefit from the performance of a contract but was not intended by the parties to benefit from it. The neighbor's improved view is an incidental benefit and not the primary purpose of the Logger-Landholder contract.

4. **Maintainer of Nuisance Litigation**: This option does not apply directly to the scenario described. The concept of being a maintainer of nuisance litigation typically refers to someone who supports or encourages frivolous or vexatious lawsuits, which is not relevant to the neighbor's situation in this context.

5. **Surrogate for Landholder**: The idea of the neighbor acting as a surrogate for Landholder because Landholder cannot afford to enforce the contract is not a recognized legal principle in this context. The neighbor's interests and Landholder's interests are distinct, and the neighbor's right to enforce the contract would depend on their status as a beneficiary, not on their ability to act on behalf of Landholder.

Given these considerations, the most likely outcome is that the neighbor would lose because they are, at best, an incidental beneficiary of the contract between Logger and Landholder. The contract was not entered into with the intent to benefit the neighbor directly, and therefore, they do not have a legal right to enforce it.

Answer: (A)
hyde_passage:
According to the doctrine of third-party beneficiaries, a contract may be enforced by a person who is not a party to the contract if the contract was made for their direct benefit, and the parties to the contract intended to confer a benefit on that person. However, if the benefit to the third party is merely incidental to the contract's primary purpose, that party is not considered an intended beneficiary and lacks standing to enforce the contract. In this case, the neighbor's improved view is an incidental benefit of the contract between Logger and Landholder, and therefore, the neighbor is not an intended beneficiary with the right to enforce the contract. 

Answer: (A)

evidence_store.text: "A contract made expressly for the benefit of a third person may be enforced by him at any time before the parties thereto rescind it." N.D.C.C. § 9-02-04. "To enforce a contract between two others, a third party must have been intended by the contracting parties to be benefited by the contract." Apache Corp. v. MDU Resources Group, Inc. , 1999 ND 247, ¶ 10, 603 N.W.2d 891. To determine whether the contract at issue was made expressly for the third party's benefit, "we must look to the intentions of the parties to the contract." O'Connell v. Entertainment Enterprises, Inc. , 317 N.W.2d 385, 388 (N.D. 1982). "The intentions of the parties to a contract must be ascertained from the written contract alone, if possible." Id ."Another guideline for determining whether a party is a third-party beneficiary, as opposed to an incidental beneficiary, is whether the benefit to the third party was 'within the contemplation' of the contracting parties." Moen v. Norwest Bank of Minot , 647 F.Supp. 1333, 1341-42 (D.N.D. 1986).

A contract that benefits a third party may confer rights on that party if he is an intended beneficiary rather than merely an incidental beneficiary. However, an intended beneficiary can enforce the contract only after his rights have vested. Vesting will occur when the beneficiary (i) manifests assent to the promise in a manner invited or requested by the parties, (ii) brings suit to enforce the promise, or (iii) materially changes position in justifiable reliance on the promise.

Generally, only those who are parties to a contract, or those in privity with a party, have the right to recover under a contract. However, an entity that is not a party to the contract may enforce the provisions of the contract by demonstrating that it is a third-party beneficiary thereto. A third-party beneficiary contract is one in which the promisor has a legal interest in performance in favor of the third party and in which the performance of the terms of the contract between two parties must necessarily result in a direct benefit to a third party which was so intended by the parties. A third party must show that it will derive more than an incidental benefit from the performance of the promisor.

Parties that benefit from a government contract are generally incidental beneficiaries and may not enforce the contract absent a clear intent to the contrary. Id. "This clear intent hurdle is not satisfied by a contract's recitation of interested constituencies, vague hortatory pronouncements, statements of purpose, explicit reference to a third party, or even a showing that that the contract operates to the third parties' benefit and was entered into with them in mind." County of Santa Clara v. Astra USA, Inc. , 588 F.3d 1237, 1244 (9th Cir. 2009) (quotation marks and alterations omitted) (citing Klamath , 204 F.3d at 1212 ; Orff v. United States , 358 F.3d 1137, 1145 (9th Cir. 2004) ).

In order to enforce a contract by virtue of being a third-party beneficiary, an entity must show (1) a clear intent by the actual parties to the contract to benefit the third party; (2) a duty imposed on one of the contracting parties in favor of the third party; and (3) performance of the contract terms is necessary to render the third party a direct benefit intended by the parties to the contract. Among these three factors, the intent of the contracting parties to benefit the third-party is controlling.

label: qa_CONTRACTS_mbe_1015
subject: CONTRACTS
question: For this question only, assume that Farmer rightfully refused Painter's demand for payment. If Painter immediately terminates the contract without painting the third barn, what is Painter entitled to recover from Farmer?
final_answer:
To determine what Painter is entitled to recover from Farmer, we need to analyze the situation step by step, considering the principles of contract law, particularly those related to breach of contract and the recovery of damages.

1. **Understanding the Contract**: The contract between Painter and Farmer involves painting three barns. The specifics of the payment terms are crucial: if payment is conditioned on the completion of all three barns, then Painter's right to payment is contingent upon fulfilling this condition.

2. **Breach of Contract**: If Farmer rightfully refused Painter's demand for payment, it implies that Painter has not fulfilled his obligations under the contract, or there was a misunderstanding regarding the payment terms. However, the question specifies that Painter terminates the contract without painting the third barn after Farmer's refusal. This action could be seen as a breach by Painter, depending on the contract's terms.

3. **Recovery for Partial Performance**: Generally, when a party partially performs under a contract and the other party breaches, the partially performing party can recover for the value of the services rendered, minus any damages caused by their own breach. This is often calculated as the reasonable value of the services provided.

4. **Analyzing the Options**:
    - **(A)** Suggests that because payment was conditioned on completing all three barns, Painter is entitled to nothing. This overlooks the principle that a party who has partially performed may still recover the value of those services, even if the full condition (all three barns) was not met.
    - **(B)** Refers to "expenditures plus anticipated profit," which is a method of calculating damages in certain breach of contract cases, particularly in construction contracts. However, this option introduces a specific monetary cap ($4,000) without context on how it relates to the contract's terms or the value of the work done.
    - **(C)** Offers the "reasonable value of Painter's services in painting the two barns, less Farmer's damages, if any, for Painter's failure to paint the third barn." This aligns with the general principle that a party can recover for the value of services rendered, adjusted for any damages their actions (or inactions) may have caused to the other party.
    - **(D)** Focuses on the "amount that the combined value of the two painted barns has been increased by Painter's work." While this considers the benefit to Farmer, it doesn't directly address the standard method of calculating recovery for partial performance under a contract.

5. **Conclusion**: Given the principles of contract law and the specifics provided, the most appropriate answer reflects the concept of recovering the value of services rendered, adjusted for any damages caused by the breaching party's actions.

Answer: (C)

choices.A: Nothing, because payment was expressly conditioned on completion of all three barns.

To determine what Painter is entitled to recover from Farmer, we need to analyze the situation step by step, considering the principles of contract law, particularly those related to breach of contract and the recovery of damages.

1. **Understanding the Contract**: The contract between Painter and Farmer involves painting three barns. The specifics of the payment terms are crucial: if payment is conditioned on the completion of all three barns, then Painter's right to payment is contingent upon fulfilling this condition.

2. **Breach of Contract**: If Farmer rightfully refused Painter's demand for payment, it implies that Painter has not fulfilled his obligations under the contract, or there was a misunderstanding regarding the payment terms. However, the question specifies that Painter terminates the contract without painting the third barn after Farmer's refusal. This action could be seen as a breach by Painter, depending on the contract's terms.

3. **Recovery for Partial Performance**: Generally, when a party partially performs under a contract and the other party breaches, the partially performing party can recover for the value of the services rendered, minus any damages caused by their own breach. This is often calculated as the reasonable value of the services provided.

4. **Analyzing the Options**:

    - **(B)** Refers to "expenditures plus anticipated profit," which is a method of calculating damages in certain breach of contract cases, particularly in construction contracts. However, this option introduces a specific monetary cap ($4,000) without context on how it relates to the contract's terms or the value of the work done.
    - **(C)** Offers the "reasonable value of Painter's services in painting the two barns, less Farmer's damages, if any, for Painter's failure to paint the third barn." This aligns with the general principle that a party can recover for the value of services rendered, adjusted for any damages their actions (or inactions) may have caused to the other party.
    - **(D)** Focuses on the "amount that the combined value of the two painted barns has been increased by Painter's work." While this considers the benefit to Farmer, it doesn't directly address the standard method of calculating recovery for partial performance under a contract.

5. **Conclusion**: Given the principles of contract law and the specifics provided, the most appropriate answer reflects the concept of recovering the value of services rendered, adjusted for any damages caused by the breaching party's actions.

Answer: (C)

evidence_store.text: performance: In contract law, there must be consideration for the contract to be enforceable. In some contracts, this means that one party promises something in exchange for a performance from second party. The action of completing that performance fulfills the second party’s obligations in the contract. For example, one party may promise to pay another party $100 if a second party paints their house. The painting of the house is the performance required of the second party and doing so would complete their role in the contract. It is usually not necessary for the completed performance to be perfect; instead, the standard that must be met is substantial performance. In some cases, a party who has made a valid contract and does not adequately perform their role in a contract. This is called a breach of contract. A breach might be that a party does not perform their role at all, performs it but with major defects, or that the party only performs a portion of their obligations, called partial performance. In some scenarios, a partial performance might be acceptable–especially if contract was divisible–though the compensation under the contract would be altered to reflect the level of performance. In other cases, however, partial performance does not entitle the breaching party to any compensation. Further, a breaching party may be required by a court to fulfill their obligations under a contract if there is no other way to make the situation just; this is called specific performance.  [Last updated in August of 2020 by the Wex Definitions Team]

Contractual duties are discharged where it has become impossible to perform them. The occurrence of an unanticipated or extraordinary event may make contractual duties impossible to perform. If the nonoccurrence of the event was a basic assumption of the parties in making the contract, and neither party has assumed the risk of the event's occurrence, duties under the contract may be discharged. Impossibility must arise after entering into the contract. If there is impossibility, each party is excused from duties that are yet to be performed. If either party has partially performed prior to the existence of facts resulting in impossibility, that party has a right to recover in quasi-contract at the contract rate, or for the reasonable value of his performance if that mode of valuation is more convenient. While that value is usually based on the benefit received by the defendant (unjust enrichment), it also may be measured by the detriment suffered by the plaintiff (the reasonable value of the work performed).

However, a material breach will not discharge an obligation of the non-breaching party that arose before the alleged breach. Where the parties promise to exchange performances, it is a condition of each party's duty to render performance that there be no uncured material failure by the other party to render any such performance due at an earlier time. RESTATEMENT (SECOND) OF CONTRACTS § 237. Parties sometimes make several promises to exchange performances under a single contract. Under section 237, “only duties with respect to the performances to be exchanged under the particular exchange of promises are affected by a failure of one of those performances.... [A] duty under the same contract [is not] affected if it was not one to render a performance to be exchanged under an exchange of promises.... Furthermore, only duties to render performance are affected. A claim for damages that has already arisen as a result of a claim for partial breach is not discharged under the rule stated in this Section.” RESTATEMENT (SECOND) OF CONTRACTS § 237 cmt.

[s]pecific performance at the end of a protracted litigation under compulsion is practically never full performance of the contract; instead, there has been an extensive and injurious partial breach. In such a case, the court should decree the payment of damages for the partial breach that has already occurred, even though obedience of the decree will prevent the commission of further breaches

What a party must show to invoke the doctrine of part-performance is essentially two-fold: (1) the underlying agreement must be clearly established and (2) the partial performance must be sufficient, shown by clear and convincing evidence, and directly referable to the established oral agreement. Id. Security has failed to establish an agreement between the LLC and Crumb; thus, the application of the doctrine of part-performance is precluded.

question: Which of the following, if established, most strongly supports the college in refusing to give Barnes a statement of reasons or an opportunity for a hearing?
final_answer:
To determine which option most strongly supports the college in refusing to give Barnes a statement of reasons or an opportunity for a hearing, we need to consider the principles of due process as they apply to public employees, particularly in the context of employment termination.

Due process requires that a public employee who has a property interest in their job (i.e., they can only be fired for cause) must be given notice of the charges against them and an opportunity to respond before being terminated. This principle is established in cases such as Cleveland Board of Education v. Loudermill (1985).

Let's analyze the options given:

(A) Barnes's academic performance had been substandard.
- This option suggests a reason for termination that could be considered "cause" under a contract or policy that requires termination only for cause. However, simply stating that his performance was substandard does not inherently justify denying him due process.

(B) A speech he made that was critical of administration policies violated a college regulation concerning teacher behavior.
- This option could potentially justify termination if the speech indeed violated a specific regulation. However, public employees also have First Amendment rights, and speech critical of administration policies might be protected. The key factor here would be whether the speech was made as a citizen on a matter of public concern or as an employee on a matter of personal interest, as outlined in cases like Rankin v. McPherson (1987). If the speech was protected, terminating Barnes without due process would be problematic.

(C) Barnes worked at the college for less than five years.
- The length of time Barnes worked at the college does not directly relate to whether he has a property interest in his job or whether he is entitled to due process. What matters is the nature of his employment contract or the policies governing his employment.

(D) Barnes could be replaced with a more competent teacher.
- This option does not address the procedural due process requirements for terminating an employee. The ability to replace an employee with someone more competent does not justify bypassing due process.

Given these considerations, option (B) is the most relevant because it directly addresses a potential reason for termination that could be considered "cause" under an employment contract or policy. However, it's crucial to note that even if Barnes's speech violated a college regulation, he would still be entitled to due process, including notice of the charges and an opportunity to respond, before being terminated.

Therefore, none of the options directly justify refusing Barnes due process without considering the nature of his employment contract and the specific circumstances of his termination. But if we must choose based on the information given and the context of due process, option (B) comes closest to potentially justifying the college's actions, albeit still requiring due process to be followed.

Answer: (B)

snap_answer:
To determine which option most strongly supports the college in refusing to give Barnes a statement of reasons or an opportunity for a hearing, we need to consider the context of due process rights, particularly in the context of employment and public institutions like colleges. Due process, as guaranteed by the Fourteenth Amendment of the U.S. Constitution, requires that individuals be given fair procedures before being deprived of life, liberty, or property. In the context of employment, this often means that before a public employee can be fired, they must be given a reason for the termination and an opportunity to respond or appeal, especially if the employee has a property interest in their job (such as tenure).

Let's analyze the options:

(A) Barnes's academic performance had been substandard. - While poor performance could be a valid reason for termination, it does not inherently negate the need for due process. Thus, it doesn't strongly support refusing a hearing.

(B) A speech he made that was critical of administration policies violated a college regulation concerning teacher behavior. - If Barnes's speech violated a specific regulation, this could potentially justify disciplinary action, including termination, without necessarily violating due process rights, especially if the regulation is clear and the violation is egregious. However, even in such cases, due process would typically still require some form of notice and opportunity to be heard.

(C) Barnes worked at the college for less than five years. - The length of time an individual has worked at an institution can affect their due process rights, particularly if they have not yet achieved tenure or a similar form of job security. If Barnes is considered an at-will employee or has not yet vested in a form of job protection, the college might have more latitude in terminating his employment without extensive due process.

(D) Barnes could be replaced with a more competent teacher. - The potential to replace Barnes with someone more competent does not directly relate to the procedural rights owed to him before termination. Due process is concerned with the fairness of the procedures used to terminate employment, not the potential benefits of replacing an employee.

Considering these options, the one that most strongly supports the college in refusing to give Barnes a statement of reasons or an opportunity for a hearing is related to his status as an employee and whether he has a protected property interest in his job. If Barnes has not vested in such protections due to the length of his employment, the college might argue it has more flexibility in terminating his employment without extensive procedural protections.

Answer: (C)
hyde_passage: According to the doctrine of due process as outlined in the Fourteenth Amendment, a public employee's right to a hearing and statement of reasons before termination is contingent upon their having a property interest in their job, such as tenure. The U.S. Supreme Court has established that a public employee's property interest in their job is determined by the specific terms of their employment contract or applicable state law. If an employee has not vested in a property interest, such as through achieving tenure, they may be considered an at-will employee, in which case the institution may have greater latitude in terminating their employment without extensive procedural protections.

evidence_store.text: Under the Due Process Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment, a public employee who is subject to removal only for "cause" under a statute, ordinance, or personnel document has a property interest in continued employment that cannot be taken away without due process of law. The Court has held that such an employee generally must be given notice of the charges and apretermination opportunity to respond to those charges. The employee must also be given a subsequent evidentiary hearing regarding the termination (with reinstatement if the employee prevails). [Cleveland Board of Education v. Loudermill (1985)]

[¶10] Due process in the context of a public employee's continued employment requires that the public employee have notice and an opportunity to be heard before termination of the employment. Loudermill , 470 U.S. at 542, 105 S.Ct. 1487. Due process requirements are met when the employee is provided with appropriate pre-termination and post-termination proceedings. Smutka v. City of Hutchinson , 451 F.3d 522, 526 (8th Cir. 2006).

Thus, in the normal course of terminating a public employee who has a property interest in his or her job, "the root requirement of the Due Process Clause" is the provision of adequate notice and "some kind of a hearing" to a public employee "before he is deprived of any significant property interest." Loudermill , 470 U.S. at 542, 546, 105 S.Ct. 1487 (internal quotations omitted); accord, e.g., Gilbert , 520 U.S. at 929, 117 S.Ct. 1807 ; Zinermon , 494 U.S. at 132, 110 S.Ct. 975 ("In situations where the State feasibly can provide a predeprivation hearing before taking property, it generally must do so regardless of the adequacy of a postdeprivation tort remedy to compensate for the taking.").

Under the Due Process Clause of the Fifth Amendment, a person has a liberty interest in the exercise of specific rights provided by the Constitution, including freedom of speech. A government employee may not be fired for expressing his views regarding public issues, but can be fired for speech that disrupts the employer's policies or undermines the employer's authority. The Court has an expansive interpretation of what a public issue is in this context. [see Rankin v. McPherson (1987)] A public employee who is subject to removal only for "cause" has a property interest in his job and must be given notice of the charges against him that are to be the basis for his job termination, and a pretermination opportunity to respond to those charges. Due process does not necessarily entitle to a pretermination evidentiary hearing; a post-termination evidentiary hearing is probably sufficient. [See Cleveland Board of Education v. Loudermill (1985)]

Next, Defendants argue that the Complaint does not plausibly plead a procedural due process claim under the Fourteenth Amendment because Plaintiffs held temporary, at-will positions and, as such, had no property interest in their continued employment with the OSC. See Docket Nos. 11 at 11; 11-1 at 10; 22 at 2; see also Maymi v. P.R. Ports Auth. , 515 F.3d 20, 29 (1st Cir. 2008) (holding that a plaintiff plausibly pleads a procedural due process violation by showing (i) that she is a public employee with a property interest in her continued employment; and (ii) that the defendant deprived her of this property interest without due process). The Court agrees.

question: On Ladd's claim against Commuter, Commuter's best defense is that
final_answer:
To determine Commuter's best defense, let's analyze each option in the context of a negligence claim, which seems to be the basis of Ladd's claim against Commuter.

(A) "Her conduct was not the cause in fact of the harm" - This defense addresses the issue of causation. For Ladd to succeed in a negligence claim, he must prove that Commuter's actions (or lack thereof) were the cause in fact (actual cause) of his injuries. If Commuter can show that her conduct did not directly lead to Ladd's harm, this could be a viable defense. However, this option is more about challenging the factual link between Commuter's actions and the harm suffered by Ladd.

(B) "She used reasonable care in the maintenance of her brakes" - This defense goes to the heart of a negligence claim, which requires the plaintiff to prove that the defendant breached a duty of care owed to the plaintiff. If Commuter can demonstrate that she exercised reasonable care in maintaining her brakes, she can argue that she did not breach her duty of care to Ladd. This is a strong defense because it directly challenges the allegation of negligence.

(C) "She could not reasonably foresee Ladd's presence in the street" - This option relates to the foreseeability of harm, which is a component of the duty of care in negligence. If Commuter could not have reasonably anticipated that someone like Ladd would be in the street, she might argue that she did not owe a duty of care to him in that specific circumstance. However, this defense might be weaker if there were circumstances that should have put Commuter on notice of potential pedestrians or if the presence of someone in the street is generally foreseeable.

(D) "She did not act willfully and wantonly" - This defense speaks to the level of culpability. Willful and wanton conduct is a higher level of fault than ordinary negligence and involves a disregard for the safety of others. While this might be relevant in certain contexts, such as in determining punitive damages or in cases involving more egregious conduct, it does not directly address the core issue of whether Commuter was negligent.

Considering these options, the best defense for Commuter, given the context of a negligence claim, would be to argue that she used reasonable care in the maintenance of her brakes. This directly addresses the issue of whether she breached her duty of care to Ladd, which is central to a negligence claim.

Answer: (B)

evidence_store.text: "In any action for negligence, a plaintiff must establish the defendant owed a duty of care to the plaintiff, the defendant breached that duty, and the defendant's breach proximately caused the plaintiff's injury." Wieland v. Owner-Operator Servs., Inc. , 540 S.W.3d 845, 848 (Mo. banc 2018).

"The threshold issue in any negligence action is whether the defendant owed the plaintiff a duty." Rando v. ANCO Insulations, Inc. , 08-1163, 08-1169, p. 27 (La. 5/22/09), 16 So.3d 1065, 1086. Whether a duty is owed is a question of law for the court to decide, subject to de novo review. Id. ; Burch v. SMG Schindler Elev. Corp. , 14-1356, 14-1357, 14-1358, p. 6 (La. App. 4 Cir. 4/7/16), 191 So.3d 652, 658. "There is an almost universal duty on the part of the defendant in a negligence action to use reasonable care to avoid injury to another." Rando , 08-1163, p. 27, 16 So.3d at 1086. "In general, the duty owed by a business owner to its customers is that of reasonable care." Caronia v. McKenzie's Pastry Shoppes , 97-0681, p. 3 (La. App. 4 Cir. 10/1/97), 700 So.2d 1315, 1317. "This duty extends to keeping the premises safe from unreasonable risks of harm or warning persons of known dangers." Id. "[T]he basic standard is that the defendant must exercise the degree of care that we might expect from an ordinarily prudent person under the same or similar circumstance." Chatman , 15-1179, p. 14, 197 So.3d at 376. A reasonably prudent person will avoid creating an unreasonable risk of harm. Id.

To answer this question, we need to understand the concept of risk of loss and how it is allocated between the buyer and seller under the Uniform Commercial Code (UCC). 

The UCC provides default rules for allocating the risk of loss, but parties can also agree to allocate the risk of loss between themselves through a contract provision. 

In a shipment contract, the risk of loss passes to the buyer when the goods are duly delivered to the carrier, even if the shipment is under reservation. This means that once the seller delivers the goods to the carrier, the risk of loss shifts to the buyer.

Given this information, if Byer is suing Singer for breach of contract, and assuming that the contract between Byer and Singer is a shipment contract, the risk of loss would likely be on Byer. This is because, under a shipment contract, the risk of loss passes to the buyer when the goods are delivered to the carrier.

Therefore, Byer's action against Singer for breach of contract will probably not succeed, because the risk of loss was on Byer.

To answer this question, we need to consider the principles of contract law, specifically those related to the risk of loss in contracts for the sale of goods. Under the Uniform Commercial Code (UCC), which governs sales contracts in the United States, the risk of loss can shift between the buyer and the seller depending on the terms of the contract and the circumstances of the transaction.

Generally, in a contract for the sale of goods, the risk of loss passes from the seller to the buyer when the buyer takes physical possession of the goods. However, if the contract specifies that the seller is responsible for shipping the goods to the buyer, the risk of loss may remain with the seller until the goods are delivered to the carrier, unless the contract states otherwise.

Without specific details on the contract between Byer and Singer, such as who was responsible for shipping and whether the goods were damaged or lost during shipping, it's difficult to determine with certainty who bears the risk of loss. However, we can analyze the options given:

(A) Suggests that the carrier will be deemed Singer's agent. This could imply that Singer retains the risk of loss, as the carrier's actions could be imputed to Singer. However, this is not necessarily true without more context on the relationship between Singer and the carrier.

(B) Implies that the risk of loss was on Singer. This could be true if the contract specified that Singer was responsible for ensuring the goods were delivered safely to Byer.

(C) Suggests that the contract is impossible to perform. This would only be relevant if some unforeseen event occurred that made it literally impossible for Singer to fulfill the contract, which is not indicated by the information provided.

(D) States that the risk of loss was on Byer. This would be true if Byer had taken possession of the goods or if the contract specified that the risk of loss passed to Byer at some point before delivery.

Given the lack of specific information about the contract terms and the circumstances of the transaction, it's challenging to definitively choose between options (B) and (D), as both could potentially be correct depending on the specifics of the contract and the transaction. However, since the question asks for the most probable outcome based on typical contract law principles and without specific details suggesting otherwise, we might lean towards the general principle that the risk of loss passes to the buyer upon delivery to the carrier, unless the contract specifies otherwise.

Therefore, without explicit details indicating that Singer retained the risk of loss, the most probable answer based on standard contract law principles would be:

Answer: (D)

This general rule applies unless the parties have agreed to shift the risk of loss to one of the parties under specific circumstances. In the absence of explicit contractual terms indicating that the seller retains the risk of loss, the buyer generally bears the risk of loss once the goods are delivered to the carrier for shipment.

Answer: (D)

evidence_store.text: risk of loss: The responsibility that a carrier, borrower or user of property or goods takes on if there is a damage or loss to the object is the risk of loss. An insurance company can also agree to insure the object against the risk of loss. The Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) § 2–509 allocates the risk of loss when there is no contractual breach, and shifts the risk of loss to the buyer when the seller or bailee take certain steps to deliver the goods in certain circumstances. UCC § 2–510 lays out when risk of loss passes to the buyer when one of the parties breaches the contract. The UCC only provides default rules, however, and parties may allocate the risk of loss between themselves as they wish through a contract provision. Parties may even require that one party obtains insurance to cover risk of loss. [Last updated in December of 2020 by the Wex Definitions Team]

41-02-57. (2-509) Risk of loss in the absence of breach. 1. Where the contract requires or authorizes the seller to ship the goods by carrier a. if it does not require him to deliver them at a particular destination, the risk of loss passes to the buyer when the goods are duly delivered to the carrier even though the shipment is under reservation (section 41-02-53); but b. if it does require him to deliver them at a particular destination and the goods are there duly tendered while in the possession of the carrier, the risk of loss passes to the buyer when the goods are there duly so tendered as to enable the buyer to take delivery.

Under the U.C.C., when the contract authorizes or requires the seller to ship the goods by carrier but does not explicitly require the seller to deliver them at a particular location, risk of loss passes to the buyer when the goods are duly delivered to the carrier. [U.C.C. 2-509] However, if the goods are so defective that the buyer has a right to reject them, the risk of loss does not pass until the defects are cured or the buyer accepts the goods. [U.C.C. 2-510(1)]

§ 2-509. Risk of Loss in the Absence of Breach. (1) Where the contract requires or authorizes the seller to ship the goodsby carrier (a) if it does not require him to deliver them at a particular destination, the risk of loss passes to the buyer when the goods are duly delivered to the carrier even though the shipment is under reservation (Section 2-505); but (b) if it does require him to deliver them at a particular destination and the goods are there duly tendered while in the possession of the carrier, the risk of loss passes to the buyer when the goods are there duly so tendered as to enable the buyer to take delivery. (2) Where the goods are held by a bailee to be delivered without being moved, the risk of loss passes to the buyer (a) on his receipt of a negotiable document of title covering the goods; or (b) on acknowledgment by the bailee of the buyer's right to possession of the goods; or (c) after his receipt of a non-negotiable document of title or other written direction to deliver, as provided in subsection (4)(b) of Section 2-503. (3) In any case not within subsection (1) or (2), the risk of loss passes to the buyer on his receipt of the goods if the seller is a merchant; otherwise the risk passes to the buyer on tender of delivery. (4) The provisions of this section are subject to contrary agreement of the parties and to the provisions of this Article on sale on approval (Section 2-327) and on effect of breach on risk of loss (Section 2-510).

Shipment Contract: Under Article 2 of the Uniform Commercial Code, a shipment contract is one way in which buyer and seller could contract to allocate risk of loss between buyer and seller when goods or lost or damaged before the buyer obtains them from the seller and neither buyer nor seller is to blame for the loss. With a shipment contract, the buyer bears the risk of loss for the goods prior to actually receiving them. Here, the seller's only duty is to get the goods to a common carrier and make proper delivery arrangements for the goods to get to the seller. After this, under a shipment contract, if any loss occurs the buyer bears the risk of loss and is responsible for the costs. A shipment contract could be identified with language stating it is free on board and the city where the seller is located.

question: Venner, the owner of Greenacre, a tract of land, entered into an enforceable written agreement with Brier providing that Venner would sell Greenacre to Brier for an agreed price. At the place and time designated for the closing, Venner tendered an appropriate deed, but Brier responded that he had discovered a mortgage on Greenacre and would not complete the transaction, because Venner 's title was not free of encumbrances, as the contract required. Venner said that it was his intent to pay the m
final_answer:
To determine Venner's best legal argument, let's analyze the situation step by step:

1. **Contract Terms**: The contract between Venner and Brier requires Venner to provide a title that is free of encumbrances. However, it does not explicitly state how encumbrances are to be dealt with at the time of closing.

2. **Venner's Proposal**: Venner offers to put the proceeds of the sale into escrow to pay off the mortgage, ensuring that the title will be free of encumbrances after the sale. This proposal is made because the balance due on the mortgage is substantially less than the contract purchase price, implying that the sale can proceed with the understanding that the mortgage will be paid off from the proceeds.

3. **Analysis of Options**:
   - **(A)** This option suggests an implied right for the seller to use contract proceeds to clear the title. While this might seem reasonable, the contract's requirement for a title free of encumbrances at the time of closing does not necessarily imply that the seller can use the sale proceeds to achieve this.
   - **(B)** The concept that the lien of the mortgage shifts from Greenacre to the contract proceeds is not a standard principle in real estate law without specific contractual or statutory provisions to that effect.
   - **(C)** The doctrine of equitable conversion generally applies once the contract is signed, making the buyer the equitable owner of the property and the seller the equitable owner of the purchase price. However, this doctrine does not directly address the issue of how encumbrances are to be handled at closing.
   - **(D)** This option suggests that no provision of the contract has been breached by Venner. Given that Venner is offering a solution (placing the proceeds in escrow to pay off the mortgage) that would ultimately result in Brier receiving a title free of encumbrances, it can be argued that Venner has not breached the contract. The contract requires the title to be free of encumbrances, and Venner's proposal ensures this will be the case, albeit after the closing.

4. **Conclusion**: The best argument for Venner is that he has not breached the contract because he is willing to ensure the title is free of encumbrances through the escrow arrangement. This approach aligns with the contractual requirement without necessitating a strict interpretation that the title must be free of encumbrances at the exact moment of closing, as long as the mechanism for clearing the title is in place.

Answer: (D)

choices.B: the lien of the mortgage shifts from Greenacre to the contract proceeds
choices.C: under the doctrine of equitable conversion, title has already passed to Brier, and the only issue is how the purchase price is to be allocated
choices.D: no provision of the contract has been breached by Venner

evidence_store.text: Absent a provision to the contrary, a contract for the sale of land contains an implied promise by the seller that she will deliver to the buyer a marketable title at the time of closing. This promise imposes on the seller an obligation to deliver a title that is free from reasonable doubt; i.e.,free from questions that might present an unreasonable risk of litigation. Title is marketable if a reasonably prudent buyer would accept it in the exercise of ordinary prudence. An easement that reduces the value of the property (e.g., an easement of way for the benefit of a neighbor) generally renders title unmarketable. If the buyer determines, prior to closing, that the seller's title is unmarketable, he must notify the seller and allow a reasonable time to cure the defect. If the seller is unable to acquire title before closing, so that title remains unmarketable, the buyer can rescind, sue for damages caused by the breach, or obtain specific performance with an abatement of the purchase price. However, the buyer cannot rescind prior to closing on grounds that the seller's title is unmarketable. Thus, a buyer cannot withhold payments or seek other remedies on grounds that the seller's title is unmarketable prior to the date of promised delivery.

There is an implied covenant in every land sale contract that at closing the seller will provide the buyer with title that is marketable. It need not be perfect title, but it must be free from questions that might present an unreasonable risk of litigation.

Unless expressly provided otherwise, all contracts for the sale of land contain an implied covenant by the seller that he will deliver to the buyer at closing a marketable title. Generally, inability to establish a record chain of title will render title unmarketable. If a seller attempts to rely on adverse possession to establish marketable title, many courts will hold that such title is not marketable until the adverse possessor has perfected it by a judgment quieting title. label: qa_EVIDENCE_mbe_920
subject: EVIDENCE
question: Mr. Denby was charged with the sale of narcotics. The federal prosecutor arranged with Mrs. Denby for her to testify against her husband in exchange for leniency in her case. At trial, the prosecution calls Mrs. Denby, who had been granted immunity from prosecution, to testify, among other things, that she saw her husband sell an ounce of heroin. Which of the following statements is most clearly correct in the federal courts?
final_answer:
In the federal courts, the decision in Trammel v. United States (1980) is relevant here. According to this decision, a spouse's testimony against the other spouse can be compelled, even over the spouse's objection, as long as the testifying spouse has been granted immunity from prosecution. The adverse spousal privilege, which allows a spouse to refuse to testify against the other spouse, is a privilege that belongs to the testifying spouse, not the defendant spouse. However, the defendant spouse does have a privilege to prevent the other spouse from testifying against them if the other spouse wishes to invoke the privilege. But in this case, since Mrs. Denby has been granted immunity and is testifying as part of an agreement, the key issue is whether she can be compelled to testify against her will if she wishes to invoke the adverse spousal privilege.

Given the details of the case, Mrs. Denby can indeed be called as a witness and required to testify, including about seeing her husband sell heroin, because she has been granted immunity and the adverse spousal privilege belongs to her, not her husband. However, the critical aspect here is that Mrs. Denby herself cannot refuse to testify based on the adverse spousal privilege if she has agreed to testify as part of her plea agreement and has been granted immunity. The statement that "Mrs. Denby can refuse to be a witness against her husband" might seem plausible because it references the adverse spousal privilege, but given the context that she has agreed to testify and has been granted immunity, this option might not directly apply as the most clearly correct statement in the context provided.

Considering the specifics of the question and the context of federal courts, the most clearly correct statement, given that Mrs. Denby has agreed to testify and has been granted immunity, would relate to her ability to be compelled to testify. However, the question's phrasing and the context of legal precedents like Trammel v. United States suggest that the adverse spousal privilege's invocation and the implications of immunity are crucial.

Thus, considering the nuances of the adverse spousal privilege and the impact of immunity, the most accurate reflection of the law in this context, given the provided options and understanding that Mrs. Denby has agreed to testify and been granted immunity, would lead us to consider the implications of these legal principles carefully.

Answer: (D)

choices.A: Mrs. Denby cannot be called as a witness over her husband's objection.
choices.B: Mrs. Denby can be called as a witness but cannot testify, over Mr. Denby's objection, that she saw him sell heroin.
choices.C: Mrs. Denby can refuse to be a witness against her husband.
choices.D: Mrs. Denby can be required to be a witness and to testify that she saw her husband sell heroin.

evidence_store.text: It is a longstanding tradition in our jurisprudence that a witness has the right to assert or waive spousal privilege when given the opportunity to testify against her spouse. Trammel v. United States , 445 U.S. 40, 52, 100 S.Ct. 906, 63 L.Ed.2d 186 (1980) ("[T]he witness-spouse alone has a privilege to refuse to testify adversely; the witness may be neither compelled to testify nor foreclosed from testifying."). While our Court has not squarely addressed the issue of spousal privilege in the context of a criminal trial in which the spouses are co-defendants, analogous cases suggest that we should afford it significant protection. In In re Malfitano , 633 F.2d 276, 280 (3d Cir. 1980), we recognized that a wife may refuse to testify against her husband before a grand jury even when she is alleged to be involved in the charged crime. We grounded our holding in public policy concerns, namely, that the social benefits of marriage counsel against creating strife in a marriage by compelling a witness to testify against her spouse. Id. at 278-80. Later, in Ammar , we noted that a severance may be granted to a co-defendant spouse in order to protect her right to testify in her own defense. 714 F.2d at 257.

Federal courts recognize two distinct marital privileges under Rule 501 of the Federal Rules of Evidence: the marital confidential communication privilege and the adverse spousal testimony privilege. See United States v. Jackson, 939 F.2d 625, 627 (8th Cir.1991). Under the adverse spousal testimony privilege, the privilege at issue in this case, an individual “may be neither compelled to testify nor foreclosed from testifying” against the person to whom he or she is married at the time of trial.1 Trammel v. United States, 445 U.S. 40, 53, 100 S.Ct. 906, 914, 63 L.Ed.2d 186 (1980); see also Jackson, 939 F.2d at 627. The privilege therefore rests with the testifying spouse, who may waive the privilege without the consent of the defendant spouse. See Trammel, 445 U.S. at 53, 100 S.Ct. 906.
