evidence_store.text: The Supreme Court recently discussed the two principles that govern the authority of a State to regulate interstate commerce: "[f]irst, state regulations may not discriminate against interstate commerce; and second, States may not impose undue burdens on interstate commerce." South Dakota v. Wayfair, Inc., --- U.S. ----, 138 S.Ct. 2080, 2090-91, 201 L.Ed.2d 403 (2018). The Court stated that laws "that discriminate against interstate commerce face 'a virtually per se rule of invalidity.' " Id. at 2091 (quoting Granholm, 544 U.S. at 470, 125 S.Ct. 1885 ). However, "[s]tate laws that 'regulat[e] even-handedly to effectuate a legitimate local public interest ... will be upheld unless the burden imposed on such commerce is clearly excessive in relation to the putative local benefits.' " Id. (quoting Pike v. Bruce Church, Inc., 397 U.S. 137, 142, 90 S.Ct. 844, 25 L.Ed.2d 174 (1970) ). As the Supreme Court noted, "these two principles guide the courts in adjudicating cases challenging state laws under the Commerce Clause." Id.

It has long been accepted that the Commerce Clause not only grants Congress the authority to regulate commerce among the States, but also directly limits the power of the States to discriminate against interstate commerce. See, e.g., Hughes v. Oklahoma, 441 U.S. 322, 326, 99 S.Ct. 1727, 1731, 60 L.Ed.2d 250 (1979); H.P. Hood & Sons, Inc. v. Du Mond, 336 U.S. 525, 534-535, 69 S.Ct. 657, 663, 93 L.Ed. 865 (1949); Welton v. Missouri, 91 U.S. (1 Otto) 275, 23 L.Ed. 347 (1876). This “negative” aspect of the Commerce Clause prohibits economic protectionism-that is, regulatory measures designed to benefit in-state economic interests by burdening out-of-state competitors. *274 See, e.g., Bacchus Imports, Ltd. v. Dias, 468 U.S. 263, 270-273, 104 S.Ct. 3049, 3054-3056, 82 L.Ed.2d 200 (1984); H.P. Hood & Sons, supra, 336 U.S., at 532-533, 69 S.Ct., at 662; Guy v. Baltimore, 100 U.S. (10 Otto) 434, 443, 25 L.Ed. 743 (1880). Thus, state statutes that clearly discriminate against interstate commerce are routinely struck down, see, e.g., Sporhase v. Nebraska ex rel. Douglas, 458 U.S. 941, 102 S.Ct. 3456, 73 L.Ed.2d 1254 (1982); Lewis v. BT Investment Managers, **1808 Inc., 447 U.S. 27, 100 S.Ct. 2009, 64 L.Ed.2d 702 (1980); Dean Milk Co. v. Madison, 340 U.S. 349, 71 S.Ct. 295, 95 L.Ed. 329 (1951), unless the discrimination is demonstrably justified by a valid factor unrelated to economic protectionism, see, e.g., Maine v. Taylor, 477 U.S. 131, 106 S.Ct. 2440, 91 L.Ed.2d 110 (1986).

Our precedents place state and local laws into one of three categories for purposes of commerce clause analysis, depending on the degree to which they affect interstate commerce: (1) laws that expressly discriminate against interstate commerce; (2) laws that, although neutral on their face, bear more heavily on interstate than local commerce; and (3) laws that may have a mild effect on interstate commerce but in practice do not give local firms any competitive advantage over firms located elsewhere. Park Pet Shop , 872 F.3d at 501-02 (citing Nat'l Paint , 45 F.3d at 1131 ). A law falling into the first category is presumed to be almost per se unconstitutional and is subject to rigorous scrutiny that will allow the law to stand only if it serves a legitimate governmental interest and there is no reasonable non-discriminatory means of furthering that interest. Park Pet Shop , 872 F.3d at 501 (citing Nat'l Paint , 45 F.3d at 1131 ); see Davis , 553 U.S. at 338, 128 S. Ct. at 1808 ; United Haulers Ass'n, Inc. v. Oneida-Herkimer Solid Waste Mgmt. Auth. , 550 U.S. 330, 338-39, 127 S. Ct. 1786, 1793, 167 L.Ed.2d 655 (2007) ; Or. Waste Sys., Inc. v. Dep't of Environ. Quality of State of Or. , 511 U.S. 93, 100-01, 114 S. Ct. 1345, 1351, 128 L.Ed.2d 13 (1994). A law falling into the second category is analyzed according to its effect. If the impact is so strong that the law effectively operates as an embargo on interstate commerce, it is treated as the equivalent of a facially discriminatory law and is subject to the same demanding scrutiny given to such a law. Id. (citing Nat'l Paint , 45 F.3d at 1131 ). But if the law regulates even-handedly and only incidentally burdens interstate commerce, then it is examined under the balancing test set forth in Pike v. Bruce Church, Inc. , 397 U.S. 137, 142, 90 S. Ct. 844, 847, 25 L.Ed.2d 174 (1970), to determine whether it is animated by a legitimate public purpose and, if so, whether the burden the law imposes on interstate commerce is excessive in relation to that interest. Park Pet Shop , 872 F.3d at 501-02 ; see Davis , 553 U.S. at 338-39, 128 S. Ct. at 1808-09 ; Brown-Forman Distillers Corp. v. N.Y. State Liquor Auth. , 476 U.S. 573, 579, 106 S. Ct. 2080, 2084, 90 L.Ed.2d 552 (1986). A law falling into the third category is examined solely to determine whether it has a rational basis. Park Pet Shop , 872 F.3d at 502 (citing Nat'l Paint , 45 F.3d at 1131-32 ). The district court concluded that the Hammond ordinance falls into the third category and indeed is justified by a rational basis. We review its conclusion de novo. Legato Vapors, LLC v. Cook , 847 F.3d 825, 828-29 (7th Cir. 2017).

Discrimination claims under the dormant Commerce Clause require a two-step analysis. “ ‘[D]iscrimination’ simply means differential treatment of in-state and out-of-state economic interests that benefits the former and burdens the latter.” Or. Waste, 511 U.S. at 99, 114 S.Ct. 1345 (emphasis added). “[I]f a state law discriminates against out-of-state goods or nonresident economic actors, the law can be sustained only on a showing that it is narrowly tailored to ‘advanc[e] a legitimate local purpose.’ ” Tennessee Wine, ––– U.S. ––––, 139 S. Ct. at 2461 (alteration in original) (quoting Dep't of Revenue of Ky. v. Davis, 553 U.S. 328, 338, 128 S.Ct. 1801, 170 L.Ed.2d 685 (2008)). However, “[w]here [a] statute regulates evenhandedly to effectuate a legitimate local public interest, and its effects on interstate commerce are only incidental, it will be upheld unless the burden imposed on such commerce is clearly excessive in relation to the putative local benefits.” City of Philadelphia v. New Jersey, 437 U.S. 617, 624, 98 S.Ct. 2531, 57 L.Ed.2d 475 (1978) (quoting Pike v. Bruce Church, Inc., 397 U.S. 137, 142, 90 S.Ct. 844, 25 L.Ed.2d 174 (1970)). “The crucial inquiry, therefore, must be directed to determining whether [the statute] is basically a protectionist measure, or whether it can fairly be viewed as a law directed to legitimate local concerns, with effects upon interstate commerce that are only incidental.” Ibid.

"[I]n all but the narrowest circumstances, state laws violate the Commerce Clause if they mandate 'differential treatment of in-state and out-of-state economic interests that benefits the former and burdens the latter.' " Granholm v. Heald, 544 U.S. 460, 472, 125 S.Ct. 1885, 161 L.Ed.2d 796 (2005) (quoting Or. Waste Sys., Inc. v. Dep't of Envtl. Quality of Or., 511 U.S. 93, 99, 114 S.Ct. 1345, 128 L.Ed.2d 13 (1994) ). However, "[w]here [a] statute regulates evenhandedly to effectuate a legitimate local public interest, and its effects on interstate commerce are only incidental, it will be upheld unless the burden imposed on such commerce is clearly excessive in relation to the putative local benefits." Philadelphia v. New Jersey, 437 U.S. 617, 624, 98 S.Ct. 2531, 57 L.Ed.2d 475 (1978) (quoting Pike v. Bruce Church, Inc., 397 U.S. 137, 142, 90 S.Ct. 844, 25 L.Ed.2d 174 (1970) ). "The crucial inquiry, therefore, must be directed to determining whether [the statute] is basically a protectionist measure, or whether it can fairly be viewed as a law directed to legitimate local concerns, with effects upon interstate commerce that are only incidental." Ibid.

label: qa_CONTRACTS_mbe_672
subject: CONTRACTS
question: For this question only, assume the following facts. Paul commenced work on Saturday morning, and had finished half the painting by the time he quit work for the day. That night, without the fault of either party, the office building was destroyed by fire. Which of the following is an accurate statement?
final_answer:
To answer this question, let's analyze the situation step by step, considering the principles of contract law related to impossibility of performance and quasi-contract recovery.

1. **Impossibility of Performance**: When an event occurs that makes it impossible to perform a contractual duty, and this event was not foreseeable or assumed by the parties, the contractual duties may be discharged. In this case, the office building was destroyed by fire without the fault of either party. This event makes it impossible for Paul to complete the painting, as the subject of the work (the office building) no longer exists.

2. **Discharge of Contractual Duties**: Given that the destruction of the building was unforeseen and not the fault of either party, both parties' contractual duties are discharged due to impossibility. Paul cannot paint a building that no longer exists, and Alice's obligation to pay is tied to the completion of the work, which is now impossible.

3. **Quasi-Contract Recovery**: When a contract is discharged due to impossibility, a party who has partially performed may recover in quasi-contract for the value of the work done before the impossibility arose. This is based on the principle of unjust enrichment, ensuring that one party is not unfairly benefited at the expense of the other. Since Paul had completed half the painting before the fire, he can recover the value of this work from Alice.

Considering these points:

- **Option (A)** is incorrect because, although both parties' contractual duties are indeed discharged, Paul can recover in quasi-contract for the work he had already done.
- **Option (C)** is incorrect because both parties' duties are discharged, not just Paul's. The destruction of the building affects both parties' obligations under the contract.
- **Option (D)** mentions reliance damages, which is not directly relevant to the quasi-contract recovery for the value of work done, as described in the provided sources.
- **Option (B)** is correct because both parties' contractual duties are discharged due to the impossibility caused by the fire, and Paul can recover in quasi-contract for the half of the work he completed before the fire.

Answer: (B)

choices.A: Both parties' contractual duties are discharged, and Paul can recover nothing from Alice.
choices.B: Both parties' contractual duties are discharged, but Paul can recover in quasi-contract from Alice.
choices.C: Only Paul's contractual duty is discharged, because Alice's performance (payment of the agreed price) is not impossible.
choices.D: Only Paul's contractual duty is discharged, and Paul can recover his reliance damages from Alice.

gold_passage: A cause of action for quasi-contract invokes consideration of equitable principles, rather than of contract. “ ‘... [It] is an obligation ... created by the law without regard to the intention of the parties, and is designed to restore the aggrieved party to [its] former position by return of the thing or its equivalent in money. [Citations.]’ ” (Unilab Corp. v. Angeles-IPA (2016) 244 Cal.App.4th 622, 639, 198 Cal.Rptr.3d 211; 1 Witkin, Summary of Cal. Law (11th ed. 2017) Contracts, § 1050; Rest
snap_answer:
To answer this question, we need to consider the concept of frustration of contract and the doctrine of quasi-contract.

When a contract becomes impossible to perform due to circumstances beyond the control of either party, the contract is said to be frustrated. In this case, the destruction of the office building by fire makes it impossible for Paul to complete the painting, and for Alice to receive the benefit of the painting.

The general rule is that when a contract is frustrated, the contractual duties of both parties are discharged. This means that neither party is liable for breach of contract.

However, the discharge of contractual duties does not necessarily mean that one party cannot recover anything from the other. In cases where one party has partially performed the contract before it was frustrated, that party may be able to recover in quasi-contract (also known as restitution) for the benefits conferred on the other party.

In this case, Paul had finished half the painting before the building was destroyed. Although his contractual duty is discharged, he may be able to recover in quasi-contract from Alice for the value of the work he had done.

Therefore, the correct answer is:

Answer: (B)
hyde_passage: According to the doctrine of frustration of contract, when a contract becomes impossible to perform due to circumstances beyond the control of either party, the contractual duties of both parties are discharged. However, the discharge of contractual duties does not preclude a party from seeking restitution for benefits conferred prior to the frustration of the contract, pursuant to the doctrine of quasi-contract. Under this doctrine, a party who has partially performed a contract that is subsequently frustrated may recover the value of the benefits conferred on the other party, as long as such recovery is not inconsistent with the intention of the parties as expressed in the contract.

evidence_store.text: Contractual duties are discharged where it has become impossible to perform them. The occurrence of an unanticipated or extraordinary event may make contractual duties impossible to perform. If the nonoccurrence of the event was a basic assumption of the parties in making the contract, and neither party has assumed the risk of the event's occurrence, duties under the contract may be discharged. Impossibility must arise after entering into the contract. If there is impossibility, each party is excused from duties that are yet to be performed. If either party has partially performed prior to the existence of facts resulting in impossibility, that party has a right to recover in quasi-contract at the contract rate, or for the reasonable value of his performance if that mode of valuation is more convenient. While that value is usually based on the benefit received by the defendant (unjust enrichment), it also may be measured by the detriment suffered by the plaintiff (the reasonable value of the work performed).

Frustration of purpose arises when "the obligor's performance can still be carried out, but the supervening event fundamentally has changed the nature of the parties' overall bargain." Id. at 246, 67 A.3d 702. "The frustration must be so severe that it is not fairly to be regarded as the risks that [the party invoking the doctrine] assumed under the contract." Id. at 247, 67 A.3d 702 (alteration in original) (quoting Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 265 cmt. a). Relief from performance of contractual obligations on the theory of frustration of purpose "will not be lightly granted; the evidence must be clear, convincing[,] and adequate." A-Leet Leasing Corp., 150 N.J. Super. at 397, 375 A.2d 1208. By comparison, under the related doctrine of impossibility or impracticability of performance, a party is excused from having to perform his contract obligations "where performance has become literally impossible, or at least inordinately more difficult, because of the occurrence of a supervening event that was not within the original contemplation of the contracting parties." JB Pool Mgmt., 431 N.J. Super. at 246, 67 A.3d 702.

[¶75] Commercial frustration concerns "the effect of supervening circumstances upon the rights and duties of the parties [where] '[p]erformance remains possible but the expected value of performance to the party seeking to be excused has been destroyed by a fortuitous event[.]' " Wallace v. Pinnacle Bank-Wyoming , 2012 WY 64, ¶ 19, 275 P.3d 1250, 1255 (Wyo. 2012) (quoting Downing v. Stiles , 635 P.2d 808, 811 (Wyo. 1981) ) (internal citation omitted). The commercial frustration defense is not "available if the difficulties that frustrate the purpose of [the] contract ... reasonably could have been foreseen by the promisor when the parties entered into [the] contract. To apply the doctrine of commercial frustration, there must be a frustrating event not reasonably foreseeable[.]" 30 Williston on Contracts § 77:95 (4th ed.), Westlaw (database updated November 2018).

Unjust enrichment claims are based on quasi-contract and are predicated on the absence of an express contract controlling the circumstances. Generally, when a valid, express contract covers the subject matter of the parties' dispute, there can be no recovery under a quasi-contract theory because parties should be bound by their express agreements.

Frustration of purpose is a defense to a breach of contract claim and constitutes an avoidance of all or part of a plaintiff's contract claim. WFND, LLC v. Fargo Marc, LLC, 2007 ND 67, ¶ 18, 730 N.W.2d 841. “[F]rustration of purpose ‘occurs when “after a contract is made, a party's principal purpose is substantially frustrated without his fault by the occurrence of an event the non-occurrence of which was a basic assumption on which the contract was made.” ’ ” Id. (quoting Tallackson Potato Co., Inc. v. MTK Potato Co., 278 N.W.2d 417, 424 n. 6 (N.D.1979)).

label: qa_nan_mbe_535
subject: nan
question: Able, an attorney, sued Clinton, a client, for his fee, based on an agreed hourly rate. Clinton subpoenaed the attorney's time records for the days on which he purported to have worked for Clinton, in order to show that Able had billed an impossible number of hours to Clinton and others on those days. Clinton's subpoena provided that any information concerning the matters handled for other clients be deleted or masked. Able moved to quash the subpoena on the ground of attorneyclient privilege. T
final_answer:
To answer this question, let's analyze the situation step by step:

1. **Understanding the Attorney-Client Privilege**: The attorney-client privilege is a rule of evidence that protects confidential communications between an attorney and client during the legal consultation. It is designed to encourage full and frank communication between attorneys and their clients, allowing clients to feel secure in sharing sensitive information with their attorneys without fear of that information being disclosed to others.

2. **Application of the Privilege**: The privilege belongs to the client, not the attorney. This means that only the client can waive the privilege, not the attorney. However, the attorney has a duty to assert the privilege on behalf of the client if the client has not waived it.

3. **The Subpoena's Scope**: Clinton's subpoena is seeking time records from Able, which may include information about work done for other clients. However, the subpoena specifies that any information concerning matters handled for other clients should be deleted or masked. This suggests an attempt to balance the need for Clinton to access relevant information with the protection of other clients' privileged communications.

4. **Analyzing the Options**:
   - **(A)** Suggests that the information about hours billed is not within the privilege. This could be true to the extent that billing hours themselves might not be considered privileged. However, the context in which those hours were billed (e.g., the specific tasks performed for other clients) could be privileged.
   - **(B)** Indicates that an attorney has no right to invoke his clients' privilege without instructions from the clients. This is generally correct, as the privilege belongs to the client. However, the question doesn't specify that Able is invoking the privilege on behalf of other clients without their instruction.
   - **(C)** Mentions a "right of privacy for the work product in his files." While attorneys do have some protection for their work product under the work product doctrine, this doctrine is more about protecting the attorney's mental impressions and work product from discovery rather than a broad "right of privacy."
   - **(D)** Suggests that the subpoena should be quashed because no permission was obtained from the other clients to divulge information from their files. Given that the subpoena allows for the deletion or masking of information concerning other clients, this option seems less directly relevant, as it implies a concern about revealing confidential information about other clients without their consent.

5. **Conclusion**: The most relevant issue here is whether the information sought (hours billed) is considered privileged and whether the attorney can invoke the privilege on behalf of his clients without their explicit instruction. Given that the subpoena allows for the protection of other clients' information by deleting or masking it, the primary concern is not about revealing confidential communications but about the billing hours, which are not necessarily privileged.

**Answer: (B)**

choices.A: upheld because the information about hours billed is not within the privilege
choices.B: upheld because an attorney has no right to invoke his clients' privilege without instructions from the clients
choices.C: quashed because an attorney is entitled to a right of privacy for the work product in his files
choices.D: quashed because no permission was obtained from the other clients to divulge information from their files

2. **Application of the Privilege**: The privilege belongs to the client, not the attorney. This means that only the client can waive the privilege, not the attorney. However, the attorney has a duty to assert the privilege on behalf of the client if the client has not waived it.

3. **The Subpoena's Scope**: Clinton's subpoena is seeking time records from Able, which may include information about work done for other clients. However, the subpoena specifies that any information concerning matters handled for other clients should be deleted or masked. This suggests an attempt to balance the need for Clinton to access relevant information with the protection of other clients' privileged communications.

4. **Analyzing the Options**:
   - **(A)** Suggests that the information about hours billed is not within the privilege. This could be true to the extent that billing hours themselves might not be considered privileged. However, the context in which those hours were billed (e.g., the specific tasks performed for other clients) could be privileged.
   - **(B)** Indicates that an attorney has no right to invoke his clients' privilege without instructions from the clients. This is generally correct, as the privilege belongs to the client. However, the question doesn't specify that Able is invoking the privilege on behalf of other clients without their instruction.
   - **(C)** Mentions a "right of privacy for the work product in his files." While attorneys do have some protection for their work product under the work product doctrine, this doctrine is more about protecting the attorney's mental impressions and work product from discovery rather than a broad "right of privacy."
   - **(D)** Suggests that the subpoena should be quashed because no permission was obtained from the other clients to divulge information from their files. Given that the subpoena allows for the deletion or masking of information concerning other clients, this option seems less directly relevant, as it implies a concern about revealing confidential information about other clients without their consent.

5. **Conclusion**: The most relevant issue here is whether the information sought (hours billed) is considered privileged and whether the attorney can invoke the privilege on behalf of his clients without their explicit instruction. Given that the subpoena allows for the protection of other clients' information by deleting or masking it, the primary concern is not about revealing confidential communications but about the billing hours, which are not necessarily privileged.

**Answer: (B)**
hyde_passage:
According to the principles of evidence law, the attorney-client privilege protects confidential communications between an attorney and client, and this privilege belongs to the client, who must explicitly waive it. An attorney has a duty to assert the privilege on behalf of the client but generally cannot invoke the privilege without the client's instruction. The work product doctrine, on the other hand, protects the attorney's mental impressions and work product from discovery. In the context of a subpoena seeking time records, where the information about hours billed may not be considered privileged but the context of those hours could be, the key issue is whether the attorney can assert the privilege without client instruction. 

Answer: (B)

evidence_store.text: As a general rule, the attorney-client privilege does not protect facts, unless the client's communication to the attorney is the source of the facts. See id. 1182-83 (noting the attorney-client privilege protects only client confidences and does not protect facts obtained by the attorney from third parties or facts shared by the attorney with the client). "The privilege 'must be strictly constructed and accepted 'only to the very limited extent that permitting a refusal to testify or excluding relevant evidence has a public good transcending the normally predominant principle of utilizing all rational means for ascertaining truth.' " Id. at 1183.

There are some cases that suggest that any disclosure of a document to a third person waives the work-product immunity to which it would otherwise be entitled. Decisions to this effect confuse the work-product immunity with the attorney-client privilege. The attorney-client privilege has its basis in the confidential nature of the communication and the reason for the privilege ordinarily ceases to exist if confidentiality is destroyed by voluntary disclosure to a third person. But the purpose of the work-product rule is not to protect the evidence from disclosure to the outside world but rather to protect it only from the knowledge of opposing counsel and his client, thereby preventing its use against the lawyer gathering the materials.

There is no privilege regarding a communication that is relevant to an issue of breach of duty by the lawyer to his client or by the client to her lawyer. If privilege is applicable, the lawyer would be able to invoke the privilege on behalf of his clients. The lawyer's authority to do this is presumed in the absence of any evidence to the contrary. Documents prepared by the lawyer for his own use in prosecuting his client's case are protected by the work product rule. Time records are not prepared for litigation purposes; they are not related to the substance of the client's case. Because time records are not communications to or from the client and the identity of clients is often not considered to be within the privilege, the billing records of other clients may not be privileged and their consent may not have been necessary even without deletions.

"Work product is distinguishable from attorney-client privilege." Id. at 366 n.3. "While the attorney-client privilege protects disclosures by the client to the attorney and is absolute in all but the most extraordinary situations, work product 'is designed to prevent a party from reaping the benefits of his opponent's labors ... for the same or a related cause of action.' " Id. (quoting State ex rel. Friedman v. Provaznik, 668 S.W.2d 76, 80 (Mo. banc 1984) ). As discussed, supra , the documents at issue are protected by attorney-client privilege. Thus, Brennan's argument that they are not protected as work product is inapplicable.

As is clear from the text of the rule as well as the jurisprudence surrounding the work product doctrine, confidentiality is not a cornerstone of the accompanying privilege. Pa.R.C.P. 4003.3 ; Bagwell , 103 A.3d at 417-18 (contrasting the attorney-client privilege, which flows from confidential communication, to the attorney work product doctrine, which does not) ; U.S. v. Am. Telephone & Telegraph Co. , 642 F.2d 1285, 1299 (D.C. Cir. 1980) ("[T]he work product privilege does not exist to protect a confidential relationship"); U.S. v. Massachusetts Inst. of Tech. , 129 F.3d 681, 687 (1st Cir. 1997) ("the [attorney-client privilege] ... is designed to protect confidentiality, so that any disclosure outside the magic circle is inconsistent with the privilege; by contrast, work product protection is provided against adversaries[.]"); see also Barrick , 91 A.3d at 687 (Baer, J., Opinion in Support of Affirmance) ("If [an attorney work product] document was sent to the expert witness, it would be protected by Rule 4003.3's work product provision."). In fact, some courts and commentators have suggested that the minority of courts finding waiver of the work product doctrine on the basis of disclosure are confusing the work product and attorney-client privileges. See Kittitas Cnty. v. Allphin , 190 Wash.2d 691, 416 P.3d 1232, 1243 n.13 (2018) ("While it does not appear that any states or federal courts have officially adopted the same standard of waiver for both the attorney-client privilege and work product protection, the standards for waiver are sometimes conflated.") (citing 8 Wright & Miller, Fed. Prac. & Proc. (civ.), The Work-Product Rule - Matters Protected by the Work-Product Rule § 2024 (3d. ed. April 2017)). Accordingly, because the purposes of the attorney-client privilege and the work product doctrine are different, the waiver analysis for each rule necessarily diverges as well. See Allphin , 416 P.3d at 1243 ("The different standards of waiver for the attorney-client privilege and work product protection result from the differing purposes behind the doctrines.").

label: qa_nan_mbe_68
subject: nan
question: The committee wants you to draft a statute governing the recording of deeds that fixes priorities of title, as reflected on the public record, as definitely as possible. Which of the following, divorced from other policy considerations, would best accomplish this particular result?
final_answer:
To determine which option best accomplishes the goal of fixing priorities of title as reflected on the public record as definitely as possible, let's analyze each option step by step:

(A) Eliminate the requirement of witnesses to deeds. 
- Eliminating the requirement of witnesses to deeds could potentially reduce the reliability of the deeds recorded, as witnesses can provide additional assurance of the authenticity and voluntariness of the transaction. This change does not directly address the issue of fixing priorities of title based on the public record.

(B) Make time of recording the controlling factor.
- By making the time of recording the controlling factor, the priority of title would be determined solely by when the deed was recorded. This approach provides clarity and simplicity in determining priorities, as it relies on an objective and easily verifiable factor (the time of recording). It ensures that the public record directly reflects the order in which titles were recorded, thereby fixing priorities as definitely as possible.

(C) Make irrebuttable the declarations in the deeds that valuable consideration was paid.
- While ensuring that declarations of valuable consideration are irrebuttable might add certainty to the validity of the transactions, it does not directly impact the priority of title. The consideration paid is relevant to the validity and enforceability of the contract, but it does not determine the order of priority among competing claims.

(D) Make the protection of bona fide purchasers the controlling factor.
- Protecting bona fide purchasers is an important policy consideration, but making it the controlling factor for determining priorities could introduce variability and subjectivity. The status of a bona fide purchaser depends on several factors, including good faith, lack of notice, and the payment of valuable consideration. This approach might not provide the same level of definiteness as a purely time-based system, as it requires an assessment of the purchaser's state of knowledge and intentions.

Based on the analysis, making the time of recording the controlling factor provides the most objective and straightforward method for determining priorities of title, as it directly ties the priority to the public record without introducing subjective elements.

Answer: (B)

choices.A: Eliminate the requirement of witnesses to deeds.
choices.B: Make time of recording the controlling factor.
choices.C: Make irrebuttable the declarations in the deeds that valuable consideration was paid.
choices.D: Make the protection of bona fide purchasers the controlling factor.

gold_passage: A deed to personalty needs no attesting witness to make it valid; in other respects, the principles applicable to deeds to lands are applicable to deeds to personalty. However, generally a deed is not necessary to convey title to personalty.

question: A federal statute requires United States civil service employees to retire at age 75. However, that statute also states that civil service employees of the armed forces must retire at age 65. Prentis, a 65-year-old civil service employee of the Department of the Army, seeks a declaratory judgment that would forbid his mandatory retirement until age 75. The strongest argument that Prentis can make to invalidate the requirement that he retire at age 65 is that the law
final_answer:
To approach this question, let's break down the key elements and apply them to the information provided in the retrieved passages.

1. **Understanding the Claim**: Prentis, a 65-year-old civil service employee of the Department of the Army, is challenging a federal statute that requires his retirement at age 65, while other civil service employees can work until age 75.

2. **Identifying the Legal Issue**: The issue here involves a federal statute (not a state law), so we're dealing with the Fifth Amendment to the U.S. Constitution, which applies to the federal government, rather than the Fourteenth Amendment, which applies to the states.

3. **Relevant Constitutional Provision**: The Fifth Amendment's Due Process Clause is relevant here. According to the passages, particularly Source 1 and Source 3, the Supreme Court has recognized that discrimination by the federal government can violate the Due Process Clause of the Fifth Amendment, and the standards for equal protection analysis under the Fifth Amendment are the same as those under the Fourteenth Amendment.

4. **Analyzing the Options**:
   - **(A)** Denies him a privilege or immunity of national citizenship: This argument might be relevant in certain contexts but doesn't directly apply to the age discrimination claim based on the information provided.
   - **(B)** Deprives him of a property right without just compensation: This argument pertains to the Takings Clause of the Fifth Amendment, which is not directly relevant to the age discrimination claim.
   - **(C)** Is not within the scope of any of the enumerated powers of Congress in Article I, 8: This argument questions the legislative authority of Congress but does not address the discrimination claim.
   - **(D)** Invidiously discriminates against him on the basis of age in violation of the Fifth Amendment: Given that the law treats similarly situated individuals (civil service employees) differently based on the branch of service they belong to (armed forces vs. other), and considering that age is the basis for this differentiation, this option directly addresses the claim. The passages, especially Source 2, indicate that age-based classifications are subject to rational basis review under the Fifth Amendment (as applied to federal actions) and the Fourteenth Amendment (as applied to state actions).

5. **Conclusion**: The strongest argument Prentis can make is that the federal statute invidiously discriminates against him on the basis of age, which is a violation of the Fifth Amendment's Due Process Clause. This aligns with the principle that equal protection analysis under the Fifth Amendment (for federal actions) is the same as under the Fourteenth Amendment (for state actions), as noted in the passages.

Answer: (D)

To approach this question, let's analyze each option step by step:

(A) Denies him a privilege or immunity of national citizenship: The privileges or immunities clause of the Fourteenth Amendment is primarily concerned with protecting rights of national citizenship from state interference. However, since this is a federal statute, this argument might not be the strongest, as the clause is more directly applicable to actions by states rather than the federal government.

(B) Deprives him of a property right without just compensation: This argument refers to the Takings Clause of the Fifth Amendment, which requires just compensation when private property is taken for public use. While Prentis's job could be considered a property right, the issue here is not about the government taking his property but rather about the conditions under which he can continue to work. This argument might be relevant but does not directly address the age discrimination aspect of the statute.

(C) Is not within the scope of any of the enumerated powers of Congress in Article I, 8: This argument questions the constitutional authority of Congress to enact the statute. However, Congress has broad powers under Article I, Section 8, including the power to raise and support armies, which could encompass regulations regarding the employment of civil service personnel in the armed forces. Thus, this might not be the strongest argument, as Congress likely has the authority to regulate the terms of employment for its employees, including those in the armed forces.

(D) Invidiously discriminates against him on the basis of age in violation of the Fifth Amendment: The Fifth Amendment's Due Process Clause prohibits the federal government from depriving anyone of life, liberty, or property without due process of law. The concept of "invidious discrimination" refers to laws or government actions that unfairly single out a particular group for different treatment. Age can be a suspect classification, but it is not as strictly scrutinized as race or gender. However, the Supreme Court has recognized that age discrimination can be unconstitutional if it is arbitrary or capricious. Given that the statute creates two different retirement ages based solely on the type of civil service employment (armed forces vs. other), this could be seen as discriminatory, especially if the distinction does not serve a rational government interest.

Considering these options, the strongest argument Prentis can make is that the law invidiously discriminates against him based on age, as this directly addresses the differential treatment he receives compared to other civil service employees based solely on his age and the branch of service he is employed by.

Answer: (D)

question: Parr sued Davis for damages for physical injuries allegedly caused by Davis's violation of the federal civil rights law. The incident occurred wholly within the state of Chippewa but the case was tried in federal court. The Chippewa state code says, "The common-law privileges are preserved intact in this state." At trial, Davis called Dr. Webb, Parr's physician, to testify to confidential statements made to him by Parr in furtherance of medical treatment for the injuries allegedly caused by Davi
final_answer:
To determine which law applies in this scenario, we must consider the nature of the case and the court in which it is being tried. The case involves a federal civil rights law claim, which is being tried in federal court. However, the claim of privilege involves a physician-patient relationship, which is typically governed by state law, as it pertains to the regulation of medical practice and the protection of confidential communications within the state.

The key issue here is whether the federal court should apply federal law or state law regarding the physician-patient privilege. In federal cases, the applicability of state privileges is determined by federal common law, as established by the Supreme Court in cases such as Trammel v. United States. However, in civil actions and proceedings, with respect to an element of a claim or defense as to which state law supplies the rule of decision, whether a privilege applies shall be determined in accordance with state law. Since this case involves a claim under federal civil rights law, the general rule is that federal common law governs the application of privileges. 

However, the physician-patient privilege is not recognized as a privilege under federal common law in civil actions, as the Supreme Court has not recognized such a privilege in federal cases. 

Given these considerations:

- The case is being tried in federal court, which suggests that federal law should apply.
- The claim involves a federal civil rights law, further indicating the application of federal law.
- The specific issue of physician-patient privilege in federal civil cases is not recognized under federal common law.

Therefore, the court should apply federal law and reject the claim of privilege because there is no federally recognized physician-patient privilege in civil cases.

Answer: (D)

Armco Steel Corp. , 446 U.S. 740, 749-50, 100 S.Ct. 1978, 64 L.Ed.2d 659 (1980), "it governs ... unless it exceeds statutory authorization" under the Rules Enabling Act or "Congress's rulemaking power" under the Constitution. Shady Grove , 559 U.S. at 398, 130 S.Ct. 1431. "If no Federal Rule answers the question in dispute, we undertake an 'unguided Erie ' inquiry to decide whether to apply the state statute or federal common law." Carbone v. Cable News Network , 910 F.3d 1345, 1349 (11th Cir. 2018) (quoting Hanna v. Plumer , 380 U.S. 460, 471, 85 S.Ct. 1136, 14 L.Ed.2d 8 (1965) ). That choice-of-law inquiry requires us to "apply Erie and its progeny to determine 'whether failure to apply the state law would lead to different outcomes in state and federal court and result in inequitable administration of the laws or forum shopping.' - **(D)** This choice is correct because, based on the information provided, Jackson and Brannick did plan and conspire to steal the stamps, which meets the requirements for a conspiracy charge if an overt act was committed.

The prosecution did not cross-examine Kingsley. As rebuttal evidence, however, the prosecutor introduced records, over Kingsley's objection, showing that Kingsley had two prior convictions for narcotics
final_answer:
To address this question, let's analyze the two key issues presented:

1. **Admission of Prior Convictions**: The introduction of Kingsley's prior convictions for narcotics-related offenses as rebuttal evidence is the first issue. Generally, in the United States, evidence of prior convictions can be used to impeach a witness's credibility under certain conditions, as outlined in the Federal Rules of Evidence (Rule 609). However, using such evidence as substantive proof of guilt in the current case is typically not allowed, especially when the defendant is testifying. The concern here is whether the introduction of these records was appropriate, given that Kingsley was testifying in his own defense and the prosecution did not cross-examine him. If the records were used to impeach Kingsley's character for truthfulness, it might be permissible, but if used as substantive evidence of his guilt, it could be considered an error.

2. **Burden of Persuasion for Entrapment Defense**: The second issue revolves around the allocation of the burden of persuasion for the entrapment defense. In the United States, the Supreme Court has held that the defendant bears the burden of proving the defense of entrapment by a preponderance of the evidence (Matthews v. United States, 485 U.S. 58 (1988)). This means that Kingsley must show that he was induced by government agents to commit the crime and that he was not predisposed to commit it. The instruction given by the court, requiring Kingsley to establish the elements of the entrapment defense by a preponderance of the evidence, aligns with federal law and does not constitute a due process violation.

Given these considerations:

- The admission of prior convictions could potentially be an error if it was used improperly as substantive evidence of guilt rather than for impeachment purposes. However, without more specific details on how the evidence was used, it's difficult to definitively state this was an error.

To address this question, let's analyze the two key issues presented:

1. **Admission of Prior Convictions**: The introduction of Kingsley's prior convictions for narcotics-related offenses as rebuttal evidence is the first issue. Generally, in the United States, evidence of prior convictions can be used to impeach a witness's credibility under certain conditions, as outlined in the Federal Rules of Evidence (Rule 609). However, using such evidence as substantive proof of guilt in the current case is typically not allowed, especially when the defendant is testifying. The concern here is whether the introduction of these records was appropriate, given that Kingsley was testifying in his own defense and the prosecution did not cross-examine him. If the records were used to impeach Kingsley's character for truthfulness, it might be permissible, but if used as substantive evidence of his guilt, it could be considered an error.

2. **Burden of Persuasion for Entrapment Defense**: The second issue revolves around the allocation of the burden of persuasion for the entrapment defense. In the United States, the Supreme Court has held that the defendant bears the burden of proving the defense of entrapment by a preponderance of the evidence (Matthews v. United States, 485 U.S. 58 (1988)). This means that Kingsley must show that he was induced by government agents to commit the crime and that he was not predisposed to commit it. The instruction given by the court, requiring Kingsley to establish the elements of the entrapment defense by a preponderance of the evidence, aligns with federal law and does not constitute a due process violation.

evidence_store.text: Generally, evidence is admissible if it is relevant, i.e., if it has a tendency to prove any fact of consequence to the action. Evidence law generally prohibits admission of hearsay—out-of-court statements offered to prove the truth of the matter asserted—but the rule does not apply to admissions by party-opponents—they are treated as nonhearsay under the Federal Rules. Past instances of misconduct may be used to impeach a witness, at least if they are probative as to whether he is worthy of belief. The best evidence rule requires that when the contents of a writing are sought to be proved, the writing itself should be admitted if it is available. Under the Federal Rules convictions generally are not admissible for impeachment of a witness if more than 10 years old.

In Pacheco, the Hawai'i Supreme Court held that the trial court erred in allowing the prosecution to introduce the defendant's prior theft conviction for impeachment purposes under Hawaii Rules of Evidence ("HRE") Rule 609(a). 96 Hawai'i at 99, 26 P.3d at 588. The court noted that "[i]n every instance where a witness is sought to be impeached, the only issue that arises is whether the witness is telling the truth," and that as a preliminary matter, "if a party proffers evidence of a prior conviction to impeach the credibility of a witness, the trial court must first expressly determine whether the proffering party has 'shown that the conviction ... rationally carries probative value on the issue of the truth and veracity of the witness .' " Id. (emphasis added) (ellipses in original) (quoting Asato v. Furtado, 52 Haw. 284, 292, 293, 474 P.2d 288, 294, 295 (1970) ). The supreme court held that "a theft offense is not, per se, a 'crime of dishonesty' such that it is admissible to impeach a criminal defendant's credibility. Rather, to be admissible impeachment evidence pursuant to HRE Rule 609(a), the defendant must have committed the prior theft offense under circumstances that, by their very nature, render his or her prior conviction of the offense relevant to and probative of his or her veracity as a witness ." Id. at 100, 26 P.3d at 589 (emphasis added) (citations omitted).

Federal Rule of Evidence 609 supplies the controlling analytical framework. Rule 609(a)(2) allows into evidence-for purposes of impeachment-a witness's prior conviction for a crime involving dishonesty. But the Rule sets up different presumptions for convictions more than ten years old, like Narkiewicz-Laine's here. Evidence of such a conviction is admissible only if "its probative value, supported by specific facts and circumstances, substantially outweighs its prejudicial effect." Fed. R. Evid. 609(b)(1).

"Generally, the credibility of witnesses is always a relevant issue." State v. Austin , 411 S.W.3d 284, 289 (Mo. App. E.D. 2013). But this Court has noted that "[o]nly in very limited circumstances is evidence of the character of a crime victim relevant and admissible." State v. Clark , 747 S.W.2d 197, 200 (Mo. App. E.D. 1988). "The most common methods of impeaching a witness involve the admission of evidence regarding the following: the witness's incapacity or ability to perceive or remember; prior convictions; bias, interest, or prejudice; prior inconsistent statements of the witness; and the witness's reputation for truthfulness and veracity." State v. Eisele, 414 S.W.3d 507, 517 (Mo. App. E.D. 2013). "However, the trial court, in the exercise of its discretion, can limit the admission of evidence adduced through these methods if the prejudicial value of the evidence outweighs its probative value." Austin , 411 S.W.3d at 289.

When a defendant takes the stand in his own defense, the prosecutor can attack his credibility as a witness. Under Federal Rule 609, evidence of conviction of a crime requiring proof of an act of dishonesty or false statement can always be used to attack a witness's character for truthfulness. Even if fraud were probative of the tendency to commit violence, evidence of other crimes is not admissible to prove that a person has a propensity to commit criminal acts. No foundation is needed to show a prior conviction for impeachment purposes.

question: If Dunbar is prosecuted for murder on the basis of being an accessory to Balcom in committing a murder and the jury believes her claim, she should be found
final_answer:
To answer this question, let's break down the key elements involved in determining Dunbar's guilt or innocence as an accessory to murder.

1. **Understanding Accessory Liability**: Generally, to be found guilty as an accessory to a crime, it must be proven that the individual aided, abetted, or assisted in the commission of the crime, or that they counseled, commanded, induced, or procured the crime's commission.

2. **Dunbar's Claim**: If Dunbar claims she had no idea Balcom was armed and did not plan to use force, this suggests she did not intend for a violent act to occur. Her lack of knowledge about Balcom being armed and her lack of intent to use force are crucial.

3. **Analysis of Options**:
   - **(A)** This option suggests guilt because Balcom's actions were in aid of Dunbar. However, if Dunbar had no knowledge of the gun and no intent for violence, this option does not directly apply.
   - **(B)** Acting in concert in a dangerous undertaking could imply shared responsibility, but if Dunbar did not know about the gun and did not intend for force to be used, this shared responsibility might not apply fully.
   - **(C)** This option directly addresses Dunbar's claim of lack of knowledge about the gun and lack of intent for force. If believed, these factors could exonerate her from accessory liability because she did not aid, abet, or intend for the violent act to occur.
   - **(D)** While exercising self-help might be a factor, the key issue is whether Dunbar intended to steal or commit a crime that led to the murder. The question focuses on her role as an accessory to murder, not the initial intent behind her actions.

Given the information and focusing on the accessory liability aspect, the most relevant factor is Dunbar's claim of not knowing Balcom was armed and not planning to use force. This lack of knowledge and intent directly impacts her potential liability as an accessory to the crime.

Answer: (C)

choices.A: guilty, because in firing the shot Balcom was trying to help her.
choices.B: guilty, because she and Balcom were acting in concert in a dangerous under- taking.
choices.C: not guilty, because she had no idea that Balcom was armed and she did not plan to use force.
choices.D: not guilty, because she was exercising self- help and did not intend to steal.

evidence_store.text: ¶ 11 The trial court instructed the jury that Dreewes was guilty as an accomplice and legally accountable for the conduct of another person in the commission of the crime if, with knowledge, she solicited, promoted, or facilitated the commission of the crime. Clerk's Papers (CP) at 30. The court also instructed the jury that to convict the defendant of the crime of assault in the second degree as charged it must be proved beyond a reasonable doubt that on or about January 23, 2014, the defendant assaulted Marty with a deadly weapon, and that this act occurred in the state of Washington. Id. at 27.

The defense rightly does not ask this Court to hold that an accomplice must intend that a person will be killed. Such a rule would remove any distinction between felony murder and intentional murder in the aiding-and-abetting context, a result inconsistent with the statutory design. Rather, the defense argues that the prosecution must prove that Gyamfi subjectively knew, in advance of the attempted robbery, that there was a genuine risk someone would be killed during the robbery. The Court agrees. It is well established that a defendant may be held accountable for the foreseeable harms caused by his unlawful actions. See, e.g., United States v. Gonzalez, 399 Fed. App'x 641, 647 (2d Cir. 2010) (noting that "the requisite malice [for murder] can be found when the assailant acts with awareness of a serious risk of death or serious bodily harm"); United States v. Velasquez, 246 F.3d 204, 214 (2d Cir. 2001) (same); cf. Rosemond, 572 U.S. at 76 n.7, 134 S.Ct. 1240 (noting that "[s]ome authorities" support imposing aiding-and-abetting liability "when another crime is the 'natural and probable consequence' of the crime the defendant intended to abet," but expressing no view on the correctness of that rule). If Gyamfi recognized that he was participating in not just an armed robbery, but an armed robbery in which there was a real risk that someone might be killed, he may be held accountable for the fact that a death did, in fact, result, for he "knowingly took part in that more dangerous crime." Rosemond, 572 U.S. at 79, 134 S.Ct. 1240.

Because the state charged the defendant as a principal and not an accessory, however, proof that either the defendant or one of his codefendants intended to commit a felony against Clemente would be legally insufficient to support a judgment of conviction against the defendant, who was charged only as a principal. Similarly, any suggestion that the jury could infer that the defendant had the requisite intent because one of his codefendants was about to assault Clemente simply is incorrect. The state had not charged the defendant as an accessory, and the court had not been asked to instruct the jury on accessorial liability. See State v. Davis , 163 Conn. App. 458, 470, 136 A.3d 257 (2016) ("[A] reviewing court may not uphold a conviction premised on accessorial liability if the court foreclosed the jury from basing its guilty verdict on that theory. See State v. Faulkner , 48 Conn. App. 275, 277, 709 A.2d 36 [1998] [noting in review of sufficiency of evidence to support conviction as accessory that trial court instructed jury as to both principal and accessorial liability];

Defendants correctly observe that, contrary to a possible implication of former CALJIC No. 3.00, an actual killer and an aider/abettor are not always guilty of the same offense. Rather, in a homicide prosecution not involving felony murder or the natural and probable consequences doctrine, the aider/abettor's guilt is based on the combined acts of all the principals and on the aider/abettor's own knowledge and intent. Consequently, in some circumstances an aider/abettor may be culpable for a greater or lesser crime than the actual killer. ( People v. McCoy (2001) 25 Cal.4th 1111, 1120, 108 Cal.Rptr.2d 188, 24 P.3d 1210.) People v. Bryant, Smith and Wheeler , supra , 60 Cal.4th 335, 178 Cal.Rptr.3d 185, 334 P.3d 573 recognized that the standard instruction "generally stated a correct rule of law," in that "[a]ll principals, including aiders and abettors, are 'equally guilty' in the sense that they are all criminally liable." ( Id. at p. 433, 178 Cal.Rptr.3d 185, 334 P.3d 573.) However, former CALJIC No. 3.00"could be misleading if the principals in a particular case might be guilty of different crimes and the jury interprets the instruction to preclude such a finding." ( Ibid . )

Moreover, even if the Supreme Court did mean to hold definitively that an accomplice need not know that a firearm will be brandished or discharged in order to convict him of aiding and abetting violations of those subsections of 924(c), that would not resolve this case. At most, that would mean that an accomplice must know some fundamental things about the crime - that a gun will be used, for example - but not less important details, such as how the gun will be used. The question then becomes whether the risk of murder is a crucial kind of fact or some minor kind. Common sense alone suggests it is the former.

question: Rimm and Hill were fooling around with a pistol in Hill's den. Rimm aimed the pistol in Hill's direction and fired three shots slightly to Hill's right. One shot ricocheted off the wall and struck Hill in the back, killing him instantly. The most serious crime of which Rimm can be convicted is
final_answer:
To determine the most serious crime of which Rimm can be convicted, let's analyze the situation step by step:

1. **Intent**: The first factor to consider is Rimm's intent. Rimm aimed the pistol in Hill's direction but fired slightly to Hill's right, indicating that Rimm did not intend to shoot Hill directly. This lack of intent to kill or harm Hill directly rules out a premeditated or intentional murder charge.

2. **Action and Consequence**: Rimm's actions, although not directly intended to harm Hill, resulted in Hill's death due to the ricochet. This unintended consequence of a potentially dangerous action is crucial.

3. **Recklessness or Negligence**: By playing with a loaded pistol and firing it in a direction that could potentially harm someone, Rimm exhibited a level of recklessness or negligence. This behavior can lead to criminal liability, even if the harm caused was not intended.

4. **Voluntary vs. Involuntary Manslaughter**: Voluntary manslaughter typically involves an intentional killing that occurs under circumstances that would mitigate the offense from murder to manslaughter, such as in the heat of passion. Involuntary manslaughter, on the other hand, involves an unintentional killing that results from recklessness or criminal negligence.

5. **Analysis of Options**:
   - **(A) Murder**: This would require intent to kill or a more reckless state of mind than described.
   - **(B) Voluntary Manslaughter**: This requires an intent to kill or cause serious bodily harm, which is not present here.
   - **(C) Involuntary Manslaughter**: This is a possibility because Rimm's actions, although not intended to harm, were reckless and resulted in Hill's death.
   - **(D) Assault with a Dangerous Weapon**: While Rimm did use a dangerous weapon, the question asks for the "most serious crime" of which Rimm can be convicted, considering the outcome was Hill's death.

Given the analysis, the most serious crime that aligns with Rimm's actions and the unintended but fatal consequence is involuntary manslaughter, as it accounts for the reckless behavior leading to an unintended death without the intent required for murder or voluntary manslaughter.

Answer: (C)

choices.A: murder
choices.B: voluntary manslaughter
choices.C: involuntary manslaughter
choices.D: assault with a dangerous weapon

Involuntary manslaughter is defined as an unintentional killing that results from recklessness or criminal negligence, where the defendant's actions, although not intended to cause harm, create a high risk of death or serious injury. This doctrine applies when a person's conduct is so reckless or negligent that it demonstrates a disregard for human life, even if the killing was not intentional. Under this rule, a person can be held criminally liable for involuntary manslaughter if their reckless or negligent behavior results in another person's death, as seen in the scenario where Rimm's actions, though not directly intended to harm Hill, led to his death due to recklessness with a loaded pistol.

evidence_store.text: Involuntary manslaughter is an unintentional killing occurring while a defendant is engaged in wanton or reckless conduct that creates a high degree of likelihood that substantial harm will result to another. Commonwealth v. Power-Koch, 69 Mass. App. Ct. 735, 736-737, 871 N.E.2d 1085 (2007). "[W]here a defendant is charged with murder, an instruction on involuntary manslaughter is appropriate if any 'reasonable view of the evidence would [permit] the jury to find "wanton [or] reckless" conduct rather than actions from which a "plain and strong likelihood" of death would follow.' " Commonwealth v. Tavares, 471 Mass. 430, 438, 30 N.E.3d 91 (2015), quoting Commonwealth v. Braley, 449 Mass. 316, 331, 867 N.E.2d 743 (2007). Here, an involuntary manslaughter instruction was not warranted. The evidence showed that the defendant armed himself with a firearm and planned to rob the victim. A reasonable view of the evidence suggests that the defendant exhibited conduct from which a plain and strong likelihood of death would result. The defendant pulled out a revolver and pointed it at the victim before shooting him in the chest. See Commonwealth v. Alebord, 68 Mass. App. Ct. 1, 7, 859 N.E.2d 440 (2006) ("The likelihood of death ensuing when a loaded weapon is aimed at a person or group of people and then intentionally discharged is plain and strong indeed").

When a death is caused by criminal negligence, it is involuntary manslaughter. Criminal negligence requires a greater deviation from the reasonable person standard than is required for civil liability. The defendant must have taken a very unreasonable risk in light of the utility of his conduct, knowledge of the facts, and the nature and extent of the harm that may be caused. An excusable homicide is one for which there is a defense to criminal liability. An intentional killing is reduced from murder to voluntary manslaughter if the defendant committed the killing under a provocation that would arouse sudden and intense passion in an ordinary person such as to cause him to lose self-control, and there was insufficient time between the provocation and the killing for the passions of a reasonable person to cool. Murder is the unlawful killing of a human being with malice aforethought. Malice aforethought exists if the defendant: (i) intends to kill; (ii) intends to inflict great bodily injury; (iii) is aware of an unjustifiably high risk to human life; or (iv) intends to commit a felony.

"Involuntary manslaughter is the unlawful killing of a human being without malice, without premeditation and deliberation, and without intention to kill or inflict serious bodily injury." Wilkerson , 295 N.C. at 578, 247 S.E.2d at 915. "[W]hile involuntary manslaughter imports an unintentional killing, i.e., the absence of a specific intent to kill, it is ... accomplished by means of some intentional act." Id. at 582, 247 S.E.2d at 918.

Common law involuntary manslaughter is of two types: (i) a killing committed by the criminal negligence of another; or (ii) a killing occurring during the commission  of an unlawful act. Criminal negligence requires a greater deviation from the "reasonable person" standard for civil liability. Some states require that the defendant have had a subjective awareness of the risk. A jury can infer the required general intent from the performance of an act. However, it would not relieve the prosecution of the burden of proving that the defendant acted in a criminally negligent manner, an essential element of the crime of involuntary manslaughter. The state of mind for involuntary manslaughter can be established by criminal negligence.

Reckless involuntary manslaughter requires only the killing of a human being committed "[r]ecklessly." K.S.A. 2018 Supp. 21-5405(a)(1). A "person acts 'recklessly' or is 'reckless,' when such person consciously disregards a substantial and unjustifiable risk that circumstances exist or that a result will follow, and such disregard constitutes a gross deviation from the standard of care which a reasonable person would exercise in the situation." K.S.A. 2018 Supp. 21-5202(j) ; see also State v. Gonzalez , 307 Kan. 575, 581, 412 P.3d 968 (2018) (outlining requirements of unintentional second-degree murder, reckless involuntary manslaughter).

question: If Driver asserts a claim against City, the theory on which he has the best chance of prevailing is that City
final_answer:
To determine which theory gives Driver the best chance of prevailing, let's analyze each option step by step:

(A) Strict liability for harm caused by a defective traffic signal: Strict liability typically applies to situations where the activity or product is inherently dangerous, such as keeping wild animals or manufacturing defective products. While a defective traffic signal can cause harm, cities are not strictly liable for such defects unless they knowingly allowed a dangerous condition to exist. This theory might not be the strongest because it doesn't account for the city's potential lack of knowledge about the defect or its efforts to maintain the signals.

(B) Negligence in not replacing the broken pole within seventy-two hours: This theory is based on the concept of negligence, which requires proving that the city had a duty of care, breached that duty, and that the breach caused the harm. If the city has a standard practice or a reasonable timeframe for replacing broken poles, and it was working within that timeframe, this claim might be stronger. It directly addresses the city's actions (or lack thereof) in response to the broken pole.

(C) Absolute duty to maintain installed traffic signals in good operating order: An absolute duty implies that the city has no defense if it fails to maintain the traffic signals, regardless of the circumstances. While cities do have a duty to maintain public safety, including traffic infrastructure, this duty is not absolute. The city might have defenses, such as allocation of resources, prioritization of repairs, or unforeseen circumstances, that could mitigate its liability.

(D) Created a dangerous trap by not promptly replacing the broken pole: The concept of a "dangerous trap" usually implies a hidden danger that the victim could not have anticipated or avoided. If the broken pole was obvious and drivers were able to navigate around it or through the intersection with caution, this theory might not apply. Additionally, proving that the city intentionally or recklessly created a trap could be challenging.

Considering these analyses, the theory that Driver has the best chance of prevailing on is negligence. This is because it directly addresses the city's actions and omissions in response to the broken pole, allowing for an examination of whether the city acted reasonably under the circumstances.

Answer: (B)

The City next argues that it is entitled to summary judgment under the law governing claims against municipalities. (Doc. 34 at 19). A municipality may not be held liable under § 1983 solely because its employee inflicted injury; municipal liability cannot be found by application of the theory of respondeat superior. Monell v. Dept. of Soc. Servs. of City of New York , 436 U.S. 658, 694, 98 S.Ct. 2018, 56 L.Ed.2d 611 (1978). "[L]ocal governments are responsible only for 'their own illegal acts.' " Connick v. Thompson , 563 U.S. 51, 60, 131 S.Ct. 1350, 179 L.Ed.2d 417 (2011) (quoting Pembaur v. City of Cincinnati , 475 U.S. 469, 479, 106 S.Ct. 1292, 89 L.Ed.2d 452 (1986) ).

"[A] municipality cannot be held liable solely because it employs a tortfeasor-or, in other words, a municipality cannot be held liable under § 1983 on a respondeat superior theory." Monell v. Dep't of Soc. Servs. of City of N.Y. , 436 U.S. 658, 691, 98 S.Ct. 2018, 56 L.Ed.2d 611 (1978). "Instead, it is when execution of a government's policy or custom, whether made by its lawmakers or by those whose edicts or acts may fairly be said to represent official policy, inflicts the injury that the government as an entity is responsible under § 1983." Id. at 694, 98 S.Ct. 2018.

I would answer the certified questions as follows: the municipality is not entitled to good-faith immunity, punitive damages may be available against a municipality upon a proper showing, and attorney fees may be available under the bad faith or private attorney general theories.

"The tort liability of a municipality has been codified in [General Statutes] § 52-557n. Section 52-557n (a) (1) provides that '[e]xcept as otherwise provided by law, a political subdivision of the state shall be liable for damages to person or property caused by: (A) The negligent acts or omissions of such political subdivision or any employee, officer or agent thereof acting within the scope of his employment or official duties ....' Section 52-557n (a) (2) (B) extends, however, the same discretionary act immunity that applies to municipal officials to the municipalities themselves by providing that they will not be liable for damages caused by 'negligent acts or omissions which require the exercise of judgment or discretion as an official function of the authority expressly or impliedly granted by law.' " Id., at 320, 907 A.2d 1188.

question: In a negligence action by Price against Motorco, the proof needed to establish a prima facie case is
final_answer:
To establish a prima facie case for negligence, the plaintiff must show the existence of a legal duty owed by the defendant to the plaintiff, breach of that duty, actual and proximate cause, and damages. In the context of a products liability case, such as the one described involving Price against Motorco, the key elements to focus on are the breach of duty and the proximate cause, which often hinge on the defectiveness of the product and the defendant's knowledge or constructive knowledge of the defect.

Option (A) "only that the bolt was defective" is insufficient because merely showing the defect does not establish that the defendant was negligent or that the negligence was the proximate cause of the injury.

Option (B) "that the bolt was defective and had not been inspected by Motorco" introduces the idea of a potential breach of duty (failure to inspect) but does not fully address whether the defect would have been discoverable with reasonable care, which is crucial for establishing negligence.

Option (C) "that the bolt was defective, was inspected by Motorco, and the defect was not discovered" suggests a scenario where Motorco did attempt to fulfill its duty of care by inspecting the bolt but failed to discover the defect. However, this option does not directly address whether the defect would have been discoverable with the exercise of reasonable care, which is a critical element in determining negligence.

Option (D) "that the bolt was defective and the defect would have been discovered in Motorco had exercised reasonable care in the inspection of component parts" directly addresses the elements necessary to establish a prima facie case of negligence. It implies that Motorco had a duty to inspect the component parts (including the bolt) with reasonable care, that the defect was present and would have been discovered with such care, and thus, by not discovering it, Motorco breached its duty. This option aligns with the principles outlined in the provided sources, which emphasize the importance of the defendant's ability to discover the defect with reasonable care as a key factor in determining negligence.

The best answer is D.

choices.B: that the bolt was defective and had not been inspected by Motorco

2. **Breach**: The defendant breached that duty.
3. **Causation**: The breach caused the plaintiff's injury.
4. **Damages**: The plaintiff suffered damages as a result.

In the context of the question, focusing on the inspection of a defective bolt by Motorco, we can analyze the options as follows:

- **Option (A)**: "only that the bolt was defective" - This option does not address whether Motorco had a duty to inspect the bolt, whether they breached that duty, or whether the breach caused the injury. It only establishes the existence of a defect, which is not enough to prove negligence.

- **Option (B)**: "that the bolt was defective and had not been inspected by Motorco" - This option implies a breach of duty (failure to inspect) but does not necessarily establish that the defect would have been discovered if Motorco had exercised reasonable care. It also doesn't directly address causation or damages.

- **Option (C)**: "that the bolt was defective, was inspected by Motorco, and the defect was not discovered" - This option suggests that despite an inspection, the defect was not found. However, it does not directly address whether the inspection was conducted with reasonable care, which is crucial for determining negligence.

- **Option (D)**: "that the bolt was defective and the defect would have been discovered in Motorco had exercised reasonable care in the inspection of component parts" - This option directly addresses the breach of duty (failure to exercise reasonable care in inspection) and implies causation (the defect would have been discovered with reasonable care). It suggests that Motorco's negligence (breach of duty) led to the failure to discover the defect, which is a key element in establishing a prima facie case of negligence.

Given the analysis, the most comprehensive option that aligns with the elements needed to establish a prima facie case of negligence is **Option (D)**, as it addresses the duty, breach, and causation elements by implying that with reasonable care, the defect would have been discovered, thus potentially preventing the injury.

Answer: (D)

question: If Johnson's claim is based on false imprisonment, will Johnson prevail?
final_answer:
To determine if Johnson will prevail in a claim based on false imprisonment, we need to consider the elements required to establish a prima facie case for false imprisonment. These elements, as outlined in the provided sources, include:

1. An act or omission by the defendant that confines or restrains the plaintiff to a bounded area.
2. Intent on the part of the defendant to confine or restrain the plaintiff.
3. Causation, meaning the defendant's act caused the plaintiff's confinement.
4. Awareness of the confinement by the plaintiff (though some sources note exceptions, such as actual injury).

Given these requirements, let's analyze the options:

(A) Yes, because he was confined against his will. - This option addresses the first element (confinement) but does not consider intent or awareness.

(B) Yes, because he was harmed as a result of his confinement. - Harm is not explicitly listed as a required element in the sources provided, though it could relate to causation and potentially the awareness requirement if the harm made the plaintiff aware of the confinement.

(C) No, unless the security guard was negligent in locking the gate. - Negligence is not a required element for false imprisonment, which is an intentional tort. The focus is on intent to confine, not negligence.

(D) No, unless the security guard knew that someone was in the lot at the time the guard locked the gate. - This option directly addresses the intent requirement. For Johnson to prevail in a false imprisonment claim, it must be shown that the security guard intended to confine him, which would require knowledge that someone was in the lot when the gate was locked.

Based on the analysis, the most relevant factor for determining if Johnson will prevail in a false imprisonment claim is whether the security guard had the intent to confine Johnson, which would necessitate the guard knowing someone was in the lot when locking the gate.

Answer: (D)
